<!-- page 210 -->

5.3 Contextual functionality of converbal participles 
ever, there are some tendencies. All the contextual functions found with converbal participles are relatively less common with participles that precede their noun, and by implication relatively more common with those that follow their noun. Only when a purely temporal-aspectual relation exists between converbal participle and matrix verb is the participle more likely to precede its noun. As mentioned in §5.3.10, purely temporal-aspectual relations are most common with non-nominative participles, which lends some weight to the hypothesis that the relative infrequency of participles preceding their noun is due to the topicality of nominative nouns. It also suggests that the infrequency of converbal participles preceding their nouns is attributable to the usual early position of topical nominative nouns rather than to any positioning tendencies specific to converbal participles as such.
In contrast to converbal participles, adnominal participles are slightly more common preceding their noun. The difference between converbal and adnominal participles in this respect shows some correlation to the observation that, in the relatively fixed word order of Vedic prose, adnominal modifiers precede their noun, while converbal or appositive nouns, adjectives, and participles tend to follow their noun (Delbrück, 1888: 19–20).
Returning now to the position of participles relative to their matrix verb, with participles in certain converbal functions there are clear variations from the average tendency that participles precede their verb approximately half the time.
Table 5.4 shows only those converbal functions for which there is a distinct tendency towards preverbal or postverbal position. So, while 51.4 of all participles precede their verb, 60.3 of those categorized as expressing cause do so. Almost exactly the same proportion of participles expressing means precede their verb. All concessive participles precede their matrix verb; there are only fifteen participles so categorized in the corpus, so we cannot consider this an absolute restriction, but it is nevertheless a clear tendency. In contrast, 68 of participles expressing purpose follow their matrix verb, considerably higher than the average of 43.6. Most of these in fact occur in

Table . Position of participles relative to verb by semantic function

Function

Cause Means Concession Purpose Result
All participles

60.3 60.6 100
27 3
51.4

38 30.6
0 68 94

---

<!-- page 211 -->

 The semantics of participles
absolute final position in their clause, which is in line with Knobl’s (2005) observation on the position of purposive participles mentioned at the start of this section. Of participles expressing result, 94 follow their matrix verb, a much higher proportion than would be expected (even granted that this figure is relatively insecure, since there are only 36 such forms in the corpus).
These figures show a small but notable statistical tendency for participles in certain converbal functions to appear either before or after the verb more regularly than on average. When we consider the functions involved, it seems more likely that the reasons for this are based more in discourse pragmatics than that they are due to any strictly syntactic or semantic processes. The expression of cause or means by its very nature tends to involve reference to eventualities that temporally precede that of the matrix verb, while the expression of purpose or result, conversely, involve reference to eventualities that are temporally subsequent to that of the matrix verb. The tendencies in relative ordering therefore reflect the logical order of events. That these are only tendencies shows that there is no grammatical or even pragmatic requirement to observe this logical order; but the principle of communicative efficiency makes it more likely that such an order will be observed than not.
. Other syntactic uses
I have dwelt at some length on the semantics of adnominal and converbal participles. I noted that the semantic treatment of converbal participles essentially applies also to absolute participles and the left-dislocated ‘nominativi pendentes’ (e.g. (4.82) and (4.83) in §4.6). I have not provided any formal semantic analysis for the other minor syntactic contexts in which participles can be found, namely completive and periphrastic uses, and the arguable use of participles as primary predicates (such as (4.81)). There is nothing specific to the completive use of participles beyond the inherent semantics of the participles themselves and the inherent semantics of the verb that governs them: the meaning of the matrix verb takes the meaning of the participial clause as an argument and simultaneously fixes its reference time. The semantic combination of participial and matrix verb meanings is therefore not a feature of participial semantics, but of the lexical semantics of the verbs that take participial complements. Likewise, the meanings of periphrastic or proto-periphrastic constructions is entirely lexical, and not directly a matter of the constructional meaning of participles. As discussed in §4.6, the use of participles as primary clausal predicates is a poetic construction, and not part of the ordinary functional range of participles. It can be modelled in formal terms by inferring a meaning constructor almost identical to the finiteness meaning constructor ((3.38)), which satisfies the temporal dependency of the participial phrase and thereby produces a meaning of type t, i.e. a meaning equivalent to a finite clausal meaning.

---

<!-- page 212 -->

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 

⊕ P(t, e)] : ((↑σ rt)

↑σ )

↑σ

. Tense-aspect in the participial system
We now move on to the question of tense-aspect in the participial system. Many, perhaps most, works on the Rigvedic verbal system assume that there is a strong uniformity between the tense-aspect properties of finite and participial forms of any one tense-aspect category. That is, the common assumption, and perhaps indeed the null assumption, is that the tense-aspect properties of present participles are derivable by some regular principle from the tense-aspect properties of the present tense-aspect itself, and so the question of tense-aspect in the participial system is merely a subpart of the wider question of tense-aspect in the verbal system as a whole. We will see, however, that this assumption is not entirely valid: that the participial system diverges from the rest of the verbal system in some important respects.
A widespread view of participial tense-aspect in Sanskrit holds that participles have no inherent tense reference, but express only aspect. Indeed, in the foregoing semantic formalism I have followed this assumption, arguing that a participle’s reference time is identified with the event time of its matrix verb, and that participles define only the temporal relation between their own event time and reference time (i.e. aspect). To a large extent this analysis is supported by the data, but it must also be admitted that it originates, at least partly, in the transfer of analyses of Ancient Greek participial semantics to Sanskrit. There is no necessary reason why participles should express only aspect and not also, or alternatively, tense, and we will see in this section that there is evidence for an ongoing shift from aspect marking to tense marking in the Rigvedic participial system.
One argument in favour of the assumption that participles do not express tense is that their tense reference appears to be determined by their matrix verb. That is, a present participle in a clause with a past-tense matrix verb will be interpreted as having occurred in the past relative to the perspective time (or speech time) of the utterance, while a present participle in a clause with a future-tense matrix verb will be interpreted as occurring in the future relative to the perspective time (/speech time) of the utterance, and so on.35 In other words, the absolute tense reference of a participle is determined by that of the matrix verb, so there is no reason to assume tense reference on the part of the participle. However, finite verbs in subordinate clauses
35 This almost goes without saying, but Dahl (: e.g. –) explicitly demonstrates the point with examples for all tense-aspect stems.

---

<!-- page 213 -->

 The semantics of participles
can be similarly constrained in their external temporal reference by the verb of the matrix clause, even though they do (or at least can) have independent tense properties. Moreover, we have seen (§5.1) that some adnominal participles can have independent temporal reference relative to the perspective/speech time of an utterance.
A related argument is based on the morphological properties of participles and finite verb forms. On the assumption that the different tense-aspect stem formants make a consistent semantic contribution across the forms in which they appear, this contribution must be aspectual. This is because finite indicative present and imperfect tense verb forms share the same tense-aspect stem (the present) but differ in tense: the former has present-tense reference, but the latter has past-tense reference. The tense distinction between these present and imperfect forms is marked partly by prefixation of a- (the augment) in the imperfect, and partly also by the use of distinct person/number suffixes (‘primary’ suffixes in the present, ‘secondary’ in the imperfect), leaving only aspect to be expressed by the tense-aspect stem itself. Since participles do not have augments or person/number suffixes that might distinguish tense, but do have a tense-aspect stem, they can be treated as expressing purely aspect. This is certainly the approach of Dahl (2010), and the same principle underlies Kiparsky’s (1998) approach to Vedic tense and aspect.
However, it is not in reality possible to distinguish the exponence of tense and aspect so clearly. If indicative aorist forms have present (or immediative) tense reference (cf. §2.3.5), this must be marked by the same augment and secondary endings that mark past tense in the imperfective. The future, if it is analysed as having future tense reference, is distinguished from the present only by its distinct tense-aspect stem. More widely, as discussed in §2.3.1, the tense-aspect, diathetic, and modal categorization of a form can often only be established on the basis of the form as a whole, and does not derive piecemeal from distinct morphological components. So it does not necessarily follow that the tense-aspect stem formant correlates to one particular semantic feature such as aspect, since such features are not necessarily associated with particular morphemes, but with words as unitary wholes.
Furthermore, there is no reason to expect a morphological marker to necessarily be associated with a consistent semantic contribution in all forms in which it occurs. Diachronic processes such as grammaticalization and analogy can affect individual words and subsets of larger categories, leaving shared morphology no more than a synchronically meaningless relic. This is the case with the present tense-aspect stem in Sanskrit, found in both finite present and finite (indicative) imperfect verb forms, which under the analysis of Rigvedic tense-aspect advanced in Chapter 2 (see (2.5)) share neither the same tense value nor the same aspect value.36
36 Parallel examples are not hard to find in other languages. In Agul, a Lezgic language spoken in Southern Dagestan, the ergative morpheme appears as an empty morph in other cases such as the genitive and the dative; e.g. gada-ji ‘boy-erg’, gada-ji-n ‘boy-∅-gen’. In English, some uses of the modal verbs would, should, and could reflect their inherited past-tense morphology, while other uses have clear present-tense

---

<!-- page 214 -->

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 

It is not impossible, therefore, that there might be no tense-aspect properties that are shared between participles and finite verbs formed to the same stems. Such an extreme divergence is clearly not the case, as we will see; but there is evidence for some divergence in the tense-aspect reference of participles and finite verbs that belong to the same morphological tense-aspect categories. This possibility does not seem to have been sufficiently taken into account by recent semantic analyses of tense and aspect in the Rigveda such as that of Dahl (2010), who assumes rather too easily that a given tense-aspect stem will have a unified range of meaning across all the categories in which it appears.
I have argued in previous chapters that participles are inflectional forms of verbs and should therefore correspond semantically with finite verbal forms insofar as inflectional paradigmatic uniformity requires; but we have also seen that to a certain extent participles are less prototypically ‘verbal’ than finite verbs in syntactic terms, so we should not be surprised if they also display corresponding differences in semantics. Rather than make assumptions about the tense-aspect properties of participles on the basis of their morphology, it is preferable to begin by analysing actual forms in their context, and to work back from this to determine the semantic properties of the different categories of participles.

.. Present participles
The majority of present participles unambiguously predicate an eventuality that is, in basic terms, ongoing at either the event time of the main verb or the perspective/speech time of the clause. This has already been demonstrated in the discussion of adnominal participles above (§5.1); it is particularly clear with adnominal participles and with converbal participles in cases where no contextual relation can be inferred between participial and matrix eventualities, that is, where we are dealing with a purely temporal-aspectual relation between the two. In (5.95), the ‘dwelling’ expressed by the present participle ks.iyánt- clearly began before the ‘finding’ expressed by the finite verb, and extends up to and perhaps beyond the accomplishment of the ‘finding’. The participle tápant- ‘burning’ in (5.96) can be interpreted as an epithet; it expresses a permanent quality of its subject, the sun, and accordingly this property is understood to extend temporally both before and after the ‘flying’ of the matrix verb, and also (or alternatively) before and after the speech time.

(5.95) yáh. śámbaram párvates.u ks.iyántam. /

catva¯rim. śya¯´m.

śarády anvávindat

autumn.l found

‘He who found Śambara in the fortieth autumn as he was dwelling in the

mountains.’ (RV 2.12.11ab)

reference; the inherited past-tense morphology on must no longer has any association with past-tense semantics.

<!-- page 215 -->

 The semantics of participles

(5.96) ghr. n. a¯´ tápantam

su¯´ryam paráh. / śakuna¯´ iva paptima

heat.i burn.prs.ptc.a.sg.m beyond sun.a past birds like flew.1pl

‘Past and beyond the sun, burning with heat/ we have flown like birds.’

Many of the passages provided in the earlier part of this chapter exemplify the same temporal relationship between a present participle and its matrix verb (or, less commonly, the perspective/speech time of the utterance—cf. the discussion in §5.1). As mentioned in §5.1, this relation can be interpreted as the aspectual relation of imperfectivity. This matches with the aspectual reference of the present tense-aspect as defined in §2.3.5.
Imperfective aspect applied to a telic verb can express iteration over a period of time.37 This is commonly found with epithetic participles and some restrictive participles, where the present participle expresses a characteristic or habitual action or activity of the subject, whereas with atelic verbs such epithetic and restrictive participles expre√ss a permanent characteristic.38 In (5.97) stanáyant-, present participle to the telic root stan ‘thunder’, may refer either to a characteristic, iterated, activity of thundering and/or to a present ongoing thundering (necessarily consisting of a repeated series of individual thunderings√).39 This contrasts with (5.96), where tápant-, present participle to the durative root tap ‘be hot, burn’, indicates a permanent characteristic of its subject.

(5.97)

prá sus..tutí

stanáyantam.

ruvántam/

forth good_praise thunder.prs.ptc.a.sg.m bellow.prs.ptc.a.sg.m

i.lás

pátim. jaritar nu¯nám aśya¯h.

refreshment.g lord.a singer.v now reach.opt

‘May our good praise now reach, O singer, the thundering, bellowing lord of

refreshment.’ (RV 5.42.14ab)

The inference of the contextual relations manner, attendant circumstance, equivalence, cause, and concession with converbally employed present participles all depend on this same temporal-aspectual relation: the temporal extent of the eventuality expressed by the matrix verb is conceived of as contained within or equal to the temporal extent of the eventuality expressed by the present participle. This can be seen in the examples provided above for each of these functions. In contrast, the expression of means usually implies that the eventuality expressed by the participle is completed before, or at least bounded by, that of the matrix verb. In (5.98) the ‘sending’ referred to

‘(By) going along the true (path), Sarama¯ found the cows.’ (RV 5.45.7c)

It might be possible to see in such examples evidence in favour of Dahl’s (2010) claim that the present tense-aspect expresses the neutral aspect, since this includes both imperfective and perfective readings. However, it is still possible to interpret such examples as displaying imperfectivity, albeit one ‘edge’ of the imperfective relation. If we assume, in passages like these, that the temporal relation between the two eventualities is such that the temporal extent of the participle is bounded at one side by the accomplishment of the main verb, then the imperfective relation R⊆E still holds. Given the relative rarity of examples such as these beside the unambiguous imperfectivity of the vast majority of present participles, it is clearly preferable to assume the more restricted analysis and avoid assuming ‘neutral’ aspect.
The expression of purpose and result appear to present a greater obstacle to the argument that all uses of the present participle can be understood in terms of imperfectivity, since the eventuality expressed by the participle should in principle only begin with the accomplishment or termination of the eventuality expressed by the main verb. However, it is notable that present participles expressing purpose are usually formed to telic verbs. As noted above, the combination of imperfective aspect and telicity can result in an iterative interpretation; another possible outcome of this combination is a conative reading. Under this reading, the imperfective nature of participles such as daśasyánt- in (5.100) becomes clear.40

(5.100)

a¯´ yád rayím. guhádavadyam

asmai/ bhárad ám. śam.

to when wealth.a hiding_deficiencies.a him.d brought portion.a

naítaśo daśasyán

like=Etaśa do_worship.prs.ptc.n.sg.m

‘When Etaśa brought wealth which hides deficiencies to him, his portion as

it were, (attempting/in order) to do worship.’ (RV 2.19.5)

40 Similarly Tikkanen (: ).

---

<!-- page 217 -->

 The semantics of participles
There is therefore no need to assume that the present participle ever expresses the neutral aspect, as argued by Dahl (2010: 258–60).41 Rather, all uses of the present participle can be understood as variant realizations of imperfective aspect. Although this does not necessarily imply that the same property need be attributed to all present tense-aspect forms, including finite verb forms, it does correspond to the traditional interpretation of the present tense-aspect as a primarily imperfective category, and in this case we are justified in assuming a close semantic correlation between the present participle system and the finite present system.
There is, however, another way to understand this shared property of present participial and finite verb forms. To this point I have followed the assumption that participles express aspect and not tense, but in the case of present participles there is no practical difference between the two. If participles were specified to identify the event time of the matrix verb as their perspective time, rather than their reference time, then the expression of present tense, P⊆R, is equivalent to, and not clearly distinguishable from, the expression of imperfective aspect, R⊆E. Moreover, this is exactly how subordinate clauses containing finite verbs must acquire their temporal reference, i.e. by defining their perspective time, not their reference time, on the basis of the event time of the verb in the superordinate clause. To the extent that participial clauses are similar, at least, to embedded subordinate clauses, there is little reason to assume that participles express purely aspect, when the expression of tense (in combination with imperfective aspect) is indistinguishable.
From a formal point of view this is, perhaps, a moot point in relation to the present participle, since P⊆R⊆E where P=Emain, i.e. present tense and imperfective aspect, is equivalent to R⊆E where R=Emain, i.e. purely imperfective aspect. It is not so trivial in relation to other tense-aspect stems, and I will return to this question in §5.5.6.
.. Stative participles
The unclear status of the stative as a distinct tense-aspect was discussed in Chapter 2 (§2.3.4). Although I treat it as a separate category (distinct from the present in terms of Aktionsart), its tense and aspect features are identical to those of the present tenseaspect, into which it was ultimately assimilated. This identity is not only found in finite stative forms, but extends also to stative participles. That is, the temporal-aspectual relation between a stative participle and its matrix verb can be treated as a relation of imperfectivity (or, given the discussion at the end of §5.5.1, present time).
The difference between stative and present participles lies in the types of eventuality that the two categories can express. While present stems are in principle unrestricted in the types of verb they can appear with, the stative forms stems with stative Aktionsart.
41 Of the passages cited by Dahl (: –), some can be explained in the terms described here; a couple should be interpreted as iterative uses of telic verbs or as atelic verbs; one verse contains no participle (..); and one does not exist (‘..’).

---

<!-- page 218 -->

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 

This means that stative stems are necessarily atelic and non-dynamic. Since no stative stem is telic, no iterative or conative readings, exemplified with present participles in (5.97) and (5.100), are possible with stative participles. Because stative stems are inherently non-dynamic, the expression of semantic relations that prototypically involve agentivity, such as manner, purpose, cause, and means, are considerably rarer with stative than with present participles.
The semantic range of stative participles is therefore a subset of the range of present participles. Example (5.101) shows the participle dúha¯na-, which corresp√onds to the finite stative duhé ‘gives milk, is productive (as regards milk)’ to the root duh ‘milk’, used adnominally to express a property that extends beyond the temporal extent of the matrix verb; this parallels the present participle tápant- in (5.96). The stative participle śáya¯na- in (5.102) is used converbally with purely temporal-aspectual reference (i.e. with no contextual relation inferrable), parallel to the p√resent participle in (5.95). The converbally used stative participle in (5.103), huva¯ná- ( hu¯ ‘call’), can be interpreted as expressing a relation of contingency with the eventuality of the matrix verb, just like the present participle varivasyánt- in (5.87). The stative participle gr. n. a¯ná- in (5.104) is a rare example of converbal use in which it is possible (though not necessary) to infer a causal relation with the matrix verb.

---

<!-- page 219 -->

 The semantics of participles
Stative participles, then, display a subset of the functionalities found with present participles, but in purely temporal-aspectual terms show exactly the same properties. Their similarity to present participles parallels the similarity between stative and present finite forms, such that we can treat the stative participle system as closely aligned with the finite stative system, just like the present participle with the finite present system.
.. Aorist participles
In contrast to the relatively regular temporal-aspectual properties of present and stative participles, those of the aorist participle category are distinctly problematic. Previous literature on the subject attests the existence of quite strongly divergent views. Dahl (2010: 336–7) argues that the aorist participle corresponds closely with the finite aorist in being essentially perfective: ‘Aorist Participle forms of telic predicates either unambiguously express that a situation has been terminated prior to the reference time of the sentence or are perfectly compatible with this interpretation.’42 This view is strikingly different from that of Tikkanen (1987: 106, 113), who argues that the aorist participle has primarily a present time reference (≈imperfective), but may have relative past-time reference in combination with ingressive aspect, being therefore ‘temporally less positively marked for relative present tense than the present participle or the non-past gerund in am . . . It is rather weakly marked temporally, showing, however, mostly past relative time reference in combination with ingressive aspect and middle voice.’ Tikkanen’s view echoes that of Delbrück (1888: 381), who concluded: ‘zusammenfassend kann man sagen, dass nur in seltenen Fällen in dem aoristischen Part. auch der aoristiche Sinn sichtbar wird’ (‘in conclusion one can say that only in rare instances does the aorist participle display aorist sense’). In a more recent work Dahl (2011b: 290–91), while maintaining that the aorist participle essentially reflects the perfective aspect, does admit as an alternative possibility that present and aorist participles were not clearly distinguished in semantic terms, even in Indo-Iranian.43
These contrasting views have arisen partly, at least, because there is no one consistent temporal property that can be attributed to all instances of aorist participles. We will see in Chapter 6 (§6.2.2) that the category of aorist participles (in the restricted sense defined there) includes a number of forms with analogical origins alongside the more regular members of the category, such that a corresponding lack of uniformity in tense-aspect reference is perhaps to be expected.
In some instances aorist participles unambiguously refer to an eventuality that is completed prior to the event time of the matrix verb. In (5.105) the aorist√participle gmánt- and the finite perfect form jagmathuh. , both part of the same root gam ‘go, come’, refer to the same event.
42 Dahl says that aorist participles to atelic predicates are extremely rare in the Rigveda; when this does happen, he admits there can be an indication of temporal overlap, though he does not indicate how this fits with the supposedly perfective nature of the aorist participle.
43 A similar analysis is provided by Do¯yama ().

---

<!-- page 220 -->

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 

(5.105)

‘Uśanas asks you two, having come:/ for what purpose to our house/ have you

come from afar,/ from heaven and earth to the mortal?’ (RV 10.22.6)

The question asked of the addressees (Indra and Kutsa), ‘for what reason have you come . . .’, is in the perfect tense, necessarily therefore referring to a ‘coming’ that wholly precedes the ‘asking’ of the matrix verb; the ‘coming’ referred to by the participle gmánt- is the same ‘coming’ referred to by the finite perfect form, and so must, like that form, wholly precede the ‘asking’ of the matrix verb. The same participle occurs also at 1.122.11a ((5.84)), which likewise unambiguously refers to a temporally prior event, and at 10.32.1a ((5.106)), which is more ambiguous but can very easily be interpreted in parallel manner.44

The act of worship performed by the priest (possibly the r. tá- ‘truth, true/right act’ in the last pa¯da) corresponds to the chariot in the simile, metaphorically conceived as the means by which Agni will bring

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 

‘The (An˙girases) of good works, well-shining, who serve the gods,/ like gods smelting like iron the races,/ bright, who increase Agni (and) Indra,/ who besieged the cow enclosure, have come.’ (RV 4.2.17)

In some passages, aorist participles appear in contexts very similar to contexts in which perfect participles to the same roots occur. It is therefore natural to interpret them in a parallel manner, i.e. with reference to an eventuality completed prior to the matrix event. For example, the aorist participle budha¯ná- in (5.112) appears in√an almost identical context to bubudha¯ná-, perfect participle to the same root ( budh ‘awake’), in (5.113). It is difficult to be certain, however, that one context is sufficiently similar to another for precisely the same sequence of events to be conceived; so, in this case, it is also possible to interpret the aorist participle as if it were a present participle, for example with iterative reference (both possibilities are given in the gloss).

(5.112)

es.á syá ka¯rúr jarate su¯ktaír/

ágre budha¯ná

this dct poet sings well-spoken.i front.l awake.aor.ptc.n.sg.m

us.ása¯m. sumánma¯ dawns.g of_good_intent

‘This poet sings with well-spoken (words),/ having awoken/when he awakes

at the start of the dawns with good intent.’ (RV 7.68.9ab)

(5.113)

dadhikra¯´va¯n. am bubudha¯nó

agním/ úpa bruva us.ásam.

Dadhikra¯van.a awake.pf.ptc.n.sg.m Agni.a on call.1sg dawn.a

su¯´ryam. ga¯´m sun.a cow.a

‘Having awoken (early) I call on Dadhikra¯van, Agni, the dawn, the sun, the

cow.’ (RV 7.44.3ab)

Reference to an eventuality that is completed prior to the matrix event time can be interpreted in a number of ways. Perfective aspect (E⊆R) can have this reading, as with the aorist participles in Ancient Greek, but so can anterior aspect (E≺R). If we admit the possibility of participles expressing not aspect but tense, or a combination of both, then past tense (R≺P) would give the same reading, or past tense with perfective aspect, or indeed present (R⊆P) or immediative (P⊆R) tense with anterior aspect. Past tense and/or perfective aspect could represent the inherited value of the aorist tense-aspect, while anterior aspect could represent the synchronic value of the same.
Such reference is not, however, the most common that is found with aorist participles. As suggested by the analyses of Tikkanen (1987) and Delbrück (1888) quoted above, the temporal reference of the majority of aorist participles is not discernibly anything that could be analysed as distinctively ‘aorist’: rather, there is very often no clear difference between aorist and present participles to the same roots.

 The semantics of participles

√ The two uses of yuj in this verse appear to be referring to the same event, the accusative object ellipsed in the participial clause but the same instrumental adjunct explicit in both. This makes an anterior or past perfective reading of the participle less likely; the most natural way to interpret the participle is as imperfective (expressing equivalence): ‘in our present yoking of the horses . . . we now yoke the chariots with them’. Even the two s-aorist participles, the only aorist participles in the Rigveda (and all Sanskrit) built to this clearly marked aorist stem, show no obvious perfectivity or anteriority, as shown in (5.123) and (5.124):

(5.123)

‘As though burning everything he (Indra) scorches the thirsty one,/ he

scorches up Arśasa¯na.’ (RV 1.130.8fg)

(5.124) yás tva¯´m agna inádhate yatásruk/

‘He who will kindle you, O Agni, with ladle raised,/ (who) thrice will make

you food on this day;/ may he excel with splendour, as (being) a conqueror,/

perceptive through your mental power, Ja¯tavedas.’ (RV 4.12.1)

√ One particularly interesting form is jus.a¯n. á-, aorist participle to jus. ‘enjoy’. Its context of use is at least superficially similar to that of the perfect participle to the same

root, jujus.a¯n. á-: both most commonly appear with imperatival matrix verbs, usually ‘come’ or ‘drink’; when the object of the participle is expressed, it is either the pressing,

offering, or sacrifice being made, or the invocation or praise of the deity. That is, both

aorist and perfect participles are used in the context of a sequence of events such as

the following: the divinity hears the invocation, prayer, or praise, and enjoys it; on

this basis he/she decides to come to the sacrifice or offering, comes, partakes, tastes,

enjoys, and behaves favourably towards the sacrificers; different parts of this sequence

can be seen in (5.125) and (5.126):

(5.125)

hávam.

dev¯´ı jujus.a¯n. a¯´

ghr. ta¯´c¯ı/ śagma¯´m. no

invocation.a goddess enjoy.pf.ptc.n.sg.f facing_ghee powerful.a our

va¯´cam uśat¯´ı śr. n. otu

speech.a willing hear.imp.3sg

---

<!-- page 226 -->

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 

‘Let the goddess, having enjoyed the invocation, turned to the ghee,/ willingly hear our powerful speech.’ (RV 5.43.11cd)

(5.126)

a¯´ śubhra¯ ya¯tam

aśvina¯ sváśva¯/

gíro

here bright.v come.imp.2du Aśvins.v with_good_horses songs.a

dasra¯

jujus.a¯n. a¯´

yuva¯´koh. /

havya¯´ni ca

marvellous.v enjoy.pf.ptc.n.du.m devoted_to_you.g oblations.a and

prátibhr. ta¯ v¯ıtám.

nah.

offered.a pursue.imp.2du our

‘Come here, O bright Aśvins, with good horses,/ O marvellous ones, having

enjoyed the songs of the one devoted to you,/ and pursue the oblations offered

by us.’ (RV 7.68.1)

According to Migron (1990: 128), the aorist jus.a¯n. á- and the perfect jujus.a¯n. á- are used in such similar contexts because they are semantically eq√uivalent; Migron uses this assumption to argue that the lexical meaning of the root jus. in the Rigveda is not the traditionally assumed ‘taste, enjoy’, but rather ‘choose’. He argues that if the participles are based on a root meaning ‘taste, enjoy’, then the ‘enjoying’ they refer to could only precede the ‘coming’ or ‘drinking’ of the matrix verb when the object of the enjoyment is an invocation or praise; when the object of the enjoyment is the sacrifice or pressing, then this enjoyment must necessarily occur after the commanded action of ‘coming’ or ‘drinking’. If, however, the root means ‘choose (to favour)’, then the participles can always refer to a ‘choosing’ that precedes the commanded action, since choosing to favour an offering or act of praise can precede coming to or consuming it. Since the perfect participle unambiguously refers to an eventuality com√pleted prior to that of the matrix verb, it appears that ‘choose’ is the true meaning of jus..
However, as with budha¯ná- and bubudha¯ná- discussed above, the similarity between aorist jus.a¯n. á- and perfect jujus.a¯n. á- is only a broad similarity of context, not of tense-aspect reference. The aorist participles are found with both kinds of object, but the perfect middle participles are found only with objects of invocation or praise. That is, the use of perfect p√articiples in this context is consistent with the traditional meaning ‘taste, enjoy’ for jus.; the aorist participles are used differently, but their use too is consistent with the meaning ‘taste, enjoy’, as long as their temporalaspectual reference is not to a completed or prior eventuality. In one passage, a perfect participle does occur with the genitive object ándhasas ‘(Soma) juice’ (2.36.3cd, (5.128)), but significantly the imperative in that passage is different: mandasva ‘rejoice’, which logically can follow the tasting/enjoyment. There is theref√ore a clear functional distinction between the perfect and aorist middle participles of jus., which not only supports the traditional meaning assigned to the root but also shows that superficially similar aorist and perfect participles can in fact have distinctly different temporalaspectual properties.

---

<!-- page 227 -->

 The semantics of participles

The aorist participle jus.a¯n. á-, then, has non-past reference. In fact, it usually expresses a purposive relation with the matrix verb: ‘come/drink in order to taste/enjoy
the offering/praise’; this is seen in (5.127) below, and also in (6.21).

(5.127)

a¯´ no devébhir úpa deváhu¯tim/

ágne ya¯hí

here our gods.i to divine_oblation.a Agni.v come.imp.2sg

vás.a.tkr. tim. jus.a¯n. áh. vas.at.-cry.a enjoy.aor.ptc.n.sg.m ‘Come here, O Agni, with the gods to our divine oblation/ to enjoy the cry of

‘vas.at.’.’ (RV 7.14.3ab)

The expression of purpose is found with present participles to telic roots, as

discussed above (§5.3.1), which could be taken to suggest that jus.a¯n. á- displays a temporal-aspectual reference equivalent to the present. However, the sense ‘enjoy’ is

not particularly telic, and it might also reflect a perfective reference: an eventuality

viewed perfectively from the matrix event time may (as regularly in Ancient Greek)

be interpreted as preceding that of the matrix, but can also be interpreted as following

it (as with the Russian perfective future), the point being that it does not overlap with

it. The clearly intended difference between the aorist participle jus.a¯n. á- and the present participle jus.áma¯n. a- in (6.21) may be taken to support the purposive use of jus.a¯n. á- as a specifically non-present, perfective use.

Finally, some aorist participles that unambiguously refer to an ongoing eventuality,

usually a state, may still diachronically reflect a perfective or anterior reference if

they are formed to the aorist participle

ttoelic√rpo¯ıo‘stws.eFllourpe’,xaanmdp√sloe,repfílyea¯cntsat-h‘eswmeellainngin, gsw‘hoalvleinn’g

at 1.79.3a is swollen up’

vel sim.; guháma¯na- ‘hiding’ at 4.1.11c to guh ‘conceal’ re√flects an original ‘having

concealed’; jásama¯na- ‘exhausted’ at 1.112.6a and 7.68.8a to jas ‘become exhausted’

reflects ‘having become exhausted’. This parallels the semantic development of stative

perfect participles, discussed in the following section, and in some cases (including

píya¯na-) is most likely based on the perfect (cf. the discussion in §6.2.2).

It is easy to see why the tense-aspect properties of aorist participles are controversial.

The evidence, as shown above, is not consistent, some supporting a perfective or

anterior reading, some an imperfective or at least ‘present-like’ reading. It is not, in

fact, possible to account for the varied readings of aorist participles by reference to one

unified temporal-aspectual property. There are two main reasons for this. As I discuss

in §6.2.2, the mediopassive aorist participles show a degree of analogical productivity

in the Rigveda, based partly on perfect participles and partly on stative participles

and other aorist participles.48 The influence of the perfect and stative was not just

formal, but also affected the semantics of analogically created aorist participles. At

the same time, the category of aorist participles as a whole was obsolescent: they are

48 Cf. also Lowe (forthcoming b).

---

<!-- page 228 -->

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 
unexpectedly infrequent in the Rigveda when compared with the relative frequency of present and perfect participles and finite aorist forms, and they are all but absent from later Vedic and Classical Sanskrit. It is not improbable that aorist participles survived longer in the archaizing language of the Vedic poets than in the contemporary vernacular, and it is likely that some poets used aorist participles without fully understanding their original paradigmatic status and function.
Not only the aorist participles but the aorist category as a whole was undergoing semantic changes during the Vedic period. Though originally a perfective category, it must have developed into a resultative category at an early stage, whence it underwent the cross-linguistically attested process of ‘aoristic drift’ (Bybee et al. 1994; Squartini and Bertinetto 2000) through to a recent past category, before being caught up in the development of an evidentiality-based system in late Vedic. The different tenseaspect references of aorist participles may reflect different diachronic stages in the development of the category. Forms that can be interpreted perfectively (such as jus.a¯n. á- used purposively) may attest the inherited perfective aspect of the aorist; those that unambiguously refer to a completed or prior action may do also, but might equally well reflect the contemporary anterior aspect of the aorist. Forms that must be interpreted imperfectively represent, at least in part, the misuse of this obsolescent category, perhaps due to secondary morphological association with the present participle (with which it shared both active and mediopassive suffixes, in contrast to the perfect).49
Despite our inability to define the aorist participle in terms of a single tense or aspect property, or even a single combination of tense and aspect, what we have seen of the aorist participle does contribute to our overall understanding of the participial system as a whole. In contrast to the present participles discussed above, the evidence cannot support a one-to-one correspondence between the aorist tense-aspect stem as used in finite verbal forms and the same aorist tense-aspect stem as used in the participles. The varied temporal-aspectual reference of the aorist participles does not align with the synchronically consistent features of the finite aorist. Although an inflectional verbal category, the aorist participle here displays a distinct development that sets it somewhat apart from the rest of the inflectional aorist system. This will be considered further, in the wider context of the participial system as a whole, in §5.5.6.
.. Perfect participles
In comparison with the aorist participle category just discussed, the category of perfect participles is relatively consistent in its temporal-aspectual reference. The only real variation found is that between the two major subcategories of perfect stem, the past
49 Similar developments explain the development of the stative perfects from an originally resultative category. On the partially similar diachronic developments affecting the originally resultative perfect in Ancient Greek, see Haspelmath () and Haug (b).

---

<!-- page 229 -->

 The semantics of participles

Stative Stative Stative Past Stative Stative Stative Stative Stative Past

perfect and the stative perfect (cf. §2.3.4). These two subcategories are not equally represented in terms of participles in the corpus. The stative perfect stems, despite being considerably fewer than the past perfect stems, have a significant majority in terms of number of participle tokens in the text. There are 158 different perfect participle stems in the Rigveda, with a total of 900 individual tokens. But 56 of these occurrences are found with only the twenty (12.7) most frequent stems. The eight most frequent stems (5 of the 158) provide 36 of the total occurrences of perfect participles in the Rigveda; of these the top three (<2) provide 24.6, the most frequent, vidvám. s-, providing over 13 on its own. Eight of the ten most frequent stems are among those listed by Kümmel (2000a: 69–71) as stative perfects, including the top three. Figures for the ten most frequent perfect participles are summarized in Table 5.5. So although the majority of perfect stems are of the past perfect type (indeed, this type may be the only synchronically productive type in the Rigveda), the majority of participles are of the stative type.
In terms of the contextual semantics of perfect participles, there is a clear distinction between the two types of perfect stem. Stative perfect participles are very similar in use to present or stative participles, and broadly share the same functional range as stative participles proper. Past perfect participles, on the other hand, unambiguously express a temporal-aspectual relation of precedence between their event time and that of the matrix verb.
... Past perfect participles As stated, past perfect participles specify that the eventuality to which they refer temporally precedes, and is completed prior to, the event time of the matrix verb. This can most obviously be understood as reflecting the anterior aspect of the perfect. In (5.128) the ‘enjoying’ referred to by the perfect participle jujus.a¯n. á- precedes the ‘exhilaration’ of the finite mandasva; in (5.129) the ‘striking’ of the participle jaghanvám. s- temporally precedes the ‘assuming’ referred to

---

<!-- page 230 -->

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 

by the finite verb; and in (5.130) Etaśa is unambiguously stopped after he had started running.50

 The semantics of participles

... Stative perfect participles In contrast to the past perfect participles, the stative perfects display temporal-aspectual reference equivalent to present and stative participles; that is, they refer to an eventuality ongoing at the time of the matrix event. This is clear in the following two examples:

(5.134)

tva¯´m agne támasi

‘All the gods, fearing, paid homage to you,/ O Agni, as you stood in darkness.’

(RV 6.9.7ab)

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 

‘Now by knowing both races of the gods, he obtains what is from there and

‘May we be clothed in riches, son of might,/ and proceed beyond constriction,

... Analysis The perfect participle category is closely aligned in semantic terms with the finite perfect system, both in the sharp semantic and functional distinction between the two types of perfect stem and in the temporal-aspectual reference of both these stem types. The past perfect participles can be analysed as expressing anterior aspect (and perhaps even present tense), which correlates with the anterior reference of the finite past perfect. The stative perfects, on the other hand, can be interpreted as expressing imperfective aspect and/or present tense, just like the present and stative participles. Insofar as anterior reference had been lost in the stative perfect category as a whole, the stative perfect participles share this reference with the finite stative perfect.
The stative perfect participles are considerably more common than past perfect participles, but this is due to the high frequency of a relatively small number of roots. In fact, the majority of stative perfect participles were no longer productive in the language of the Rigveda (Kümmel, 2000a: 69). Moreover, several show evidence of incipient reanalysis as present stems, which reflects their altered tense-aspect reference. Synchronically it is the rarer past perfect participles which represent the functionality of the productive perfect tense-aspect.

.. Future participles
As discussed briefly in §2.3.4, what I refer to as the ‘future’ tense-aspect is a category of somewhat uncertain status in the Rigveda. This is partly due to the paucity of evidence, there being fewer than fifty occurrences of future verb forms in the corpus. It is partly also due to the complex nature of future time reference, which is inherently connected to concepts traditionally treated as modal: possibility, necessity, belief, desire, etc.

---

<!-- page 233 -->

 The semantics of participles

Nevertheless, the future does occupy a distinct semantic niche separate from those of the categories closest to it: the present tense-aspect, the subjunctive mood, and the desiderative.
The modal (or at least atemporal) concept most closely associated with the future is that of ‘intentionality’. A verbal form in the future ‘tense-aspect’ is not simply predictional, that is, it does not (only) make reference to a predicted future occurrence, but indicates that its subject actively intends to bring about the eventuality referred to. In this it contrasts with the subjunctive, which in its most temporal, least modal, uses expresses prediction or likelihood. Th√e contrast is apparent in the following two examples: the finite future form karis.yási ( kr. ‘make’) appears in (5.140), contrasting with the four subjunctives in (5.141).

(5.140)

yád an˙gá da¯śús.e

tvám/ ágne bhadrám. karis.yási/

what.a pcl worshipper.d you Agni.v good.a make.fut.2sg

távét

tát satyám an˙girah.

you.g=pcl that true An˙giras.v

‘That good thing which you, O Agni, will [/intend to] do for the worshipper,/

that very thing of yours is [i.e. comes] true, O An˙giras.’ (RV 1.1.6)

(5.141)

katha¯´ deva¯´na¯m. katamásya ya¯´mani/ sumántu

na¯´ma

how gods.g which.g request.l good_to_think_on.a name.a

śr. n. vata¯´m

mana¯mahe/ kó mr.l.a¯ti

katamó no

hear.prs.ptc.g.pl.m think.sbj.1pl who show_mercy.sbj which us.d

máyas

karat/ katamá u¯t¯´ı abhy a¯´ vavartati

enjoyment.a make.sbj which aid.i to to turn.sbj

‘How and of which of the gods shall [/should] we think on their name, good

to think on, as they listen to our request?/ Who will [/is likely to] show mercy?

Which will [/is likely to] make enjoyment for us? Which will [/is likely to] turn

this way with their aid?’ (RV 10.64.1)

The future form in (5.140) does not express an inevitable future occurrence; if it did, the last pa¯da would be tautologous. Rather, it expresses a definite intention on the part of its subject, the god Agni, to bring about the eventuality referred to. The last three subjunctives in (5.141) are clearly different: the three questions relate to the likelihood of one or another god fulfilling the actions referred. The first subjunctive in (5.141) exemplifies one of the modal functions of the category. It appears in a deliberative question, reflecting either bouletic or deontic modality.52
The subjunctive is a purely finite category, so there can be no direct contrast between subjunctive and future in the participle. The intentional force of the future

52 In the absence of the question words this subjunctive would have an imperatival (often called ‘hortative’) function, giving the meaning ‘let us think on the name . . . ’.

---

<!-- page 234 -->

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 

is clear in the participle, however, and contrasts clearly with the volitional sense of
the desiderative, which is found in both finite and participial forms. The contrast is
best observed where both participles are attested to the same root, as e.g. hanis.yántin (5.142)√and jígha¯m. sant- in (5.143), respectively future and desiderative participles to the root han ‘strike, slay’.

(5.142) átha¯brav¯ıd vr. trám índro hanis.yán then=spoke Vr.tra.a Indra slay.fut.ptc.n.sg.m ‘Then spoke Indra, intending/about to slay Vr.tra.’ (RV 4.18.11c)

(5.143)

drúham. jígha¯m. san

dhvarásam anindra¯´m. / tétikte

lie.a slay.des.prs.ptc.n.sg.m destructive.a un-Indric.a sharpens

tigma¯´ tujáse án¯ıka¯

sharp.a thrust.d tips.a

‘Desiring to slay the destructive, un-Indric lie,/ he sharpens the sharp (spear-)

tips for thrusting.’ (RV 4.23.7ab)

In (5.143) the temporal reference of the desiderative participle is clearly to the

present relative to the event time of the main verb: it expresses the mental cause behind

the action of sharpening the spear-tips. The future participle in (5.142) refers to a more

definite intention on the part of the subject. It is not merely that Indra desires to

slay Vr.tra, but that he actively intends to do so, and is indeed poised to do so. This brings out a further semantic property of the future: the eventuality intended to take

place by the subject is also implied to be relatively imminent. This is different from the

desiderative, which implies nothing as to when or how soon the desired eventuality

may take place, nor even whether it will.

√

Another root that attests both future and desiderative participles is san ‘win’. Un-

like the forms just discussed, however, there are no contexts in which the intentional

sense of the future sanis.yánt-, or the volitional sense of the desiderative sís.a¯sant-, are absolutely distinguishable from the other. So, in (5.144) an intentional sense is certainly

appropriate for the future participle, especially since this is the first verse of the hymn

and can be taken to refer to the imminent sacrifice. The desiderative in (5.145) appears

in a very similar context, and while a volitional sense works, it is not clear that an

intentional sense would be any less appropriate here than in (5.144).53

(5.144) nu¯´ márto dayate sanis.yán now mortal divides win.fut.ptc.n.sg.m
‘Now the mortal who intends to win makes a distribution.’ (RV 7.100.1a)

53 On the synchronic connection of sanis.yánt- to the future stem, regardless of its possible historical origins, see Hoffmann (: ).

---

<!-- page 235 -->

 The semantics of participles

(5.145)

apáh. sís.a¯san

svàr áprat¯ıto/

b´r. haspátir hánty

waters.a win.des.prs.ptc.n.sg.m sun.a unconquerable Br.haspati slays

amítram arkaíh.

foe.a songs.i

‘Desiring to win the waters (and) the sun, unconquerable/ Br.haspati slays the foe with his song.’ (RV 6.73.3cd)

Intention and desire are conceptually close, and the fact that it is not always

possible to fully distinguish them attests to this fact. Intentionality is even included

in the semantic range of the desiderative by Heenen (2006).54 However, the sense of

intentionality is much stronger with the future than with the desiderative, and the

sense of imminent action that very often goes along with this attests to the future

being in this way closer than the desiderative to a future category proper.

Despite their closeness, then, the concepts of intention and desire are distinct, and

forms. The comparing

the ‘splitting apart’ of Vanaspati to a woman in childbirth; the sense of intention,

not flow to anyone who merely desires to sacrifice, but only to those whose intention

is definite and soon to be acted upon.

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 

(5.148) sá íd ásteva

práti dha¯d asis.yán

he pcl archer=like on places shoot.fut.ptc.n.sg.m

‘Like an archer intending/about to shoot he positions (his arrow).’ (RV 6.3.5a)

(5.149) vaks.yánt¯ıvéd

a¯´ gan¯ıganti kárn. am

speak.fut.ptc.n.sg.f=like=pcl to comes.int ear.a

‘Like one intending/about to speak he comes ever to the ear.’ (RV 6.75.3a)

Intended imminent occurrence is close to the implication of actual future occur-

rence, and in many of the passages above we can infer the successful undertaking of

the intended action from the context. (5.150), the future participle saris.yánt-

(T√hissr. i‘srunno’t)

necessarily the case, however. In must be interpreted concessively,

as it expresses an intention that was not carried out.

(5.150)

ví samana¯´ bhu¯´mir aprathis..ta¯´-/ ’ram. sta párvataś cit apart equally earth spread stopped mountain even
saris.yán run.fut.ptc.n.sg.m
‘The earth has spread wide equally (in every direction);/ even the mountain,
though intending/about to to run, has stopped.’ (RV 2.11.7cd)

In tense-aspect terms the ‘future’, despite its name, does not express future tense as such in the Rigveda. Future tense is formalized as the relation P≺R, that is, the reference time of the verb form in question follows (is in the ‘future’ relative to) its perspective time. With the Rigvedic future, however, the reference is rather to an future eventuality viewed from the relative present, at which point the eventuality is merely intended. This can be analysed as present tense combined with posterior aspect. Under present tense the reference time overlaps with (in more formal terms includes) the perspective time (P⊆R). ‘Posterior aspect’ is the label I use for the relation R≺E (the opposite of ‘anterior aspect’), expressing that the event time follows the reference time.55 This tense-aspect combination refers to a time in the relative present during which the eventuality in question is viewed as yet to occur. The sense of imminence commonly found can be treated as a specific feature of this tense-aspect combination in Rigvedic Sanskrit.56 In diachronic terms, a present posterior category can be thought of as a half-way stage in the development from a present imperfective category (with intentional sense) to future tense proper.

55 ‘Posterior’ aspect is the same as that labelled ‘prospective’ by Klein (b: ). 56 Alternatively, it would be possible to capture the sense of imminence in parallel manner to the ‘recent past’ reading of the aorist, i.e. by assuming the immediative tense relation R⊆P (§..). In the case of the
aorist, this non-standard tense relation permits the category to be distinguished from the perfect, but since
there is no such category ‘competing’, as it were, with the present posterior tense-aspect of the future, it is
not strictly necessary to assume immediative tense here. Having said that, it is interesting to note in this
regard the direct semantic parallels between the aorist and future categories in Pa¯n. ini’s analysis of the later language (cf. §A..).

---

<!-- page 237 -->

 The semantics of participles
This captures the temporal-aspectual side of the intentional semantics of the future. The atemporal side, the sense of intentionality itself, can be formally captured as a predicational meaning that takes the lexical meaning of the verb as one of its arguments. This is parallel to the semantic contribution of similar derivational categories such as desideratives and causatives, and also ‘equi’ verbs and some kinds of modals.57 Abstracting away from event semantics, the meaning o√f intention would look something like λx.λP.intend(x, P(x)), so the future stem to kr. ‘make’ would mean roughly λx.λy.intend(x, make(x, y)).
.. Analysis
As discussed above, it is not the case that a morphological category such as a category of tense-aspect stem should necessarily make a consistent semantic contribution in all the forms in which it appears. This is clear enough in the Rigvedic verbal system from the fact that the imperfect tense, which shares the present tense-aspect stem of the finite present and present participle, displays neither imperfective aspect nor present tense at any point in the history of Sanskrit. On the assumption that the tenseaspect stem must have a consistent value across all forms, Kiparsky (1998) treats the present as having (default) perfective aspect, which may work for the imperfect but is not really plausible for the finite present or the present participle. The somewhat varied, but most commonly present-like, semantics of the aorist participle likewise cannot be directly matched up with the tense-aspect properties of the finite aorist. The present participle, on the other hand, does seem to match the finite present; both can be analysed as expressing imperfective aspect, or alternatively both can be analysed as expressing present tense. Even in this case, however, it does not follow that the tense-aspect morpheme itself contributes the shared semantic property, nor that there is necessarily a functional identity of finite and non-finite form at a sublexical level that is somehow overlaid with finite and non-finite morphology and functionality.
Moreover, in considering the interrelations of the tense-aspect properties of the different participle categories, it is important not to impose comparative data onto the evidence of the Rigveda. It could be argued that this is what Dahl (2010) does when he assumes that the participial system of the Rigveda (and indeed the entire verbal system) reflects, almost without change, the tense-aspect system traditionally reconstructed for Proto-Indo-European and attested in Ancient Greek. Such comparisons have value only once the synchronic data have been independently analysed. The evidence we have seen above shows that the present participle can be treated as expressing imperfective aspect, and this correlates with the imperfective aspect of the finite present and with the supposed inherited value of the present tense-aspect stem. The tense-aspect properties of the two types of perfect (in participial and finite forms)
57 On the semantics of equi verbs in particular, see Chierchia (), Reyle (), and Asudeh (; a), and more widely on modality see Kratzer () and Kaufmann et al. ().

---

<!-- page 238 -->

5.5 Tense-aspect in the participial system 
can both be explained diachronically on the basis of an inherited present anterior category. However, the tense-aspect properties of aorist participles cannot be fully correlated with the inherited perfective aspect of the aorist stem.
Not only must we reckon with changes in the verbal system as a whole, we must also reckon with changes in the participial system independent of, though nonetheless related to, the finite system.58 The synchronic evidence of the Rigveda can be understood in terms of an ongoing diachronic development from a participial system that primarily expresses aspect to one that primarily expresses tense. As discussed above, the present participle can be analysed either in terms of imperfective aspect or relative present tense. This is true also of stative participles, and of stative perfect participles, which are both part of categories that are in the process of being reanalysed as part of the present system. On the other hand, participles to the productive past perfect consistently refer to an eventuality as temporally preceding that of the matrix verb; this can be analysed either in terms of anterior aspect or in terms of relative past tense (which may or may not be related or parallel to the development of past-tense reference in the perfect system as a whole). Conversely, the future participle refers to an eventuality that has not yet occurred at the time of the matrix eventuality; this can be analysed in terms of posterior aspect, but could conceivably also be analysed in terms of future tense. These categories, then, are ambiguous, being consistent either with a participial system that primarily expresses aspect or with one that primarily expresses tense.
The problematic status of the aorist participle is particularly interesting in this respect. The aorist participle has no tense-aspect properties that can be attributed to all its forms; instead, it seems at times equivalent to the present participle, at times to the perfect. This makes no sense within a purely aspectual system where perfective, imperfective, and anterior aspects are clearly distinct, nor within an evidentialitybased system in which aorist and perfect had distinct values. It does make sense, however, within a primarily tense-based system, where relative present and past tense are already catered for by the present and perfect respectively, there being no room left for a third non-future participle. The obsolescence of the aorist participle can then be understood against the backdrop of this emergent three-way tense distinction in the participial system.59
The development of tense distinctions in the participial system is evidenced also by the ongoing replacement of the active perfect participle suffix -vám. s- by -at- (the non-ablauting form of -nt-) in the stative perfect participles, but not in the past
58 Developments in the infinitival system, and in other non-finite verbal categories, are questions in their own right and I will make no attempt to discuss them here.
59 Looking further towards Classical Sanskrit, the ultimate obsolescence of the perfect participle can likewise be explained by its replacement by the absolutive. Again, this is explicable only if they both express relative past tense.

---

<!-- page 239 -->

 The semantics of participles
perfects.60 This is part of the transfer of stative perfect stems to the present stem more generally, but the reformation of the participle often precedes that of other verbal forms.61 This development can be analysed as reflecting a strengthening of tense distinctions in participial morphology, ensuring that -vám. s- more consistently appears with past participles (i.e. past perfects), and -nt- more consistently with all non-past participles.62 These two participial suffixes, then, were becoming associated with particular tense properties, -vám. s- with the past, -nt- with the non-past. This may contribute to the explanation of the frequent imperfective (or at least ‘present-like’) use of aorist participles: despite their inherited value they were morphologically closer to the present than the perfect, and so, as aspectual reference was replaced by tense reference in the participial system and the distinct function of the aorist participle was lost, surviving forms were more likely to adopt the properties of present participles.
Since developments in one part of the verbal system do not necessarily correspond to developments in other parts, it cannot be said that this development was necessarily related to similar developments that occurred in the finite system. It is notable that at a later stage the inherited aspectual distinctions were lost in the Middle Indo-Aryan verbal system, and that the perfect, imperfect, and aorist were essentially replaced by a single preterite category, while at the same time a proper future tense develops for the first time. In the Rigveda, it is not clear that non-indicative finite verbal categories express tense and aspect in the same way as the indicative, and it may be that the changes seen in the participles are shared, at least in part, by the non-indicative finite verb. It may be, therefore, that the less prototypically verbal parts of the verbal system were more liable to undergo these developments, while the most prototypically verbal finite indicative was more resistant.

. Conclusion
In this chapter we have explored the semantic properties of tense-aspect stem participles from two main perspectives. I have provided a formal semantic analysis of how the different elements of meaning contributed by participles are combined with the other semantic components of their clause. I have also investigated the variety of contextual functions that converbal participles display. The second part of this chapter treated the temporal-aspectual reference of the different tense-aspect stem participles, showing that although they partially correspond to the finite system in this respect, there is no necessary identity of tense-aspect properties between the participles and finite forms of a particular tense-aspect category.

60

utilize the same suffix.

---

<!-- page 240 -->

5.6 Conclusion 
This completes my analysis of the syntax and semantics of tense-aspect stem participles in the Rigveda. In the next chapter I discuss the category of tense-aspect stem participles itself in more detail, revealing the internal divisions with the category, and showing that some forms traditionally treated as participles are, in fact, not part of the synchronic category.

---

<!-- page 241 -->


The category of participles
In this chapter I discuss the constituency of the category of participles in the Rigveda, attempting to delimit what does and does not count, synchronically, as a tenseaspect stem participle, and considering the ‘edges’ of the category: the forms and categories that are partially or marginally integrated into the participial system, but that deviate in certain ways from the properties of the core participial functionality. I also treat in more detail the membership of two particularly problematic categories of participle, the stative and aorist participles. In doing all this I depend partly on morphological criteria, but also partly on the syntactic and semantic properties of participles discussed in Chapters 4 and 5.
. Overview
.. Frequency and distribution Although there are over 5,000 examples of participles in the Rigveda, representing over 1,000 distinct participial stems, the distribution of these forms is significantly skewed according to the tense-aspect of the forms concerned. Over three-quarters of participles, both instances and distinct stems, are present participles, i.e. participles formed to present tense-aspect stems, while future participles constitute only just over 1 of distinct stems, and only 0.5 of examples. Table 6.1 shows the absolute and relative frequencies of participles and participial stems according to tense-aspect.1
Similarly, there is considerable variation in the frequency of individual participial stems. To take the present participles as an example, more than half the 4,281 examples of present participles in the Rigveda are formed to only 57 stems, roughly 8 of the total 730 present participle stems. The four most common present participle stems (pávama¯na-, sánt-, yánt-, and puna¯ná-, the first and last of these formed to the same root) appear 558 times altogether in the corpus, therefore constituting 13 of the 4,281 present participles found. On the other hand, 304 present participle stems, 41.6 of the total, are found only once in the Rigveda. The figures for the perfect are similar. More
1 Participial stems compounded with preverbs are not counted as distinct stems for the purposes of this table.

than half the roughly 900 perfect participles in the Rigveda are formed to only 16, i.e. 10, of the 153 stems attested. The single most common perfect participle, vidvám. s-, occurs 120 times, over 13 of the total number of perfect participles. On the other hand, 47 perfect participle stems, 31, are attested only once. Table 6.2 shows the five most common present and perfect participles with their number of occurrences and the percentage of the total number of present/perfect participles in the Rigveda these constitute. With the less common tense-aspects the statistics are naturally more liable to variation, but the same patterns are found: the two most common future participles (karis.yánt- and sanis.yánt-) occur seven times each, i.e. fourteen (56) of the total twenty-five future participles in the Rigveda, while seven (58.3) of the twelve future participle stems are found only once.
The analysis of participial functionality is not, then, about bare statistics, for example how many instances of a particular use of participles there may be as against another, since the high frequency of a small number of participles easily skews any such figures. Rather, an analysis of the syntax and semantics of participles must seek the core of participial functionality in those uses that are most widely attested with a

---

<!-- page 243 -->

 The category of participles
range of stems, both frequent and infrequent. As we will see, some of the most frequent participial stems are in fact those that lie at the edges of the category and that deviate in specific ways from more prototypical participles.
.. Core participial properties
The majority of participial stems, and by implication the most prototypical participles, share a set of properties that can be considered ‘core’ to the definition of the category. In this section I present a brief overview of these, which will be of relevance to the discussion of peripheral and non-participial forms below. Some of these properties will be discussed in much more detail in subsequent chapters.
My definition of the category participle in §1.2 includes reference to morphological regularity, such that all the forms considered in this work are morphologically ‘regular’ in basic terms: that is, participles are formed by regular morphological processes that add specific participial suffixes to specific verbal tense-aspect stems. There are degrees of regularity, however. In particular, although we noted above that the absence of a corresponding finite stem cannot be taken as evidence that a form was not synchronically formed to a verbal stem (§2.3.2), in the case of certain frequent forms, and a number of less frequent forms that pattern together in certain ways, the absence of a corresponding finite stem is indicative of non-core, or non-participial, status. Furthermore, certain forms will be discussed that do formally correspond to an attested verbal stem, but that also correspond to a non-verbal derivational pattern; this latter type of correspondence, in conjunction with functional divergence from the formally correspondent verbal stem, is also indicative of non-participial status.
In semantic terms, prototypical participles display the same basic meaning as the verbal stem to which they are formed, which is entirely unremarkable if they are treated as inflectional verb forms (cf. §3.4). Forms that deviate from their correspondent verbal stem in basic semantic terms may therefore be peripheral or nonparticipial.
Closely related to the core semantics of verbal stems is their argument structure. In the vast majority of cases, and hence in the core membership of the category, the grammatical subject of a participle must have the same thematic role in regard to the eventuality expressed by the participle as does the grammatical subject of the corresponding finite verb in regard to the eventuality expressed by the finite verb. So just as the subject of the finite verb kr. n. óti ‘he makes’ is an agent of an eventuality of making, so the subject of the corresponding participle kr. n. vánt- ‘making’ is also an agent of the same sort of eventuality, while the subject of the passive participle kriyáma¯n. a- ‘being made’ is a theme, just as is the subject of the corresponding finite verb kriyáte ‘is made’.
Certain caveats must be added on this point, however. A number of medial participles show patient/theme subjects where corresponding finite forms show rather

A number of factors are at work here. Several forms that appear to diverge from their verbal stems in this way have traditionally been misclassified as participles to present or aorist stems, when in fact they are regular formations to the stative. I discuss such forms in detail in the next section. Additionally, as discussed in more detail below, the existence of formally ambiguous stative participles with patient/theme subjects alongside formally identical present or aorist participles with agentive subjects licensed a degree of analogical extension, such that mediopassive participles developed a limited ability to display patient-oriented argument structure even where corresponding finite forms could not. Besides this, the mediopassive was simply multifunctional, particularly in the perfect where no distinct passive existed, so it is not always possible to exclude the existence of a corresponding patient-oriented finite stem, even when it is not attested.

sive) participle; in fact Kümmel (a: ) also argues against the attribution (originally by Hoffmann,
