analysis. On the intensive forms of this root, see also Jamison (c: –). marmr. ja¯ná- appears in (.).

---

<!-- page 245 -->

 The category of participles
In a few instances the same variation has been attributed to active participles, but in fact all such forms are non-participial; this is discussed in §7.2.2. Genuine active participles do attest other sorts of variation. Tucker (2002b: 291–2) notes that the present participle siñcánt- ‘pourin√g out’, while corresponding morphologically to the finite stem siñcáti ‘pours out’ to sic ‘pour’, does not correspond to it in terms of argument structure, being intransitive (unaccusative) while the latter is agentivetransitive; syntactically it corresponds rather to the unaccusative class 4 present sicyáte ‘pours out’. Tucker proposes a more general rule, that present participles to transitive thematic (class 6) nasal stems with corresponding intransitive class 4 presents can themselves be intransitive, being therefore synchronically the participle to the class 4 present as well as or instead of the participle to the nasal present. We must assume therefore a synchronic suppletive relationship between the finite class 4 stem and participles to transitive thematic nasal stems. In the case of siñcánt- we must say ‘as well as’, since at least half the Rigvedic occurrences of the participle do syntactically correspond to the finite nasal present stem.
Beside variation in subject ‘orientation’, variation in valency is also found. Prototypically, participles correspond fully in their argument structure to related finite stems; that is, as the finite kr. n. óti is transitive, with an agent subject and patient object (‘he makes X’), so the participle kr. n. vánt- is transitive with agent subject and patient object (‘making X’). Statistically participles lack non-subject arguments more frequently than corresponding finite stems, but in fact the majority of variation is restricted to a limited set of frequent but categorially peripheral participles (cf. §§4.8.4, 6.6).
As discussed in Chapter 5, participles have a potentially wide variety of contextual functions. However, some forms are restricted in the kinds of function in which they can occur. It is impossible to prove, of course, that a particular form could never occur in a particular function. If, however, a form which is of questionable status for other reasons happens also to be restricted to the same functional range as non-participial adjectives, this can reasonably be used as supporting evidence for synchronic nonparticipial status, particularly if the word in question is frequent and the likelihood of chance non-attestation of such functions is accordingly reduced.
. Stative and aorist participles
In this section I consider the categories of stative and aorist participles, the constituencies of which have by no means been accurately established by previous scholarship. In the case of the stative, there exist several participles whose adherence to the category has not previously been recognized; conversely, in the case of the aorist, a significant number of participles traditionally attributed to the aorist are, in fact, associated with other categories.

---

<!-- page 246 -->

6.2 Stative and aorist participles 

.. Stative participles

As noted above, I treat the stative as a distinct tense-aspect in this work, but in

synchronic terms it was formally and functionally in the process of assimilation into

the present. Its ambiguous status prejudices the analysis of relevant participial forms,

but even so it is possible to distinguish a number of forms that are, or were, synchronic

stative participles. In the case of finite forms, the distinct person/number endings

sometimes allow us to state in clear morphological terms whether a particular form

is present or stative, but in the case of the participle we cannot be so definitive. For

example, the of the stative

pfoarrmadiagvmasoraf n√(RvVas

4.2.19) ‘wear’,

is the which

only surviving unambiguous example was the inherited stem underlying the

synchronic root present váste (Kümmel 1996: 97–9). The participle vása¯na- ‘wearing’,

however, is entirely ambiguous: diachronically we may confidently call it a stative

participle, but synchronically it is as much a present participle, and can only be

considered to retain any stative affiliation to the extent that the stative still existed

as a distinct tense-aspect stem in this root. In the case of vása¯na- there is little we

can say, since the stative stem was simply rebuilt as a present, such that there is no

morphological or semantic difference between the stems that might distinguish sta√tive participle from present. The same is in principle true of śáya¯na- ‘lying (down)’ to ś¯ı

‘lie’, although here finite forms of the root are consistently stative rather than present

in early Vedic (the only exception is a single optative form, noted by Kümmel, 1996:

109–10), meaning that we can more confidently label śáya¯na- a stative participle at

this stage of the language. This problem is limited to those roots where the stative

was directly converted into a present: in other roots we can more securely distinguish

stative from present participles, since the two stems are semantically distinct, the

former having a stative, often patient-oriented, sense, while the lat√ter has a dynamic, often agentive, sense. This is perhaps best exemplified in the root duh ‘milk’, where

the participles dúha¯na- ‘giving milk, productive’ and dúgha¯na- ‘id.’ pattern with the

stative duhé ‘gives milk, is productive’, while duha¯ná- patterns with the dynamic, agent-

oriented present dógdhi ‘milks’ (Kümmel 1996: 58).

Besides confusion with the present (and as we will see, some√times the aorist), a few roots show ambiguity between stative and perfect. The root ¯ıś ‘rule’, for example,

with (lexicalized) participle ¯´ıśa¯na-/¯ıśa¯ná- ‘ruler, ruling’, is formally ambiguous be-

cause the reduplicated perfect and non-reduplicated stative stems are homophonous;

the finite stem, 3sg. ¯´ıśe ‘rules’, is widely taken as a perfect, but Kümmel (2000a: 123–4)

has the

argued that it medialization

is more likely of the stem if

‘know’, which forms a perfect stem without reduplication, it is all but impossible to

distinguish perfect middle from stative morphologically; nevertheless Kümmel (1996:

104) discerns a partial distinction between the two, particularly in the participle, where

the accentual difference between vída¯na- and vida¯ná- almost entirely corresponds to

 The category of participles

a semantic distinction, the former having patient-oriented meaning (the stative)6 and

the latter agentive-reflexive (the perfect).7 In both these roots an accentual difference

might serve to distinguish stative from perfect in the participle, but we will see below

that accent is not a reliable indicator in this context.

Despite the many √possible ambiguities,√certain stative participles are well e√stablished: gr. n. a¯ná- to g¯r. ‘prais√e’, cíta¯na- to cit ‘appea√r’,8 dúha¯na- and dúgha¯na- to
d√uh ‘milk’, dr. śa¯ná-/dr´. śa¯na- to dr. ś ‘see’, bruva¯n. á- to bru¯ ‘say, call’ and stáva¯nato stu ‘praise’.9 These are attributable to the stative tense-aspect on the basis of

formal and functional correspondence to attested finite stative stems in distinction,

for example, from potentially correspondent present stems. Besides these well-

established stative participles there are several forms, usually listed in grammars as

aorist participles, that are also best considered under the category of stative participles.

Several of these have already been connected to either the stative or the passive aorist

by Kulikov (2006a).

As noted above (§2.3.4.3) Kulikov (2006a; 2006b: 64–7; 2009: 78–9) and Jasanoff

(2002; 2003a) have argued that the passive aorist could form participles in the

Rigveda. In fact very few patient-oriented aorist participles correspond to attested

finite passive aorists; and even when they do, the productivity of the passive aorist

in Sanskrit makes any such pattern likely to be coincidence.10 It may be possible to

reconstruct a passive aorist, but it is never necessary to do so merely on the basis

of a patient-orien√ted ‘being eaten’ to ad

participle. For ‘eat’ at 4.19.9a

example, Kulikov (2006a: 55) argues reflects an unattested passive aorist

that ∗a¯´di

mean roughly the same thing; in fact it is more likely to be a nonce-creation based on

the present stem, after the analogy of other patientive -a¯ná- participles, as discussed

below (§6.4.2).11

<!-- page 248 -->

6.2 Stative and aorist participles 

The origin of the Indo-Iranian passive aorist is controversial. The only fully distinct

form is the 3sg., which could be a noun in origin, and if so any participle connected

to the formation would necessarily be secondary.12 As Kümmel (1996: 20–21) argues,

the 3pl. and (Avestan) imperative forms of the passive aorist were only secondarily

associated with the passive aorist (previously existing only in the 3sg.), but were

originally part of the morphologically and semantically similar stative paradigm. Now

since it is uncontroversial that ‘stative participles’, i.e. participles corresponding to

finite stative paradigms, are found in the Rigveda, it would be better to explain the

unexpectedly patient-oriented participles of the Rigveda only by association with an

inherited construction that derives a variety of forms, such as imperative and optative,

and that can be demonstrated to form participles in the Rigveda, than with a histori-

cally unclear formation whose only independent form is the 3sg. indicative/injunctive

and which, if it were to have associated participles, may well have imported them from

the stative anyway.

In some roots, however, patientive participles cannot correspond morphologically

to attested statives, while they could correspond both formally and functionally to

attested aorist passives. A good example is suva¯ná- ‘pressed’, which is att√ributed by Kulikov (2006a: 61) to the aorist passive ása¯vi ‘was pressed’ of the root su ‘press’,

while the attested stative is built to the nasal present stem, sunvé ‘is pressed’. In fact it

is not the only such patientive participl√e to correspond functionally but not formally

to a nasal kindled’,13

vsrta¯ant.iáv-e:‘coidvhea¯rendá,-en‘kcilnodsleedd’’((√vidr. h‘co‘vkeinr’d)lteo’)vcr. na. nvé,b14e

compared to indhé√‘is hiya¯ná- ‘impelled’ ( hi

‘impel’) to hinvé ‘is impelled’, also possibly samara¯n. á- to r. n. vé.15 These nasal statives

are a secondary, Vedic creation (Kümmel, 1996: 13) and could reflect an inherited root

stative, in the case of RV śr. n. vé ‘is famed’ ((6.68)) actually preserved in Old Avestan sruii¯e ‘id.’ according to Kümmel (1996: 152–4).16 The patientive suva¯ná- ((6.3)) must then reflect the original stative ∗suvé, replaced by the nasal stative sunvé ((6.4)), which

12 On the possible origins of the passive aorist see Kümmel (: –, ). A nominal origin was
first proposed by Osthoff apud Streitberg (: ), followed and further discussed by e.g. Reichelt (:
–), Hirt (: ; : ; –, vol. : ), Burrow (b: ), and Lubotsky (: ); for
alternative analyses, see e.g. Insler (a) and Jasanoff (a: f.). The most important studies on its
function are those by Migron () and Kümmel (). 13 On indhé, see Kümmel (a: , fn. ). 14 ví . . . vr. n. vé ‘opens’ at ..a, which shows sg. agreement with a nt.pl. subject according to Goto¯
(: ). 15 The conne√ction between these last two forms is uncertain due to the complicated relations of the two

than because they are formed to different roots. 16 OAv. sruiie¯ (Y..) is a problematic form; competing interpretations are as a sg. passive (Humbach
: vol. : ; : vol. : ) or as an infinitive (Insler : ).

6.2 Stative and aorist participles 

None of these participles corresponds morphologically to an attested stative, in the way that e.g. dúha¯na- corresponds to duhé, but the lack of formal correspondence is due to the analogical reformation of the finite paradigms as nasal statives, rather than

17 As discussed by Kümmel (: –), the distinction between the stative and present in this root was partially lost at the time of the Rigveda, since e.g. the pl. stative hinviré is often found in agentivereflexive function. Nevertheless, the pattern discussed here is still clearly visible in the genuinely stative forms of the root.

<!-- page 251 -->

 The category of participles
any process affecting the participles themselves. These participles have traditionally been labelled ‘aorist’ on the grounds that their roots form unmarked ‘root’ aorists. There is only √one possible finite stative form in the Rigveda which is built to an aoristic root, cité to cit ‘appear’. This single example does, in fact, have a corresponding participle, cíta¯na- (RV 9.101.11b). Besides this form, the lack of statives to aorist roots is at least partially due to the fact that several have been rebuilt as nasal statives, which is also the cause of the formal lack of correspondence between the original stative participles and their attested finite stative counterparts.18
Kulikov (2006a) assigns the patient-oriented occurrences of the participles yuja¯ná‘yoked’ and sr. ja¯ná- ‘released’ to the passive aorist; however, we can easily assume the existence of earlier stative paradigms, with 3sg. ∗yujé and ∗sr. jé respectively, supported by the 3pl. passive aorist, i.e. inherited stative, forms ayujran and ásr. gram/n. So yuja¯náand sr. ja¯ná- are better treated as stative participles. Synchronically unambiguous finite stative forms are not attested to these roots, but in any case the participles still pattern as stative participles, parallel to suva¯ná- etc. discussed above.19
Also stative are the participles stava¯ná- ‘praised’ and stuva¯ná- ‘id.’, both functionally equivalent to the morphologically regular stative participle stáva¯na-. According to Kümmel (1996: 131–6) stava¯ná- derives from stáva¯na- by change of accent, while stuva¯ná- is the original present middle participle (cf. Greek steu˜tai ‘boast’) which had become confused with the stative. The patient-oriented forms of huva¯ná- ‘called upon’ are more difficult to explain. Kulikov (2006a: 55–6) assumes an unattested passive aorist, but they are better analysed synchronically as stative: no stative is attested (Kümmel 1996: 142), but a stative would not be unexpected to such a root; alternatively the participle may have analogical origins, formed af√ter the stative participle bruva¯n. áto the semantically and phonologically similar root bru¯ ‘call’.
The highly problematic kra¯n. á- correspon√ds morphologically, but not functionally, to the frequent mediopassive aorist stem of kr. ‘make’. Not all instances of kra¯n. á- can be clearly interpreted, but some appear to be used as nouns ‘work, thing made/done’ ((6.14)), others appear to be used as adverbs ‘powerfully, effectively’ ((6.15)), and a few are unambiguously patient-oriented participles ‘(being) made’ ((6.16)). Geldner (RV)

however, the ablaut and accent do not match in this case. 19 Diachronically there may be an alternative explanation for yuja¯ná-: although in the Rigveda the

mediopassive root aorist is agentive while an active root aorist is not found, in Old Avestan there exists

an agentive active root aorist Y..) is patientive. If then

secondary development, yuja¯ná- ‘yoked’ may simply represent the original value of the mediopassive root

aorist participle. Diachronically we cannot be certain which is the correct explanation of yuja¯ná-, but

synchronically it remains the case that yuja¯ná- ‘yoked’ does not correspond to the RV mediopassive root

aorist but rather patterns as a stative.

6.2 Stative and aorist participles 

interpreted some instances as agentive-transitive participles, presumably to save the connection to the invariably agent-oriented mediopassive aorist, but the best overall analysis is still that of Oldenberg (1903; Noten, vol. 1: 58 ad 1.58.3): kra¯n. á- was originally a patient-oriented participle from which a noun developed by substantivization from the neuter singular, and from which also an adverb developed from the instrumental singular. The most natural explanation for kra¯n. á-, then, is as an inherited stative participle, implying a finite stative ∗kré ‘is made’; however it must be admitted that there is no other evidence, beside the highly problematic kra¯n. á-, for such a paradigm.

to succeed, follow his skill.’ (RV 1.134.2a–e)

There are several other participles that likewise show unexpectedly patient-oriented
semantics in contrast to regularly agent-oriented finite stems: añja¯ná- ‘anointed’ beside an˙kte ‘anoints’ (sometimes ‘is anointed’); iya¯ná- ‘implored’ beside ¯´ımahe ‘we
implore’; tanva¯ná- ‘stretched out’ (RV 9.22.6a) beside tanuté ‘stretches’; dádha¯na-
‘established’ (RV 1.141.13b) beside dhatté ‘places’; mr. ja¯ná- ‘groomed’ beside mr. s..té ‘grooms’; rin. a¯ná- ‘melting, melted’ beside rin. ¯ıté ‘makes (over)flow’. Some of these may reflect unattested stative stems, but with no positive evidence except the semantics of
the participles themselves, this cannot be anything more than conjecture. In fact, given
that the distinction between stative and present was breaking down, and given that

---

<!-- page 253 -->

 The category of participles

some stative and present participles either already were or had become identical (e.g.

índha¯na- both present ‘kindling’ and stative ‘kindled’), it is quite conceivable that the

apparent lability of these participles could have been analogically extended to other

stems, where perhaps no stative paradigm had ever existed.20 Once again, then, the

division between present and stative becomes unclear: is a participle with analogical

patient orientation itself synchronically a stative, or part of a present participle

system that is in this one respect functionally divergent from the corresponding

finite system? I will call such forms ‘statives’ here, but their synchronic status was

surely more ambiguous; the analogical extension of patient-oriented semantics in the

participle system, and its significance for the question of paradigmatic coherence will

be discussed further in the following section.

Kulikov (2006a: 56–7) argues for a ‘weak tendency to generalize the root accentua-

tion’ for stative participles, on the analogy of the common stative participles śáya¯na-

and stáva¯na-. This might explain, for example, the unexpected root accentuation

of índha¯na-, dú(g)ha¯na-, and cíta¯na-, but such a weak tendency clearly did not

extend to all stative participles, and moreover it does not follow that any participle

unexpectedly accented on the root reflects a stative. In fact there is a tendency

for participles of all sorts, particularly those with present tense reference, to adopt

root accentuation. The form índha¯na-, for example, is unexpectedly accented on the

root whether functionally stative ‘kindled’ or present ‘kindling’, as are corresponding

finite forms. reanalysis of

The the

accent of originally

the present participle ínvantclass 5 present inóti ‘impels’

t‘oim√p√eilli‘nimg’paenl’ti(cRipIVatEeLs EthXe:

411–21) as a class 1 present ínvati to the secondary root inv; the accentuation

of the present participle ánniyant- ‘desiring food’, denominative to ánna- ‘food’ is

likewise irregular, though in this case it may be based on the noun. Similarly, perfect

participles with present time reference often retract their accent onto the initial

syllable, the √first stage of reanalysis as a present, e.g. d¯´ıdhya¯na- ‘having thought, thinking’ to dh¯ı ‘think’ (Kümmel 2000a: 257–60). In a few forms there is no clear

explanation for accent retraction, e.g. in dyúta¯na- ‘having lit up’ √beside dyuta¯ná‘id.’, morphologically and functionally regular aorist participles to dyut ‘light up,

shine’.

Kulikov (2006a: 58) further proposes a semantic difference between accentually

distinct forms of some participles, which he attributes to distinct stative and ‘passive

a√orist’ paradigms. So he contrasts the two accentual variants of the root participle to dr. ś, translating dr. śa¯ná- ((6.18)) ‘visible’ but d´r. śa¯na- ((6.17)) ‘appearing (lit. seen)’;
however, the text supports no such distinction:21

20 This process also feeds, and is fed by, other processes of analogical extension affecting participles in
-a¯na-, discussed in the following section. 21 Indeed, Kulikov’s ‘visible’ is a more appropriate gloss for a stative, and ‘seen’ for a passive aorist.

O√n the other hand a semantic distinction between yáta¯na- and yata¯ná-, participles to yat ‘order, arrange’, is not inconceivable. The former occurs twice, with the sense ‘ordered’, as in (6.19); the single instance of the latter (6.20) is semantically and syntactically more similar to some occurrences of the present participle, expressing a change of state rather than a state itself, as the instrumental agent suggests.

The question is whether we are willing to assume the existence of a stative merely on the interpretation of two occurrences of a participle.22 In sum, while there does appear to be a tendency for stative participles to generalize root accentuation, this is simply part of a more general tendency found throughout the participial system, such that accentuation cannot be used as evidence for a particular form being stative rather than, say, present, in the absence of any other evidence.

22 Even if we accept that, there is nothing obvious with which to connect the non-stative yata¯ná-; cf. end of §...

---

<!-- page 255 -->

 The category of participles

... Conclusion The category of stative participles is, in its core membership, clearly distinct from that of the present, perfect, and aorist participles, yet in its more debatable members is actually very hard to distinguish from those categories, the present in particular. Forms like dú(g)ha¯na- and gr. n. a¯ná- are unambiguously stative, patterning morphologically and functionally with attested finite stative stems. As I have argued, there exists also a small group of stative participles, idha¯ná-, suva¯ná-, hiya¯ná-, etc., which correspond functionally but not morphologically to attested finite stative stems, since the finite stems have been rebuilt as nasal statives. Several other participles are ambiguous, since they are part of verbal paradigms where the stative stem was no longer clearly distinct from the present either morphologically or functionally. Finally, there are several participles that show unexpected patient-oriented semantics in at least some of their occurrences, contrasting with consistently agentoriented finite stems with which they otherwise appear to be associated; some or all of these may reflect unattested stative stems, but there is no positive evidence in either direction. The final constituency of the category of stative participles in the Rigveda must remain uncertain, then, reflecting the ongoing obsolescence of this tense-aspect.

.. Aorist participles
Aorist participles in Sanskrit are almost entirely confined to the Rigveda; only a very few are found in later Vedic texts, and they are entirely absent from the Classical language; moreover, they are characteristic of the earlier layers of the Rigveda according to Arnold’s (1905: 31) classification. In fact aorist participles are even rarer than usually assumed. Many of the forms listed as ‘aorist participles’ in reference works are so categorized not because of a clear association with the aorist, but only because they lack any clear association with the present or perfect stem; they are then merely the set of participles, or participle-like forms, that do not clearly fit into any of the other categories. Only just over one half of forms widely labelled active aorist participles actually correspond morphologically to an attested active aorist stem, and the corresponding figure for mediopassive participles is less than one-fifth.
The set of forms that have been labelled ‘aorist participles’ by earlier authors is, in reality, a varied group of forms with differing origins. It is notable that all but ten of the forms traditionally labelled as aorist participles are formed directly to the verbal root, i.e. there is no (or at least a zero) tense-aspect formant.23, 24 The vast majority of forms

24 A similar pattern is found in Avestan. There are only around ten aorist participles found in Avestan

(listed by Kellens : –); most are athematic root formations, as in Sanskrit, but two are identifiable

---

<!-- page 256 -->

6.2 Stative and aorist participles 

are, therefore, morphologically ambiguous: they could be formed to the zero-marked ‘root’ aorist, but they could equally be formed to a root present, a root stative, an unreduplicated perfect, or could indeed be non-verbal (i.e. lexical adjectives derived from verbal roots rather than inflectional participles formed to tense-aspect stems).25
As argued in §6.2.1, for example, several forms widely inte√rpreted as aorist participles are in fa√ct stative participles, e.g. suva¯ná- ‘pressed’ to su ‘press’, and yáta¯na‘ordered’ to yat ‘order’. The forms idha¯ná-, yuja¯ná-, and sr. ja¯ná- occur in both agent-oriented (‘kindling’, ‘yoking’, and ‘releasing’ respectively) and patient-oriented (‘kindled’, ‘yoked’, ‘released’) senses; the agent-oriented occurrences are synchronically aorist, while the patient-oriented are stative. √ √Similarly, the forms k´r. pama¯n. a- ‘lam√enting’ ( kr. p ‘lament’), jus.áma¯n. a- ‘enjoying’ ( jus. ‘enjoy’), and śus.ánt- ‘snorting’ ( śvas ‘snort’) have all been labelled aorist participles but are best treated instead as present participles. Lubotsky (1997a: 1426) lists śus.ánt- as a thematic aorist participle, but from a formal and functional perspective it is the expected form of the present participle to the root present śvásiti ‘snorts’, while the more common form of the present participle, śvasánt-, shows analogical levelling of the full-grade root (Jamison 1983b: 7). The former two are built to thematized aorist stems that were undergoing reanalysis as present stems in the Rigveda, and the participles are best treated as forms of the secondary present stem rather than the original aorist. jus.áma¯n. a- occurs at RV 4.23.1 alongside, and functionally equivalent to, the present participles píbant- and uśa¯ná-, and contrasting semantically with the regular aorist participle jus.a¯n. á- in the previous pa¯da.

 The category of participles

k´r. pama¯n. a- appears only at RV 1.116.14d and 1.119.8a (and once in the Atharvaveda); although it is in principle compatible with a thematic aorist (Jamison 1983a: 56–7), in
fact thematic aorist participles are almost nonexistent in the Rigveda, and functionally
it seems better aligned with the present, even though the finite present stem is
unambiguously attested only in the form kr. pate at RV 1.113.10c.26 Almost no perfect participles have been misanalysed as aorist, since the perfect is for
the most part unambiguously marked by reduplication. O√ne exception is the frequent manda¯ná-, a regular perfect middle participle fro√m mad ‘become exhilarated’ (Kümmel 2000a: 358), not an aorist participle from mand ‘exhilarate’ (as Lubotsky
1997a: 1035).
Certain forms widely labelled as aorist participles are in fact non-participial
adjectives. The following forms are Caland adjectives (Lowe 2012b; 2013c; and cf.
§6.10): citánt- ‘brilliant, excellent’, juránt- ‘old’, dhr. s.ánt- ‘bold’, śucánt- ‘blazing, bright, pure’, tr. s.a¯n. á- ‘thirsty’, vipa¯ná- ‘inspired’, śvita¯ná- ‘bright’, and piśa¯ná- ‘decorated’. The supposed aorist participle ∗tr. pánt- is in fact an adverb tr. pát (§6.9). A number of forms are not synchronically aligned with any verbal stem, but are merely poetic nonce-formations (§6.4): ∗stha¯´nt-, háyant-, huvánt-, ada¯ná-, áceta¯na-, ásridha¯na-, nr. táma¯na-. Synchronically rís.ant-/r¯´ıs.ant- ‘harmer’ is a noun, while diachronically it may be better treated as a present participle than as an aorist (§6.6.1). The stems kr. tát-, guhát-, and vidát- are found only in governing compounds, so their synchronic status
is somewhat problematic, but we can at least say that as first members in governing
compounds they have non-participial origins and function (see §6.7.2).
Once these spurious forms are excluded, we are left with fewer than fifty stems
that can reasonably be considered aorist participles. There are √fifteen active aorist participles, o√f which eight are hapax √legomena: r. dhánt- (×1, r.√dh ‘accomplish’), kdr(áhn)át-ks.(a×t-1,(×4√k,r.√‘mdaahke‘b’)u, rgnm’)á, npat¯´-nt(-×(3×, 2, g√ampa¯‘g‘dor’)in, kd’√y)u,2t7ábnhti-d(á×nt3-, (×d1,y√utb‘hliigdh‘stpulipt’’)),, r√udhánt- (×1, rudh ‘keep, rest√rain’), vidhánt- (×30, vidh ‘w√orship’), vr. dhánt- (×6, √vr. dh ‘increase’), śis.ánt- (×√1, śa¯s ‘instruct’), sádant- (×4, √sad ‘sit’), sánant- (×1,

Notably, all active aorist participles are derivationally regular insofar as they all have

attested corresponding finite active aorist forms, with the single exception of dyutánt-

which, however, occurs beside an s-aorist ádyaut, strongly suggesting an earlier root

aorist (Narten 1964: 148).

T√he only two aorist participles to the distinctive√s-aorists are d(h)áks.at- ‘burning’ to dah ‘burn’ and prasáks.at- ‘prevailing’ to pra- sah ‘prevail, conquer’. Morphologically they have been analysed in various ways. Wackernagel–Debrunner (AiG,

vol. 3: 162, but apparently contradicted at p. 262) considered prasáks.at- an s-aorist participle but d(h)áks.at- from a present stem dhaks.-. Renou (1937) considered both secondary, derived from the aorist imperatives dháks.i and sáks.i. However, as discussed by Narten (1964: 134), the non-ablauting suffix of d(h)áks.at- and prasáks.at- is unexpected if the forms are recent or s√econdary: the influence of non-ablauting participles like táks.at-, present participle to taks. ‘construct’, would have been weaker than that of the more common ablauting pa√rticiples such √as náks.ant-, ráks.ant√-, and yáks.ant-, respectively present participles to naks. ‘reach’, raks. ‘protect’, and yaks. ‘reveal’.29 The inherited acrostatic accent and non-ablauting suffix of the s-aorist participles

suggests then that these participles are genuinely archaic, whether or not s-aorist

participles were ever a regular or productive formation in PII or PIE.

All other active aorist participles are formed to root or thematic stems.30 The only

common active aorist participle is the clearly lexicalized vidhánt- ‘worshipper’ (cf.

28 For yuva¯ná- as an aorist participle, see Kümmel (a: ), contra the traditional interpretation as
a root present participle (as e.g. Jamison a: , fn. ). 29 Besides the occurrence of prasáks.at- at .. ((.)), the form sáks.anta at .. has also been
analysed as a form of sáks.at-. This form could only, however, reflect an ablauting stem; in fact the form is best interpreted as a morphologically regular finite verbal form (or, as argued by Narten : –, a
nonce-formation). 30 It is not always possible to tell the difference, and we generally rely on the existence of a finite thematic
aorist stem to establish that a particular participle is thematic. For example sánant-, usually assumed to be
a thematic aorist participle on the basis of the thematic aorist stem with which it appears to be associated in its one occurrence (..c), c√ould alternatively reflect the expected but unattested class  present ∗sana¯´ti beside sanóti to the set. root san ‘win’, or might in principle reflect a root aorist (for which there is no evidence). Under any analysis the accent of sánant- and the apparent corresponding finite form sánema is
difficult to explain (cf. Dahl : , fn. ). For this participle at least we have comparative evidence
for its thematic status: the corresponding form in Avestan (which preserved better, but not absolutely, the
paradigmatic distinction between thematic and athematic -nt- stems, cf. Strunk : –), hanan. t-, is unambiguously thematic.

 The category of participles

√ §6.6.3). The next most common active aorist participle is vr. dhánt- ( vr. dh ‘increase’), attested only six times, while half the remaining forms are hapax legomena. The active aorist participle is therefore a very rare and minor part of the RV verbal system, not at all in keeping with the importance of the finite active aorist, and notably rarer than the mediopassive aorist participles. Despite this, as noted above, almost all active aorist participles are morphologically regular, and they are also syntactically regular: for example, they all display the same argument structure patterns of corresponding finite verbal stems, even showing parallel variation where finite forms vary; so vr. dhánt-, like corresponding finite forms, is found in both transitive ((6.22), repeating (4.108)) and intransitive ((6.23), repeating (4.109)) uses.

The semantic properties of aorist participles were discussed in §5.5.3, but it is worth noting here that from a temporal-aspectual perspective the most clearly ‘aoristic’ of the aorist participles are all active. Despite their rarity, then, their morphological, syntactic, and even partial semantic correspondence to the aorist system shows that although the active aorist participle was obsolescent in the language of the Rigveda, it was nevertheless relatively well aligned with the aorist tense-aspect.
In contrast to the relatively regular active aorist participles, only a few of the genuine mediopassive aorist participles correspond both formally and functionally to finite mediopassive aorist stems, even after spurious examples such as the stative forms of idha¯ná- etc., discussed above, are discounted. This is perhaps surprising since they are more than twice as frequent as the active aorist participles in terms both of number of stems and of number of absolute occurrences, and so appear at first sight less obsolescent as a category.31

31 Summarizing the figures from above, there are more than double the number of mediopassive aorist participle stems than there are active, and a total of  occurrences beside only  individual occurrences of active aorist participles.

The majority of mediopassive aorist participles, however, display no clear perfective

or anterior reference. In addition, relatively few mediopassive aorist participles have

corresponding finite stems. That this is the case with the mediopassive in contrast

with the active suggests a degree of productivity on the part of the mediopassive

aorist participle, which also explains its relative frequency in comparison to the

active.

Many mediopassive aorist participles that lack corresponding finite stems may

have been formed on the basis of paradigmatic analogy, primarily with the perfect

mediopassive. For√example, on the analogy of regular pairs such as perfect jujus.a¯n. á-: aorist jus.a¯n. á- to jus. ‘enjoy’, it would have been possible to back-form aorist par-

32 This is the opposite of Jasanoff ’s (: §, pp. –) argument that the productivity of perfect middle participles (and the perfect middle generally) originated in the reinterpretation of root aorist middle participles as unreduplicated perfect participles, leading to redetermination by reduplication, e.g. jujus.a¯n. áfrom jus.a¯n. á-. Jasanoff also argues that the same process may underlie the parallel productivity of the perfect middle in Ancient Greek, e.g. beble¯ménos ‘thrown’ from an originally stative aorist ble¯´menos ‘thrown’ to bállo¯ ‘I throw’, the punctual aorist participle being due to the later systematization of the aspectual oppositions present/aorist/perfect. Functionally and paradigmatically, however, it makes more sense to assume, at least for RV Sanskrit, that the problematic mediopassive aorist participles are the secondary creations.

‘. . . (the rivers) which are fruitful, good givers of milk, of good streams,/

Analogy of this kind is often the most reasonable explanation for medial aorist participles which have no corresponding finite aorist stems, no evidence of a stative, and no obvious alternative explanation. The influence of the perfect on the creation of these ‘aorist’ participles is clear from its effect not only on the tense-aspect reference of some of these secondary forms but also on t√heir argument structure. The participle vr. dha¯ná- is the only medial aorist form of vr. dh ‘grow’ attested in the RV. It is usually intransitive, meaning ‘strengthened, increased’; once it is agentive-reflexive ‘strengthening oneself ’, with tanvàm ‘self ’ as object. A finite medial aorist has no obvious analogical support, but the existence of the (secondary) medial perfect with both transitive and intransitive argument structure provides support both formally and functionally for the medial aorist participle. The fact that the medial perfect can display both transitive and intransitive uses is due to a series of analogical developments affecting the perfect system (described by Kümmel 2000a: 469–73): first the originally intransitive active perfect is medialized, e.g. vavárdha ‘is grown strong’ becomes va¯vr. dhé ‘id.’; subsequently the active adopts the agentive sense found in the active present, in opposition to the new intransitive middle, e.g. vavárdha ‘has strengthened’ after present várdhati ‘strengthens’; finally the medial perfect optionally adopts an agentive-reflexive sense in opposition to the active, e.g. va¯vr. dhé ‘has strengthened him/herself ’. It is this variation, which develops specifically in the medial perfect, that is transferred to the analogical aorist participle when it is created on the basis of the p√erfect. Likewise, although in most of its occurrences sr. ja¯ná- is a stative participle to sr. j ‘release’, with patient-oriented sense ‘released’, two are agent oriented (‘releasing’) and must be treated formally as aorist participles; the only support for agent orientation in the mediopassive comes from the perfect, where mediopassive forms can be agent-oriented or patient-oriented.
In other cases the variable argument structure of some aorist participles cannot be explained purely on the basis of the perfect, but is also influenced by the apparent variation in roots where homophonous stative and mediopassive aorist participles

---

<!-- page 262 -->

participle didyuta¯ná-, since the only old s√tems to this root are all active.33 The creation of prasaha¯ná- ‘conquering’ (10.99.2c) to sah ‘conquer’ may have been influenced by

of the opaque reduplicated stem sa¯h- seen in the perfect active participle sa¯hvám. s- (cf. §2.3.4.4); the form might alternatively be a Caland adjective (cf. §6.10).

that aorist participles are almost exclusively formed to the zero-marked root aorist

rather than to the productive aorist stems (the thematic, -s- and reduplicated stems)

Evidence for a root aorist comes from OAv. (scan. tu¯) and Greek (héspeto, spésthai); however the Old Avestan evidence is scanty and Kümmel (a: , fn. ) notes that the Greek aorist could be an inner-Greek innovation. Functionally saca¯náitself is indistinguishable from the present participle, cf. (.) and (.).

---

<!-- page 263 -->

 The category of participles
demonstrates that synchronically the aorist participle was no longer a fully productive verbal formation. Its obsolescence is often attributed to loss of functional distinction between aorist and perfect participles, and the emerging absolutive, which later consigned also the perfect participle to obsolescence (cf. Tikkanen 1987: 216–21, 226). But it is not immediately clear why the aorist and perfect should have become functionally equivalent in the participle, but not (at this stage) in the finite system; moreover, in most instances aorist participles appear functionally more similar to present participles than perfect. An explanation for the obsolescence of the aorist participle was suggested in §5.5.6.
The category of aorist participles in the Rigveda, then, is smaller than traditionally assumed and undoubtedly obsolescent, but not without some productivity (though not paradigmatic productivity). It consists of a core of regular forms, and shows analogical extension in the mediopassive beyond the bounds of the finite system. This corresponds to what we saw in Chapter 5: in semantic terms the aorist participle both partially corresponds to, and partially diverges from, the finite aorist system.
. Productivity
We have seen in the previous sections a limited degree of productivity in the participial system that is not strictly regular (in the sense of productive non-finite inflection from pre-existing verbal stems), but that is still a fully participial phenomenon, insofar as participles are analogically created on the basis of other participles, and in functional terms are no less participles than those derived synchronically from an existing verbal stem. The perfect mediopassive served as the basis for new mediopassive aorist participles, such as vr. dha¯ná- and dyuta¯ná-, and subsequently forms like vr. dha¯náand dyuta¯ná-, could themselves serve as analogical bases for the creation of new mediopassive aorist participles to roots where neither the finite aorist nor the perfect provided any direct basis.
It has been claimed that the perfect mediopassive participle may have been somewhat productive in itself (e.g. Jasanoff 1978), since participles are often the most common or only attested form of a root’s perfect mediopassive in the Rigveda. In fact, the perfect mediopassive is itself productive as a verbal stem, and it is not necessary to suppose that this began in the participle, as may have been the case, at least partially, in Ancient Greek (Tucker 1981: 18). The attestation of mediopassive perfect participles in the absence of finite stems may be more a fact about the use of these stems than anything specifically paradigmatic, and it is not clear that there is any participial productivity here in distinction from the productivity of the verbal stem itself. In accounting for the existence of perfect middle participles in the absence of corresponding finite forms in the Rigveda, Kümmel (2000a: 91–2, 201) merely refers to the use of the medial participle suffix in place of the active, (on which see §2.3.3).

---

<!-- page 264 -->

6.4 Nonce-formations 
A√crostatic present participles from reduplicated perfects like d¯´ıdhyat- ‘thinking’ to dh¯ı ‘think’ are somewhat productive in the Rigveda, insofar as it is often the participle that is attested first (e.g. in the earlier books), while finite present forms to the same stems appear only later (late or post-Rigvedic). This could be attributed to the preference for a non-ablauting suffix (i.e. -at- here replacing ablauting -vám. s-), but it is only found with stative-perfect stems, and can therefore be explained more specifically on semantic grounds.35 The stative-perfects were morphologically perfect but increasingly equivalent to presents in semantic terms owing to the loss of their original anterior aspect, and ultimately they were fully reanalysed as presents because their inherited stative-present sense could no longer be reconciled with their perfect morphology. The fact that this happened in the participle first can be explained with reference to changes in the tense-aspect properties of the participial categories concerned, as discussed in more detail in §5.5.6.
Categories such as desiderative denominative participles, and present participles to stems in -áya- with zero-grade root, which originated as productive adjectival formations (cf. §2.3.2), do not fall under the present heading since they show no specifically participial productivity.
. Nonce-formations
A different kind of productivity is the creation of nonce-formations (Augenblicksbildungen), that is, one-off creations that fill a particular role in the context in which they are created and used, but that have no further existence beyond that single use and are not, therefore, part of the lexicon or grammar of the user.36 In the case of hapax legomena participles that have no attested corresponding verbal stem, it can be impossible to know for certain whether a particular form was a spontaneous creation or a genuine part of the language. Granted this necessary uncertainty, the default position must be to assume that a particular word is not a nonce-formation, unless there is good evidence in favour of such an interpretation: for example that such a verbal stem could not have existed given our knowledge of the Rigvedic verbal system, or evidence that the form has a non-verbal (e.g. noun or adjective) basis, or was primarily influenced by the context in which it appears.
We must distinguish two distinct kinds of nonce-formation in this context: first, those that are not participial, whose basis of creation and intended function is not participial but nominal or adjectival, even though the form itself appears morphologically
35 Cf. also Jasanoff (: ), and note the YAv. form daiδiian. t-, which corresponds almost precisely to d¯´ıdhyat-. This development differs from the superficially similar wholesale replacement of ∗-uos- by -ntas the perfect active participial suffix in Aeolic Greek (Blümel : ), which can be analysed as merely a paradigmatic simplification.
36 In Saussure’s terms, we might say that these forms are part of parole, but not langue. Nonce-formations in Vedic have been treated by Knobl (; a; b).

---

<!-- page 265 -->

 The category of participles

as if it were a participle; and secondly, participial nonce-formations: those that, while they are not derived by any kind of regular process from a verbal stem, were intended to be understood as participles in the context in which they were created and used, and hence can show participial properties such as verbal government and participial semantics. The former category can be excluded from the synchronic category of participles, but the latter, despite their morphological irregularity, can be considered genuine members of the category of participles, at least in the moment of their use, insofar as their basis and use is participial.

.. Non-participial nonce-formations

A clear example of a non-participial nonce-formation is the hapax legomenon g.pl.

Another non-participial nonce-formation is nr. táma¯na- ‘active/heroic(?)’, used of I√ndra at RV 5.33.6b ((6.29)). It is usually analysed as a present or an aorist participle to

The apparent negated participle ádvayant- ‘without duplicity’ at RV 3.29.5a ((6.30)) is clearly not a genuine verbal form, but is formed after and used as though one of a group of adjectival derivatives from dvá- ‘two’: dvayú- ‘divided, duplicitous’, ádvayas‘undivided, without duplicity’ and ádvayu- ‘id.’ Similarly, the negative ‘aorist’ participle ásridha¯na- ‘not failing’ at RV 7.69.7b ((6.31)) is connected√to the nominal forms srídh‘failing’ and ásridh- ‘not failing’ rather than the aorist of sridh ‘fail’, to which only a hapax thematic active form is found, and no mediopassive root forms.38

The form cikitvána¯ ‘perceptive’ at RV 8.60.18b ((6.32)) is more difficult to assess: it is explained as a ‘metrischer Augenblicksersatz für cikitús.a¯’ by Wackernagel–Debrunner (AiG, vol. 3: 299); but the form created is morphologically not participial. That is, it is non-participial in form, but possibly created on the basis of a participle. Functionally, it is used attributively, as an epithet, and so could equally well be treated as participial or adjectival in formal terms.

Should we therefore treat the ‘participle’ as the single survivor of an otherwise

unattested verbal root, or as a nonce-formation based on the adjectives avakraks.ínand vana-kraks.á-? Since the root is intransitive and the form itself an epithet, the syntactic and semantic facts provide no evidence either way. Although the only related

forms we have are nominal, we cannot rule out the possibility that kráks.ama¯n. arepresents a real participial formatio√n (whether nonce or not).
Similarly ambiguous is háyant- to hi ‘impel’ at RV 1.116.18b ((5.61)).46 This is listed

present with an irregular active ending (cf. §2.3.3). There is no convincing evidence

either way, so we must remain non-committal on the true status of háyant-.

6.4 Nonce-formations 

(6.37) dhis.a¯´ yádi dhis.an. yántah.

saran. ya¯´n/

fervour.i when seek_holy_place.prs.ptc.n.pl.m hasten.sbj

‘When they will hasten, with fervour seeking a holy place,/ to the mountain, to

sit in the hiding-place of Auśija.’ (RV 4.21.6ab)

As mentioned above, it is not always easy to decide whether a particular hapax

legomenon should be categorized as the only example of a genuine, but analogically

created, category, or simply as a nonce-formation. A form treated as analogical should

at least be consistent, morphologically and functionally, with the analogical pattern on

which it is based, whereas a nonce-formation is to be explained more by the context

in which appears than by any paradigmatic or other analogical pattern. These criteria

are not absolute, of course, and evidence is often ambiguous. For example, Lubotsky

t(o199√7ap:¯ı

879) considers the aorist participle píya¯na- ‘swelling’ at RV 1.79.3a ((6.26)) ‘swell’ to be nonce, presumably because it directly follows the phonetically

similar word páyasa¯ ‘with milk’.49 In contrast, I analysed it above as an analogical

formation. Despite the apparent polyptoton of the verse in which it appears, píya¯na-

functions like a participle, and correlates both formally and functionally with the

perfect mediopassive participle of the same root. Since a number of participles in

-a¯na- appear to be based analogically on perfect mediopassive participles, the same

explanation is naturally available in the cas√e of píya¯na-. The form ada¯ná-, however, cannot be explained by analogy. The root ad ‘eat’ is highly defective, so while the

verbal stem implied by píya¯na- is not impossible, though it is unattested, the verbal

stem implied by ada¯ná- is practically inconceivable. There is no perfect stem that

might have served as a basis for ada¯ná-, and semantically the analogy of stative

participles such as idha¯ná- and suva¯ná- is also unlikely, since ada¯ná- has a distinctly

processual sense. In the case of participles in -nt-, no systematic patterns of analogical

creation can be identified, so nonce status is often most likely. The form hárs.ant- at RV 1.127.6, for example, appears in the sequence hárs.ato hŕ. s.¯ıvato ‘(of him) excited, full of excitement’; this does not in itself mean that the form must be nonce, but the fact

that this root is otherwise exclusively mediopassive, and that there is no identifiable

pattern of analogy that might explain the existence of hárs.ant-, supports the analysis of this form as a nonce-formation (cf. Goto¯ 1987: 347).

As noted by Narten (1969: 81–2), a small group of hymns in book IV, possibly

the work of a single poet, contain a relatively high number of nonce participles

that share the same irregularity: all are mediopassive and (mostly) thematic, beside

regular athematic stems: us.a¯n. á- at 4.16.14c and us.áma¯n. a- at 4.22.2, both for vása¯na-

√ ‘desiring’ at RV 4.19.4 to vaś ‘desire’; sasr. ma¯n. árunning’.50 Goto¯ (2012: 27) notes in addition the

i√rregular forms vavr. dhánt- at 4.2.17c√(beside the secondary present stem va¯vr. dháti to vr. dh ‘grow’), and prastubha¯ná-, to stubh ‘sing praise’, at 4.3.12c (which he considers

analogical on stuva¯ná-) as isolated participles appearing in book IV.51 These are by

no means the only grammatically irregular words in book IV, as Goto¯ (2012) details,

and must be explained in this wider context. Whether we are dealing with dialectal

differences, a different degree of competence, or a different approach to grammatical

observance on the part of the poets who composed book IV, or indeed a combination

of such factors, is impossible to tell. As stated above, I treat such forms as ‘nonce’ only

where there is clear contextual evidence for such an explanation.

.. Conclusion
There exist, then, a variety of rare or hapax words which may or may not be ‘nonce’ in the sense defined above. There are only a few where the combined evidence of morphology, semantics, and context provide evidence that the form concerned, although appearing to represent a participle, was in fact created on adjectival or nominal bases and hence should not be considered participial. There are considerably more where some non-participial influence should be recognized in the creation of the word, but where the form was still clearly intended to be understood as a participial formation. These forms, and all ambiguous forms for which evidence does not permit us to make a clear judgment, must be considered functionally within the category of participles, even if morphologically they were not derived from verbal tense-aspect stems, and even though on one level never were a genuine part of the grammar of any Rigvedic Sanskrit speaker.

. Gaps in the participial system
We now turn from the creation of new participles where no directly correspondent verbal stem existed, to the absence of participles where they would be expected on the basis of existing finite stems. In many cases, of course, the absence of a participle to an attested finite stem might well be chance, except in the aorist where the productive aorist stems only very rarely form participles. In at least one instance, however, a participle seems to be blocked by the existence of a functionally equivalent nonparticipial form; that is, a lexical noun or adjective functions as if a participle to a
50 On sasr. ma¯n. á-, see also Knobl (: –). 51 Goto¯ () also notes śucánt- at ..c as the only transitive example of this stem, but the form ncthfe.ee(dfo.nrmot )ibsaenntodatk§iesno.laats.eHtdr,aesnfiusnircttievhene,riyanunovdta¯enissáni-niroyacuncvuya¯rcns.aáas-teabt.et.t.era.;anba,oawltyhhsepicdahrathsiceaiptaClekaselsaanrteodaas√dsijygeuncte‘ikdveeteotphaa√wnyaauy’p‘;hahorotldiwceifpavlseetr;, bind’ by Lubotsky (a: ) and Kümmel (a: –).

6.6 Lexicalized participles 

particular tense-aspect stem, and this stem√therefore does not form a morphologically regular participle. The original perfect of jan ‘produce, beget’ was an agent-oriented active paradigm (the patient-oriented mediopassive being a secondary development, as argued by Kümmel 2000a: 185–9). While finite forms of the active perfect are well attested in the Rigveda (×35), there are no examples of the expected participle in any Vedic text.52 At the same time Kümmel notes the semantic and functional parallel between the finite active perfect and the nomen agentis janit´r. -/jánitr¯ı- ‘begetter’. It appears, then, that the perfect participle was blocked by the functionally parallel -tr. agent noun. This is notable, but at the same time its isolation, contrasting with the common coexistence of participles and -tr. - agent nouns in many other Rigvedic verbs, shows that, for the most part at least, the distinction between these forms was relatively well maintained.
In some cases we see rather functional equivalence: the co-existence of a participle and a non-participle in apparently equivalent function. For example, in some passages a -tá- adjective and a present participle appear to be semantically equivalent; this is the case with ja¯´yama¯na- ‘(being) born’ at 3.6.2a ((6.38)) and 3.6.5c beside ja¯tá- ‘born’ a√t RV 3.3.10c ((6.39)) and 4.7.9d, respectively present participle and -tá- adjective of
jan ‘produce, beget’. In this root, then, these two categories were not fully distinct in functional terms.

(6.38) a¯´ ródas¯ı

apr. n. a¯ ja¯´yama¯nah.

prv world.a.du filled.2sg be_born.prs.ptc.n.sg

‘You filled the two worlds when you were born.’ (RV 3.6.2a)

(6.39) ja¯tá a¯´pr. n. o bhúvana¯ni ródas¯ı born filled.2sg creatures.a world.a.du
‘When you were born you filled (all) creatures, the two worlds.’ (RV 3.3.10c)

Likewise in some roots the desiderative adjective in -sú- appears to function in place
of an unattested present participle to the desiderative stem√, e.g. ninitsú- ‘desiring to reproach’ beside the desiderative present stem nínitsa- to nid ‘reproach’, expected participle ∗nínitsant- never occurring. In other roots, howe√ver, both forms coexist, e.g. participle dípsant- ‘desiring to deceive’ and dipsú- ‘id.’ to dabh ‘deceive’.

. Lexicalized participles
In the preceding sections we considered forms that lie within the participial system in functional terms, despite morphological irregularities caused by their being analogically created rather than formed regularly from a verbal tense-aspect stem. Lexicalized participles often show the converse: they are often morphologically ‘regular’; that is,

52

A form prajajñivám. s- occurs, but this belongs to

√ jña¯ ‘know’.

---

<!-- page 273 -->

 The category of participles
they appear to be participles regularly formed to a tense-aspect stem, but functionally they are outside, or at least on the very periphery, of the participial system.
By lexicalization I mean the development whereby a word form that is synchronically part of a verbal paradigm loses its association with that paradigm and comes to be used independently of it and stored independently of it in the language user’s mental lexicon. That is, the word form is reanalysed as a separate word, a separate lexical item, from the verb of which it was originally a part. In the case of participles, the process of lexicalization has two possible outcomes: a participle may become a lexical adjective, or a lexical noun. The former may be the more expected, since participles are adjectival in their morphology and, indeed, largely in their functionality. However, the latter is considerably more common, at least in the Rigveda. Participles, and adjectives more generally, can be used as nouns (cf. §2.2), and so the lexicalization of participles, and indeed the reanalysis of non-participial adjectives, as nouns, is unremarkable. More than this, however, the regular use of a participle in substantival function provides additional motivation for reanalysis and lexicalization, insofar as substantival use is further from the prototypical participial functionality than ordinary adjectival use. The syntax of participial lexicalization has been discussed in more detail in §§4.2 and 4.8.4, but here I discuss the forms concerned in more descriptive detail.
.. Morphologically irregular formations
The most distinctly lexicalized participles are those that are both morphologically and functionally distinct from the participial system. In a few instances, lexicalized participles that were originally morphologically regular in their formation have become irregular due to phonological developments not shared by, or lost in, the corresp√onding verbal paradigm. For example, the inherited perfect participle da¯śvám. s- to da¯ś ‘grant, offer worship’ no longer synchronically matches the perfect stem, which has otherwise been remade to invariant dada¯ś- throughout; functionally da¯śvám. s- is a noun ‘giver, worshipper’, occurring overwhelmingly in the dative singular.53 The form preserves the original, but synchronically irregular, form of the perfect stem precisely because it has become separated from the verbal paradigm and lexicalized as a noun. The negated form áda¯śvam. s- ‘non-worshipper’ is of course equally non-participial in synchronic terms, as is clear from the existence of the comparative áda¯śu¯s..tara- at RV 8.81.7c (cf. §6.8.2).
On the other hand, the four occurrences of the morphologically regular perfect participle dada¯śvám. s- are functionally identical to da¯śvám. s- (Kümmel 2000a: 243–4), and all even appear in the dative singular, just like most occurrences of da¯śvám. s-; functionally, then, dada¯śvám. s- is not a synchronic participle but is a redetermination of the noun da¯śvám. s-, based on its apparent etymology as an irregularly unredupli-
53 On the morphology of da¯śvám. s- cf. §..., and fn.  to Ch. .

---

<!-- page 274 -->

6.6 Lexicalized participles 

cated perfect participle. The participial origin of the noun da¯śvám. s- would have been clear from its suffix, despite its synchronic separation from the verbal paradigm, and

this licensed its redetermination with the regular perfect reduplication.

The nouns r¯´ıs.ant- and rís.ant-, both ‘harmer’, have been analysed √by Jamison (1983a: 144, fn. 79a) as haplologized forms from the reduplicated aorist of ris. ‘harm’, which explains the unexpected long root vowel found in r¯´ıs.ant-; this must be analogically shortened in rís.ant-. Such an origin also explains the transitivity of the forms, which contrasts with the apparently correspondent but intransitive root and thematic aorist

stems (e.g. res.at and ris.at, both 3sg. sbj. ‘he will/may he be hurt’).54 Functionally the forms are nouns, and their irregular haplologization is clear evidence of their

dissociation from the verbal paradigm.

The neuter noun jágat- ‘world’ is a substantivized present participle to an unattested class 3 present ∗jága¯ti, which was replaced by the attested class 3 present jíga¯ti at a pre-

sReimgvaendtiiccaplelyriwodit.hTthheenroouotn’s√ugsea¯

in the Rigveda clearly shows that it was still ‘step√, go’, as it frequently occurs paired and

associated contrasted

with nomin√al derivatives of the root stha¯ ‘stand’. The neuter of the present participle

of the root car ‘move’, cárant-, is three times used in the same way RV (1.146.1, 3.54.8,

10.5.3); however, the difference between the two is clear: cárant- can function as a

genuine participle, occurring with preverbs and adjectivally qualifying nouns, whereas

jágatverbal

oroccout r√s ogna¯l,yjáagsaat-ssuhboswtasnntoivpe.aSrtoicdipeisapliftuensctitlilohnaavliitnygasnodmiseaalssosomcioartipohnolwoigtihcatlhlye

dissociated from the synchronic present stem. Further evidence against any p√articipial status for jágat- is that it never occurs paired with the present participle of stha¯, as

we might expect if it were even remotely understood as a present participle. Rather,

it occurs paired with either the lexical adjective stha¯´- ‘standing’ (e.g. RV 2.27.4a), the

agent noun stha¯t´r. - ‘standing, one that stands’ (e.g. RV 4.53.6b) or the neuter of the perfect participle tasthivám. s-, all formations with stative/non-dynamic meanings.55

.. Morphologically regular present participles
Besides the few forms like da¯śvám. s-, r¯´ıs.ant-, and jágat-, which betray their nonparticipial status both by their lack of correspondence with a verbal stem and by their nominal functionality, there are many more forms like dada¯śvám. s-: forms that are morphologically indistinguishable from participles, but that functionally are simply nouns or adjectives.

54 This formation of a participle from a reduplicated aorist would be unique, and would be almost unique even just as a participle formed to a productive aorist stem in Indo-Aryan. It may be better to suppose that the reduplicated participle originated in a reduplicated present stem which was reanalysed as an aorist after the lexicalization of the participle. On the origin of the reduplicated aorist as a reduplicated present, see Jamison (a: ); for an alternative view see Willi ().
55 See Narten () for a detailed discussion of jágat-.

---

<!-- page 275 -->

 The category of participles

The fo√rm árhant- ‘worthy’ appears morphologically to be the present participle to the root arh ‘deserve’, but it does not match functionally the finite verbal stem from which it appears to be derived. Finite forms of this root in the Rigveda are all bivalent, usually appearing with an accusative theme object ((6.40)), twice with an infinitival complement (RV 4.55.7d and 5.79.10b, cf. Keydana 2013: 309–10).56 In contrast árhantnever has a complement; it is used in a purely adjectival sense ‘worthy’ ((6.41)), and is never found in specifically participial functions (as defined in Chapter 5). At RV 7.18.22c ((6.42)) it is possible to infer the object of the finite verb as an object for árhant(so Jamison and Brereton 2014), but this is not required by the sense, and the regular intransitive sense of árhant- rather argues against it. This form is therefore a lexical adjective; the adjectival status of árhant- was recognized at a later stage of the language by Pa¯n. ini (see appendix, §A.2), and is evidenced by the large number of secondary derivatives formed to it in the Classical language.

(6.40) es.á índro arhati

p¯ıtím asya

‘This Indra deserves a drink of it.’ (RV 2.14.2d)

The form dvis.ánt- ‘enemy’ √has likewise been lexicalized, in this case as a noun, from the present participle of dvis. ‘hate’; this too was recognized later by Pa¯n. ini.57

56 A unique finite form with the preverb áti is intransitive, in the sense ‘be more worthy’, at ..a; no
participle corresponding to this form is found. 57 The contrast described by the grammatical tradition between the sense ‘enemy’ found with dvis.ánt-
and the sense ‘hating’ of the finite verb, which can be used in the context of a family relationship where
‘enemy’ would be inappropriate, is already seen in the Rigveda, with dvés..ti used of a mother-in-law ((.)), but dvis.ánt- never so used.

---

<!-- page 276 -->

6.6 Lexicalized participles 

All six forms of the participle in the Rigveda function as nouns (as in (6.43)). The case distribution is typical of substantivized participles: the nominative never occurs, while the accusative is found once, the dative once, and the genitive three times (including the genitive plural once); in contrast genuine participles are most common in the nominative and accusative, with the genitive (and in particular the genitive plural) being considerably rarer. In terms of argument structure, none of the Rigvedic attestations of dvis.ánt- can be interpreted as transitive; in contrast, four of the five finite forms of the corresponding stem have unambiguous objects ((6.44)), while the object for the fifth, though unexpressed, can easily be supplied ((6.45)).58

(6.43) ma¯´ radha¯ma

dvis.até soma ra¯jan

not become_subject.sbj.1pl enemy.d Soma.v king.v

‘May we not become subject to the enemy, king Soma.’ (RV 10.128.5d)

(6.44) yám u dvis.más tám u pra¯n. ó jaha¯tu who.a pcl hate.prs.1pl him pcl life-breath leave.imp.3sg
‘Let his life-breath leave the one whom we hate.’ (RV 3.53.21d)

(6.45) dvés.t.i

śvaśru¯´r

ápa ja¯ya¯´ run. addhi

hate.prs.3sg mother_in_law away wife pushes

‘My mother-in-law hates (me), my wife pushes me away.’ (RV 10.34.3a)

Both functional evidence and the evidence √of secondary derivativation makes clear the adjectival nature of mr..láyant-.59 The root mr. d. ‘forgive’ was originally transitive, taking an object such as a¯´gas- ‘sin’, but this object was frequently ellipsed and the root came to be perceived as intransitive; it usually occurs with a beneficiary in the dative
(Jamison 1983a: 102–3). In contrast with finite forms, no occurence of mr..láyant- has an accusative object or even a dative dependent; all are simple adjectives or even nouns
meaning ‘merciful (person), forgiving (person)’ (cf. (6.54)). This analysis is supported
by the existence of the superlative mr..layáttama- ‘most merciful’ (RV 1.94.14b, 1.114.9c, 5.73.9d; cf. §6.8.2).
Some synchronic participles are frequent in both participial and nominal uses (e.g. ya¯´nt- both ‘going’ and ‘a traveller’), but some very common forms are almost exclusively used as nouns. This is the case in particular with a set of forms referring to
specific roles or activities in the context of a sacrificial rite, namely gr. n. ánt- ‘praisesinger’, sunvánt- ‘presser (of Soma)’, pácant- ‘baker (of sacrificial cakes)’, stuvánt-
‘praiser’, and yájama¯na- ‘sacrificer’. Although formally identical to present participles

58 Cf. also Kellens the formal relation of

() on the lexicalized Avestan -nt- form, OAv. daibišiian. t, which to dvis.ánt- is somewhat difficult to establish.

YAv

˜tbišiian. t-

‘enemy’,

59 The accentuation of the book I forms of the participle is irregular, appearing to reflect mr..layánt- rather

than mr..láyant-. It is perhaps due to a later reanalysis of the stem on the basis of the derivative mr..layáttama-,

though Lubotsky (: ) considers it related to a more widespread uncertainty over the accent of

participles in -áya-.

---

<!-- page 277 -->

 The category of participles

of their respective roots, these forms are used with specialized meanings in the context of the ritual, beyond the basic meaning of the f√inite verb; for example, sunvánt- means ‘presser of Soma’ specifically, whereas the verb su means ‘press’ more generally. These forms must have originated as participles used with a specialized meaning in the ritual context, but in synchronic terms they are lexical adjectives or nouns.60
With some of these words, and with others, there is evidence that what appears to be a single form such as gr. n. ánt- may in fact be two distinct words, one a synchronic participle, the other a distinct lexical noun or adjective. Such an assumption appears to be reflected in, for example, Geldner’s (RV) treatment of pávama¯na-, which he translates sometimes as a PN ‘Pavama¯na’, sometimes as genuine participle.61
In the case of pávama¯na- there is little syntactic evidence for the existence of two distinct words, one participle and o√ne noun, but there is, for example, with sunvánt-. Finite present active forms of su ‘press’ regularly have an overt object argument, usually but not necessarily sómam ‘Soma’; the apparent present active participle sunvánt-, on the other hand, is used, as described above, essentially as a noun ‘presser (of Soma)’, with no object ((6.46)). The lack of object correlates with the regular nominal function of this form. Once, however, sunvánt- does appear with an object ((6.47)); this is also the only instance where the participle has a ‘participial’ function (Chapter 5).
(6.46) yáh. sunvántam ávati yáh. pácantam who presser.a.sg.m aids who baker.a.sg.m ‘He who aids the presser (of Soma), who (aids) the baker (of sacrificial cakes).’ (RV 2.12.14a)

60 Although most commonly used as nouns, and widely translated as such (e.g. gr. n. ánt- is glossed ‘Sänger’ by Delbrück (: §, p. ) and Geldner (RV), ‘chanteur’ by Renou (EVP)), all can also be

used adjectivally, and are almost never themselves modified by adjectives, suggesting that they are in fact

adjectives usually used as nouns, rather than lexical nouns. There are two examples of yájama¯na- ‘sacrificer’

modified by adjectives (..cd and ..b); this form is probably therefore a noun in at least some of its

occurrences in the Rigveda; in later Vedic it is unambiguously a noun. 61 In the case of this word, Geldner follows the tradition dating back to at least the th-c. commentator

Sa¯yan. a, who glossed pávama¯na- variously as a PN (sóma-), or as a participle (ks.árant- or pu¯yáma¯na-). The meaning of the present stem seen in pávama¯na-, the most common participle in the Rigveda, has been the

subject of some debate. Following e.g. Pischel (: ), Pischel and Geldner (: ), Bhawe (:

vol. : –), Burrow (), and partially Böhtlingk–Roth (PW) and Grassmann (: –), but contra

e.g. Geldner (RV), Renou (EVP), Goto¯ (: –), Mayrhofer (EWA, vol. : –), Witzel and Goto¯

(: ), and Jamison and Brereton (), I see no reason why most if not all occurrences of the verbal

stem páva- in the Rigveda cannot be translated as ‘flow, move, run’ vel sim., rather than the more commonly

assumed ‘purify oneself ’; indeed, in several passages this interpretation seems distinctly preferable. The

Indian tradition recognized a verb pávate expressing motion (gatikarma¯n. ah. in the Nighan. .tu .; pava gatau cited by Sa¯yan. a ad RV ..). The expression of movement is absolutely clear in contexts where the verb is used of the wind; even Goto¯ (: –), while refusing to countenance any sense of movement in

this verb, found it impossible not to use is no clear etymology for such a stem or

the verb wehen verb; Burrow’s

when translating () attempt to

TS ... and JB connect the verb

to.√.pa¯ .‘mThoevree’

(Burrow a) is clearly unacceptable. Nevertheless etymological considerations must be subordinate to

the evidence of the texts, which support the sense ‘flow, move’.

<!-- page 278 -->

6.6 Lexicalized participles 

(6.47) sómam ín ma¯ sunvánto

ya¯cata¯

vásu

Soma.a pcl me press.prs.ptc.act.n.pl.m implore.imp.2pl wealth.a

‘Press Soma and implore me for wealth.’ (RV 10.48.5c)

So while the vast majority of Rigvedic occurrences of sunvánt- do not correspond syntactically to the finite verbal stem from which they are derived and appear to represent not a synchronic participle but a lexicalized adjective, the form in (6.47) represents the syntactically and semantically regular participial form of the finite verbal stem. Given the existence of a finite verbal stem (present or perfect, at least), it would always have been possible for a participle to be created to that stem, even if there existed a lexical adjective or noun that was morphologically identical to it, due to an earlier lexicalization of the same participle. This creates a potential for ambiguity in the analysis of lexicalized participles, but in fact in most cases we do not find both lexicalized noun/adjective and synchronic participle side by side; the existence of the former perhaps acted to discourage the (re)creation of the latter.
There is likewise some evidence for stuvánt-, us√ually just a noun ‘praiser’ (cf. (6.50)), as a synchronic participle to the present stem of stu ‘praise’; it has an object and/or genuine participial sense and functionality at RV 6.29.4c ((6.48)), 7.33.5c ((6.49)), and 8.3.14a. Nevertheless the majority of occurrences are clearly lexicalized as a noun ‘singer’. Similarly the frequent gr. n. ánt- is in almost all occurrences clearly a noun or adjective ‘praise-singer, praise-singing (one)’. But there are a few examples of gr. n. ánt- governing an accusative (RV 1.9.9b, 6.68.8c, 8.3.13d), which implies a genuine participle.62 It is interesting to note that gr. n. ánt- occurs twenty-two times in book VI, for example but only four times in book VIII, which is twice as long; this may point to dialectal differences in the lexicalization of such forms (there is no such skew in the distribution of finite forms of this root).

(6.48) índram. nára stuvánto

brahmaka¯ra¯´/ uktha¯´

Indra.a men praise.prs.ptc.n.pl.m prayer-making hymns.a

‘. . . the men who make sacred prayers, praising Indra/ and chanting hymns, as

the ones most beloved of the gods.’ (RV 6.29.4cd)

√ The form drávant- formally corresponds to the finite present stem of the verb dru

‘run’, but in most of its occurrences it is functionally no more than an adjective,

meaning ‘swift, quick’ rather than the expected participial ‘running’ ((6.54)). Its

adjectival status is clear also from its use in the a.sg.nt. as an adverb, which is not found

with synchronic participles (cf. §6.9). In one or two instances it appears with a de-

pendent phrase and can be interpreted with genuine participial sense ((6.55), also RV

10.102.6c); even in these instances an adjectival interpretation is possible, but it may

be that a genuine participle and lexicalized adjective exist side by side in this case too.

63 The negated form ápr. n. ánt- functions as the negative to the adjective ‘generous’, not to the participle.
