.. Morphologically regular non-present participles

So far we have primarily seen lexicalized present participles, but of course lexical-

ization can also affect participles to the other tense-aspect stems. The most common

active aorist participle by a long way is vidhánt-, which is so unexpectedly common

for an active aorist parti√ciple precisely because it has been lexicalized, in the sense ‘worshipper’. The root vidh ‘worship’ and its present stem vindhá- are widely

regarded as having w√hich is thought to

been back-formed from derive from the aorist of

vtíh-e√adohra¯is‘tapstpeomrtiporne’s(eorrverdathinervPidIEhá∗nuti--,

dheh ‘id.’).64 The participle is almost entirely used as a noun ‘worshipper’, occurring

mostly in the dative and genitive singular ((6.56)). However at RV 2.4.2a ((6.57)) and

10.46.2a the forms have objects and participial functionality, suggesting the marginal

existence of a parallel participial stem beside the lexicalized noun.

(6.56) dádha¯ti rátnam. vidhaté

yávis. .thah.

give.sbj wealth.a worshipper.d.sg.m most_youthful

‘The most youthful one will give wealth to the worshipper’ (RV 4.12.3c)

(6.57) imám. vidhánto

apa¯´m. sadhásthe/ dvita¯´dadhur

this.a worship.aor.ptc.n.pl.m waters.g seat.l again=placed

bhr. gavo viks.v a¯`yóh. Bhr.gus.v clans.l A¯ yu.g

‘Worshipping this one in the seat of the waters/ they again placed him, O

Bhr.gus, in the clans of A¯ yu.’ (RV 2.4.2ab)

One clearly lexicalized stative participle distinct from the transitive stem ¯´ıśe ‘rules

is ¯´ıśa¯n√a-, synchronically a noun ‘ruler’ X’ to ¯ıś ‘rule’. Its status as a noun is

and also

64 On this see esp. Hoffmann (a) and García Ramón (). √In contrast, Insler (: √–) rejects Hoffmann’s explanation of this root, assuming an inherited root vidh. The origin of the root vidh does not affect the fact that vidhánt- is primarily not synchronically a participle but rather a noun in the Rigveda.

---

<!-- page 281 -->

 The category of participles

evident from the existence of the compound¯ıśa¯na-k´r. t- ‘playing the ruler’, which would not be possible with a genuine participle.

As lexicalized perfect participles (beside da¯śvám. s-, discussed above) Kümmel (2000a: 91) lists vidvám. s-, dadvám. s- (= later dadivám. s-), rarivám. s-, and anu¯ca¯ná-. Some of these are undoubtedly lexicalized but others are less clear, and in addition

there are several other apparent perfect participles that show signs of lexicalization.

As discussed by Kümmel (2000a: 238–42), the active perfect participle dadvám. s- is

found only without finite perfect active

an of

o√bjdeca¯t

in the Rigveda, contrasting with the regularly transitive ‘give’, and has been lexicalized as a noun ‘donor, giver’.

The nominal nature of this former perfect active participle is also evident from its case

distribution: one n.sg.m. beside two g.sg.m. and one g.pl.m. Post-Rigvedic examples of

dadivám. s- are found with objects and the expected participial meaning ‘having given X’, which suggests either that a synchronic participle dadvám. s- existed at the time of the Rigveda but is unattested, or that the participle was recreated in the post-Rigvedic

period alongside the lexicalized former participle.

The common vidvám. s-, apparent participle to the perfect véda ‘knows’, is functionally an adjective ‘knowing, wise’ in most of its occurrences; the derivative forms

dúrvidvam. s- ‘of evil knowledge’ (RV 7.94.12b), súvidvam. s- ‘of good knowledge’ (RV 8.24.23c) and vidús..tara- ‘very wise’ confirm the synchronic adjectival status of this stem.65 At the same time vidvám. s- is occasionally found with participial functionality, and sometimes governing an accusative (as in (6.58)) or genitive (e.g. RV 2.29.1d,

3.31.1b), which again suggests a less common but genuine participial stem alongside

the synchronic adjective.

(6.58) sá vidva¯´n

apagohám. kan¯´ına¯m/ a¯vír

he know.pf.ptc.n.sg.m hiding.a girls.g manifest

bhávann

úd atis..that para¯vr´. k

become.prs.ptc.n.sg.m up stood outcaste

‘He, knowing the girls’ hiding (of him), stood up, becoming manifest, the

outcaste.’ (RV 2.15.7a)

6.6 Lexicalized participles 

At RV 10.169.4a ((6.60)); however, rára¯n. a- is transitive and appears to be functioning as a genuine participle to the present stem, mirroring the imperative rir¯ıhi in the preceding verse; similarly also at RV 2.32.5d and 10.183.1c. Nevertheless in the majority of instances the mediopassive participle appears to be lexicalized, in contrast to the perfect active participle.

The word anu¯ca¯ná- ‘reciter’ is attested only once in the Rigveda, at 8.58.1c. At a later stage of the language this is uncontroversially lexicalized, and for this reason is one of the few perfect participles to survive long enough to be defined by Pa¯n. ini (As..t. 3.2.109). Its lexicalized sense is often assumed to hold for the Rigveda as well: the form is listed separately from the verb by Grassmann (1873: 61), Lubotsky (1997a: 68), and most recently RIVELEX (p. 222), which describes it not as a participle, but as an ‘-a¯naBildung . . . zur Wz. vac-’.67 The word is used in RV 8.58.1c as a restrictive adjective to the noun bra¯hman. á-, and does appear to have some kind of technical sense, su√ch as ‘one who has repeated (and learned sacred texts)’. But this is the only form of ánu- vac ‘recite’ in the Rigveda, so it cannot be said with certainty that it is the participle and not the stem as a whole that had this technical meaning. It may be, then, that a√nu¯ca¯náin the Rigveda represents a perfectly regular participial formation from ánu- vac; we do not have sufficient evidence to be certain.

66 Kümmel explains this on the assumption that the active perfect and the perfect middle participle re-
flect an older (intransitive) stage of the perfect, while the finite middle reflects a newer ‘Oppositionsbildung’
to the (transitive) present and aorist. But it could equally be a secondary development of the medial perfect participle.
67 Grassmann√() actually lists the form twice, once as a separate lexical item (p. ), once as a participle under vac (p. ).

---

<!-- page 283 -->

 The category of participles
√ Finite forms of the root san ‘win’ are usually transitive, but can sometimes be intransitive with an implied object (e.g. va¯´jam ‘prize’). The perfect participle sasavám. s-, on the other hand, only very rarely has an object, and mostly displays a plain adjectival sense ‘victorious’. This participle too, then, is likely a lexical adjective in synchronic terms (cf. also Kümmel 2000a: 545–6). According to Kümmel (2000a: 221–2) the perfect middle participle tu¯´tuja¯na-/ tu¯tuja¯ná- appears to functio√n as the perfect stem counterpart of the intransitive class 6 and 10 presents of the root tuj ‘thrust forward’, while the single active perfect form, an optative, is transitive, parallel to the nasal present. Most examples of the perfect participle are functionally adjectives, meaning ‘eager, keen, hurried’; even the two that have dependent datives (toké tánaye ‘for bodily offspring’, RV 7.67.6c, 7.84.5b=7.85.5b) are no less adjectival in semantic terms.√ The perfect participle śaśama¯ná- (to śam ‘toil’) is one of the set of technical ritual terms discussed in §6.6.2, the only one that is not a present participle in origin. It is mostly used as a noun ‘one who has toiled (in preparing the rite)’, occasionally a restrictive adjective. It is considerably more common than finite forms of the corresponding stem in the Rigveda (×26 vs. ×8), which is at least consistent with a lexical analysis, and its case distribution suggests the same (d. ×7, g. ×7, l. ×1, a. ×2 vs. n. ×9).
.. Conclusion
We have seen in this section a relatively small but not insignificant number of stems that, although morphologically and historically regular participial derivatives from verbal stems, synchronically are detached from their corresponding verbal stems and lexicalized as adjectives or nouns. This lexicalization generally involves semantic differentiation from the corresponding verbal stem, usually including detransitivization where necessary, and it results in the creation of lexical adjectives and nouns that can, for example, form secondary derivatives that are not licensed with genuine participles. We have also seen several words that are lexicalized in the majority of their occurrences, but at other times appear to be genuine participles; this reflects the parallel existence of lexicalized stems alongside morphologically identical synchronic participles. Several of these lexicalized participles survived at least to the end of the Vedic period, long enough to be defined by Pa¯n. ini as nominal rather than participial stems (cf. §A.2).
I have attempted in this section to discuss all the clearly lexicalized forms that are found in the Rigveda, but other less clear forms exist that cannot be discussed for reasons of space, and in many instances, o√f course, the evidence is rather ambiguous. For example vyánt-, present participle to v¯ı ‘pursue’, appears both with and without an object; the same is true of finite forms of the corresponding stem, but Keydana (2013: 279–85) argues that objects can be supplied in the context for all finite forms and not all of the participial forms, suggesting that the participial forms without inferrable

---

<!-- page 284 -->

6.7 Participles in compounding 
objects are really lexicalized adjectives. Keydana’s argument is not unreasonable, but it is hard to talk with certainty when the distinction is what one can or cannot infer on the basis of one’s interpretation, as opposed to the explicit presence or absence of elements in a clause.

. Participles in compounding
In the previous section I made reference to restrictions on compounding and derivation that distinguish synchronic participles from lexicalized forms. In the following sections I consider these restrictions in more detail, beginning with the formation of compounds involving participial stems.

.. Participles as second element

With the exception of compounds involving preverbs as a first element, where it is

not really the participle which is compounded but the verbal stem from which the

participle is formed, tense-aspect stem participles cannot serve as the second member

of any type of compound in the Rigveda.68 This is in contrast to lexical adjectives, even

‘verbal’ adjectives such as the -tá- adjective, which freely form compounds with almost

any nominal prefix or noun. This prohibition against tense-aspect stem participles as

the second member of a compound is used by Kulikov (2010) as a criterion by which

to classify participles as ‘core’ rather than ‘periphery’ verbal forms.69

There are two apparent exceptions to this rule, neither of which, however, can be

treated as genuine (RV 6.15.17c) as a

pcaormticpiopulensd. RoIfVE∗aLn˙EkXu-(pa.n4d9)yeáxnpt-la, ipnraesn˙eknu¯tyápnatr-ti‘cgiopilnegocfro√okied‘gloy’,’

68 The same is true of most old Indo-European languages, including Ancient Greek and Classical Latin,

and is probably an inherited restriction. There appear to be a very few examples in Avestan, some of which,

however, are falsely compounded in the manuscripts or are found in corrupt or barely comprehensible

passages. Of those listed by Duchesne-Guillemin (: –), the only likely genuine compounds

are ga¯θ ro¯.raiiant- ‘proclaiming (holy) songs/the Gathas’ (Yt.., v.l. ga¯θ o¯.raiiant-) and an. ku.pәsәmna‘ornamented with hooks’ (Yt..); the former is unlikely to be archaic due to its specifically Zoroastrian

sense, the latter also need not be archaic. The problematic aˇs.aoxšaiiant- (Y..) is better read as a sandhi of aˇs.a¯ uxšiian. t- ‘growing through truth’ (following Humbach ). A few such compounds are found in Germanic, but only one or two are reconstructable for Proto-Germanic, and any PIE origin is doubtful. The

best Germanic comparisons are Old English sweord-berende ‘sword-bearing’ with Old Norse sverð-berandi

‘id.’, and Gothic all-waldands ‘overlord (lit. all-ruling)’ with e.g. OHG ala-waltenti ‘id.’; see Carr (: –

) and Voyles (). 69 The participles of

√ bhu¯

‘become’,

√ kr.

‘make’,

rule,

but only insofar as these roots function as semi-auxiliaries in forming denominal or deadverbial verb

stems; participles of these roots in such constructions are therefore not compounded but participles to

semi-distinct compounded verbal stems (cf. Kulikov : –). With these semi-auxiliaries there is

a tendency for the lexical part of the compound to appear in tmesis with finite forms, but in compound

with participial forms, somewhat parallel to the typica√l respective positioning of preverbs. This is a regular pattern, found also with e.g. the compound stem śrád- dha¯ ‘place trust in’, and shows the participial forms

patterning not with finite verbal forms but with nominal derivatives like the -tá- adjective and infinitive.

<!-- page 285 -->

 The category of participles
implying an inherited compound ∗he/onku-h ie/ont-, glossed ‘walking along side paths’. This is very similar to the interpretation of r´. jyant- ‘straight (going)’ given by Hoffmann apud Joachim (1978: 62), namely a compound of yánt- and a form ∗r. ji-, (Caland) variant of the adjective r. jú- ‘straight’. Both forms occur in the archaic book VI, but it seems unlikely that such forms should preserve an inherited means of participial compounding when there is so little comparative evidence for the existence of such compounds in PIE or even PII. There are various alternative explanations. The first elements of these two words could have been directional adverbs which became fixed in position before the participle yánt- and ultimately fossilized into apparent ca√totbmehsptu¯oaanunnidndhs√e(raailttsehd(ofunus.geh6o9tf)h,e√ayltiwh‘goeour’eg(honretevhaeerrrleimeirso∗rn√pohhoreleoaigl)iaecsvaialdcseoenmmceip-foaouurxntidlhisai)sr..yOA, lrnikteahlSetekyrtn.m√aitgikvhr.et, possibility is that both forms are denominative participles to otherwise unattested nouns. There is a morphological problem for this explanation, in that we would expect ∗r. j¯ıy- for r. jiy-; nevertheless the denominative explanation cannot be disproven as long as the only ‘participle’ identified as the second element of these ‘compounds’ is yánt-, since this is formally indistinguishable from a sequence of denominative plus participial suffix. That is, it is difficult to accept these forms as examples of an otherwise entirely unattested means of compounding without at least one such ‘compound’ that cannot be interpreted as denominative, i.e. that has a different second element, one that could only be a fully independent participle. I therefore conclude that these do not reflect genuine compounds involving participles.
.. Participles as first element
In contrast, participles are relatively common as the first element of compounds, primarily governing compounds and bahuvr¯ıhis. Historically, the first element of governing compounds such as dha¯rayát-kavi- ‘supporting the wise’ was not a participle, but rather a stem ending in a vowel, as seen in e.g. the Rigvedic PN Trasá-dasyulit. ‘frightening the enemy’, Old Persian PN Da¯raya-vauš ‘Darius’ (lit. ‘supporting the good’), Greek pheré-oikos ‘snail’ (lit. ‘carrying its house’).70 The introduction of a dental into the stem must be dated to the Indo-Iranian period due to e.g. Avestan PN Da¯rayatraθa- ‘supporting chariots’.71 Synchronically, however, we must analyse e.g.
˜ dha¯rayát- in dha¯rayát-kavi- as a present participle. This is because the accent of the governing-compound participles, which reflects not a participial accent but the accent
70 Wackernagel–Debrunner (AiG, vol. :: –) provide a list of such compounds in various old Indo-European languages.
71 For this development, see Renou (: –). This -t- insertion is not only found in governing compounds with ‘participle-like’ first elements: śatád-vasu- ‘whose wealth is hundred(-fold)’ at ..c shows the same process operating in a bahuvr¯ıhi compound with a numeral as the first element (Mayrhofer, EWA: vol. : ); here the accent of śatá-, being parallel to that of the governing compound stems, may have supported the development.

---

<!-- page 286 -->

6.7 Participles in compounding 
of the original thematic stem, has been extended to bahuvr¯ıhi compounds where the first element always was a participle, e.g. spr. hayád-varn. a- ‘of eager colour’ (RV 2.10.5c) beside expected participial accentuation ∗spr. háyant-. This could only have occurred if the first element of governing compounds had been reanalysed as a participle. Synchronically, therefore, the regular accent of the stem form of an -nt- participle falls on the final syllable.
This reanalysis extended the capabilities of participles in compound. Prior to this participles would have been common only in bahuvr¯ıhis, and occasionally in determinative compounds; however, none of these compound types was conducive to the expression of an object for a transitive participle in the compound. It is even possible that transitive participles were excluded from compounding, or that if used in compound they were necessarily used intransitively. In governing compounds, however, transitive participles are obligatory, and the object is necessarily expressed as the second element of the compound.72 Once this development became regular, there was in principle no restriction on which types of participle (or participles to which types of root) could appear in nominal compounds.
The reanalysis also resulted in the creation of new participial stems where no finite verbal stem existed. For example, the compound bhandád-is..ti-, which means perhaps ‘delighting in the sacrifice’ (Goto¯, 1987: 223-4) or ‘fortunate in the quest’ (Jamison and Brere√ton 2014), implies an active participle ∗bhándant- to the thematic present stem of bhand ‘be happy, delight in’. But the present to this root is exclusively mediopassive (forming a regular participle bhándama¯na-). Likewise the first element of the bahuvr¯ıhi rapśád-u¯dhan- ‘with full udders’ at RV 2.34.5 appears to reflect a ∗rapśánt-, which is unexpected since the corresponding verbal stem is again medium tantum, and there is no evidence that the stem was formerly active, as is the case for example with dyutánt- (cf. §6.2.2).73 This may provide evidence for a spreading of the ending -át as an all-purpose compound form for participles. Such an ending would have originated in governing compounds such as vr. s.ad-añji- ‘raining down ointment’, where the secondary participle again corresponds to a medium tantum stem; in bahuvr¯ıhis like rapśád-u¯dhan- it can only be secondary.
Governing compounds are by far the most common type of compound involving participles in the Rigveda: there are thirty such stems attested. Functionally, such compounds are used as ordinary adjectives, often epithetically, or nouns, and insofar as the participle is the syntactic head of such a compound, the function of the participle itself within the compound is the same.
72 In sa¯dád-yoni- ‘taking his seat in the womb’ (..c) and the PN Jamád-agni- lit. ‘approaching the fire’ the second element is not, at least originally, an object but an accusative of goal, suggesting that ‘tra7n3siOtivnittyh’einortihgiisncoofnttheexdt isfhfiocuulldt rboeout n√drearpsśtocoodmtpoairnecMluadyerahnoyfekri(nEdWoAf a, cvcoul.sa:tivec)aasneddeKpüemndmeenlt.(a: –).

---

<!-- page 287 -->

 The category of participles

In bahuvr¯ıhis, on the other hand, participles are necessarily adnominal in function, modifying the second element of the compound. There are only twelve bahuvr¯ıhi compounds in the Rigveda that have participle-like stems as their first element, and of these half actually involve lexicalized participles or Caland adjectives rather than synchronically regular participles: dravád-aśva- ‘whose horse is swift’, dravác-cakra‘whose wheels are swift’, and dravát-pa¯n. i- ‘whose hands are swift’, all to the adjective drávant- (§6.6.2); bhra¯´jaj-janman- ‘of sparkling origin’ and bhra¯´jad-r. s..ti- ‘whose spears sparkle’, to the probable Caland adjective bhra¯´jant- (§6.10); śucád-ratha- ‘whose chariot is bright’ to śucánt- (§6.10). The forms dadr. śa¯ná-pavi- ‘with visible wheelrim’, dyutád-ya¯man- ‘whose way is shining’, yuyuja¯ná-sapti- ‘whose horses are yoked’, rapśád-u¯dhan- ‘with full udders’, stanáyad-ama- ‘of thundering force’, spr. hayád-varn. a‘of eager colour’, and svanád-ratha- ‘whose chariot resounds’ are the only certain examples of bahuvr¯ıhis involving non-lexicalized participles in the Rigveda.
Other compound types are rare with synchronic participles, slightly less so with lexicalized stems. There are seven determinative compounds: sát-pati- ‘lord of (all that) exists’,74 krandád-is..ti- ‘desiring the roaring one (Soma)’ (RV 10.100.2b),75 ja¯grat-svapná- (on which see §6.9), ¯ıśa¯na-k´r. t- ‘playing the ruler’ (cf. §6.6.3), edhama¯na-dvís. - ‘hating those who prosper’, possibly naks.ad-da¯bhá- ‘deceiving those who approach(?)’,76 and ya¯vayatsakhá- ‘a protecting friend’.
In all compound functions, then, participles are semantically adjectival or nominal, while from a syntactic point of view it is only in governing compounds, where the participles have objects (and where the participial stem is secondary), that there is any sign of the verbal nature of participles. Non-lexicalized participles are rare in

74 The meaning and derivation of sát-pati- is controversial. Wackernagel–Debrunner (AiG: vol. :: ) analysed the first element as the g.sg. of a root noun ∗sád- ‘dwelling’, i.e. ∗sáts-pati- ‘Gildemeister’, supposedly parallel to PIE ∗déms-poti- ‘lord of the house’ underlying Vedic dám. -pati- ‘master’ and Greek despóte¯s ‘id.’,
and supported by RV sádas-pati- ‘lord of the seat’ and sádasas pati- ‘id.’. This interpretation was followed

by Thieme (: ) (‘Hausherr’) and Mayrhofer (EWA: vol. : ). This is, howeve√r, problematic on semantic grounds, since sát-pati- is primarily an epithet of Indra, while forms related to sad are generally

connected to Soma and Agni. Geldner (RV) interpreted the word as a determinative (karmadha¯raya)

compound (‘wahrer Herr’, ‘wirklicher Gebieter’, ‘rechtmäßiger Gebieter’, etc.), taking the first element sánt-

as a lexicalized participle meaning ‘true, right’. Renou’s (EVP) translations varied: he followed Geldner in

EVP: vol. : , comparing sát-pati- to satyásya pátayah. at ..; he followed Wackernagel in EVP: vol.
: , translating the form “maître de maison” at .., but in EVP: vol. :  he translated the form at .. as ‘maître de l’étant’. I follow this last interpretation. Similar compounds of the PIE word ∗póti- ‘master’

are relatively common both in the Rigveda and in other old Indo-European languages, e.g. rayi-páti- ‘lord of

wealth’, vásu-pati- ‘id.’, viś-páti- ‘lord of the clans’, Goth. bruþ-faþs ‘bridegroom’, hunda-faþs ‘centurion’ (on

the Gothic forms, see Benveniste ), Greek despóte¯s. All these compounds are dependent determinative

(tatpurus.a) compounds in which the first element is a noun in a case relation (here genitive) to the second. The same is therefore likely for sát-pati-, and since Wackernagel’s ∗sáts-pati is semantically unlikely, we must

interpret the first element as the participle sánt- ‘being’ used as a noun. 75 Following Geldner (RV: ad loc.) and Renou (EVP: vol. : ), but contra Burrow (: ), who

interpreted it as a governing compound ‘conquering riches’, and Jamison and Brereton (), who translate

6.8 Participial derivatives 

compounds other than governing compounds, suggesting that synchronic participles were disfavoured in such compounds, unlike lexical adjectives which occur freely. Compounding is therefore a rough guide to the synchronic status of a morphologically ambiguous form, as noted in the previous sections.
... Accent As stated above, the regular accent on a participle in compound is on the final syllable of the stem; this is found in almost all bahuvr¯ıhi and governing compounds involving participles in Sanskrit. Two irregular forms, bhra¯´jaj-janman‘of sparkling origin’ and bhra¯´jad-r. s..ti- ‘whose spears sparkle’, both to the stem bhra¯´jant- ‘sparkling’, will be discussed below (§6.10) as providing evidence for the non-participial status of this form, paralleling the non-participial rúśant- ‘bright’ in rúśat-paśu- ‘whose beasts are bright(-coloured)’. However, the form stanáyad-ama‘of thundering force’ (RV 5.54.3d) has as its first element an unambiguous participle. This compound may preserve the original accent of such bahuvr¯ıhis, which otherwise shifted after the analogy of the reanalysed governing compounds.
A different problem is presented by sa¯´dhad-is..ti- ‘making successful the sacrifice’, which occurs at RV 3.2.5d and 3.3.6c. This is a governing compound, meaning that its accent cannot even be an archaism. It can only be explained as reflecting an anomalous alternative strategy for homogenizing the accents of participles in governing and bahuvr¯ıhi compounds. The accentuation of sa¯´dhad-is..ti- and stanáyad-ama-, then, suggest that there may have been some variation in the accentuation of participles in compound at an early stage.

. Participial derivatives

.. Unproductive derivatives

Like constraints on compounding, constraints on derivation from participial stems

often provides clear evidence for the synchronic status of an ambiguous form.

Comparative evidence suggests that synchronic participial stems may have licensed

secondary derivatives in PIE. One possible derivational pattern is noted by Melchert

(1999: 23, in ∗-nt-i-,

fn. 32): from a participle in ∗-nt- there could be derived an e.g. Skt. rám-at-i- ‘rest’ to the participle rámant- ‘stopping’

ab√stract ( ram

noun ‘stop’;

∗-nt-i- formations in other languages include Hittite tukkanzi- ‘cultivation’, and RCS

mogutь ‘lord, master’); from this could be derived an adjective in ∗-nt-i-o-, e.g. Greek

epioús-i-os ‘for the present’ to the participle epi-ónt- of the verb épeimi ‘I am at hand’; finally a feminine abstract noun in ∗-nt-i-eh- could be derived, as seen in Latin

absent-i-a ‘absence’ to abs¯ens, participle of absum ‘I am absent’, and Latin scient-i-a

‘knowledge’ to sci¯ens ‘knowing, wise’, participle of scio¯ ‘I know’.77 There is also evidence for a ∗-io- derivative to a perfect participle in Greek, in the Hesychian gloss idu˜ıoi:

In the Rigveda, productive derivational processes are not found with synchronic

participles but only with lexicalized stems, while the only attested derivatives from

synchronic participle stems are unproductive relics of what were presumably produc-

tive derivational patterns at an earlier period. Synchronically, therefore, derivatives

were not productively formed to non-lexicalized participles in the Rigveda.

One sáścat-

from case

of ablauting participial stems, loss of ablaut. This type is of uncertain frequency in

the Rigveda because it is unclear whether all potentially relevant forms should be

analysed in this way.79 The reduplicated stem of saścát- ‘pursuer’ and its negative

asaścát- ‘without equal’ means they are unambiguous participial derivatives; sam. yát‘continguous, union’ is best derived from the participle yánt- ‘going’ due to the preverb

sám ‘together’; other possible examples, however, such as va¯ghát- ‘sacrificer’, vahát-

‘river’, sravát- ‘id.’, vehát- ‘(type of) cow’,80 and acodát-81, could equally continue the PIE suffix ∗-ét-/-ot- seen in e.g. Gothic mitaþs -adis ‘measure’, Latin teges -etis ‘cover’,

seges -etis ‘cornfield’.82

vufru (suffix ∗-ro-), which could suggest a possible link between this -át- suffix (whatever its origin) and the
Caland system.

was probably lexicalized already in PIE. There may be a few other thematic derivatives

in the Rigveda from originally participial stems, such as pa¯´nta- ‘drink’ (on which cf. fn.

27 to this chapter), but all are difficult and a synchronic productivity of the formation

is unlikely.85

.. Productive derivatives (lexicalized stems)
Besides these unproductive, inherited derivative patterns, there are some derivational suffixes that are productive, but found only with lexicalized participle stems; these can therefore be used as evidence for the synchronic status of particular forms. The only commonly found derivatives are comparatives and superlatives in -tara- and -tamarespectively. The only such derivatives in the Rigveda are mr..layáttama- ‘most merciful’ (§6.6.2), m¯ı.lhús..tama- ‘most generous’ (§2.3.4.4), and vidús..tara- ‘wisest’ (§6.6.3), as well as the further derived negatives ávidus..tara- ‘most unwise’ and áda¯śu¯s..tara- ‘most
83 On this formation, see Hoffmann (: ). 84 It has been assumed on the basis of these forms that this verb was acrostatic; moreover, Kloekhorst (: ) argues that this supports an original amphidynamic paradigm of the participle, but he fails to take into account the fact that almost all the full-grade root forms are thematized secondary derivatives and hence separate from the participle, while Skt. va¯´nt- is perfectly regular within Sanskrit for roots of this shape, and cannot necessarily be considered archaic. The original form of the participle is seen in Hittite huuant-, showing zero-grade stem and full-grade suffix; the Greek participle áeis, to áe¯si ‘blows’, may be a perfect match. On this word see also Nikolaev (: –). 85 Cf. Wackernagel–Debrunner (AiG: vol. :, §§ –) for a collection of forms.

 The category of participles

unworshipful’ (RV 8.81.7c, with unexplained irregular u¯).86 In these forms we see two distinct methods of derivation. Most commonly the participle retains its original accent, but mr..layáttama- shows the ‘stem’ accent typical of compounds. This is also the only present participle to occur in the expected stem form, sáhantamaand vra¯´dhantama- preserving the full-grade -nt- (or thematic verb) stem, which is technically irregular.
The abstract noun rara¯n. áta¯- ‘generosity’ at RV 1.171.1c to rara¯n. á- (cf. §6.6.3) is the only participial derivative of this type in the Rigveda, but shows a synchronically productive suffix attached to a lexicalized participle (cf. Oldenberg, Noten: vol. 1, p. 171).
These are the only derivatives of participles from productive derivational processes in the Rigveda, and all are to lexicalized stems.87 This provides a clear morphological distinction between participles proper and lexicalized participles, which in synchronic terms are categorially distinct. The fact that productive secondary derivatives can be formed only from lexicalized participles demonstrates that secondary derivation was not synchronically possible for participles proper, meaning that in this respect the participles pattern with finite verbal forms rather than the nominal system.

.. Negative derivatives

One particularly interesting derivational process is the formation of negated partici-

ples, which I have discussed in more detail in Lowe (2011a). In the Rigveda the regular

means of negating the predication made by a participle is by forming a negated stem

from the positive participle stem, by prefixation of the negative marker á(n)-; the

independent negative particles ná and ma¯´ are never used to negate participial clauses,

and in fact, besides a very few exceptions, can only be used to negate finite clauses

(bDy etlhberüpcakrt1i8c8ip8l:e54k0r¯´ı–.la4n3)t.-S‘pol,afyoirnegx’a(m√pklre¯ı,d.th‘peloany’l)yiws atyo

to negate the concept expressed form a negative á-kr¯ı.lant- ‘not

playing’.

Negated participles are by far the most common participial derivative in the

Rigveda: 72 negated participial stems are found, significantly more than the 5 or 6

comparative and superlative derivatives attested. As one might expect the majority,

61, of these are negated present participles, 8 are negated perfect participles, and 3

negated aorist participles.88

86 Also possibly vra¯´dhantama- ‘most boastful’, if this is a lexicalized participle rather than a Caland

adjective: cf. §..

from bharád-va¯ja- lit. ‘carrying (off) the prize’, but these are to be explained within the realm of compound

derivation and do not concern us. 88 These numbers include even clearly non-verbal nonce-formations such as ádvayant- ‘undivided’,

discussed in §... The isolated ásinvant- ‘unsated, insatiable’ (beside assumption that it reflects an unattested present ∗sinóti ‘is sated(?)’ to a

6.8 Participial derivatives 

The synchronic status of negated participles is somewhat ambiguous. As argued in Lowe (2011a), the majority of negated participles are synchronically non-participial, i.e. lexically distinct, adjectival derivatives. This is clear from the fact that they do not correspond to their positive counterparts in terms of either argument structure or semantic range. So, negated participles are almost always intransitive, even when the corresponding positive participle and the finite verbal stem is transitive. The negated participle ánapavyayant- at RV 6.75.7d ((6.61)), for example, has no obj√ect, nor can an object easily be supplied in the context; in contrast finite forms √of vye ‘cover’ are always transitive, including the single finite occurrence of ápa vye ‘uncover’ ((6.62)).

Similarly, the hapax legomenon√áyatant- at RV 2.24.5c ((6.63)) is unambiguously intransitive, while active forms of yat ‘array, line up (e.g. a battle-line)’ are consistently transitive. The line in which áyatant- appears refers to two divine or heavenly entities that move according to a fixed, though separate, order;89 the negated participle therefore has something of a reflexive sense, meaning roughly ‘not lining (themselves) up next to one another (but rather maintaining a fixed distance)’.90 The single occurrence of the positive present participle yátant- at RV 5.48.5b (6.64) is in contrast nonreflexive and transitive ‘lining up X’; likewise finite forms such as the 3sg. present yatati at RV 7.36.2d (6.65).

The same is true of the negated perfect participle to this verb, ájaghanvam. s- ((6.70)): it could perhaps be interpreted as sharing its object with the ablative adjunct to the matrix verb (‘us’), but it is unlikely to be coincidental that it has no explicit object while 17 of the 18 occurrences of the positive jaghanvám. s- do (as in (5.82); the exception is at RV 1.32.14b).

In terms of their semantic range too, negated participles are usually restricted to the functionality typical of lexical adjectives, rather than of participles proper (as discussed in Chapter 5). Further evidence, not noted in Lowe (2011a), that negated participles are derived lexical adjectives comes from a number of forms that exist despite the synchronic absence of corresponding finite√and positive participial stems. So áhim. syama¯na- ‘not being harmed, unharmed’ to him. s ‘harm’ implies a finite passive stem in -ya- to this root which does not, however, exist in Vedic. Presumably such a stem did exist at an earlier stage, licensing a participle and the creation of a derived negative adjective from that, but the finite stem then dropped out of use in the Vedic language, leaving only the lexical adjective as evidence of its existence. Sim√ilarly áchidyama¯na- ‘unbreaking, unbreakable’ implies an anticausative -ya- stem to chid ‘break, cut’, which is not otherwise part of the Rigvedic language (though such a stem appears in later Vedic).91
At the same time there are one or two instances of negated participles that are transitive or that do show participial functionality. The most likely examples of negated participles in ‘participial’ functions are ákr¯ı.lant- ‘not playing’ at RV 10.79.6c ((6.71)), and áris.an. yant- at RV 2.37.3b ((6.72)). The only clear example in the Rigveda√of a negated participle governing an accusative object is áminant- ‘not infringing’ ( m¯ı ‘diminish, change, infringe’) at RV 1.92.12c ((6.73); pa¯da c is repeated at RV 1.124.2a). Even so, the three other occurrences of áminant- (at RV 4.5.6b, 4.56.2b, 10.88.13c) do not have expressed objects, though objects might possibly be inferable.92

91 These are not the only negated participles in -ya-ma¯na- that lack corresponding positive partici-
ples. The forms áks.¯ıyama¯n. a- ‘undiminishing√’ and átapyama¯na- ‘unsuffe√ring’ do correspond to finite passive/anticausative -ya- stems of the verbs ks.¯ı ‘diminish, perish’ and tap ‘be hot’, but these stems do not form positive participles in the Rigveda. The same is true of the Avestan cognate of áks.¯ıyama¯n. a-, ajiiamna- ‘id.’; the positive participle was an inherited form, however, as it survives, lexicalized as a noun,
in OP jiyamna- ‘end’. 92 At ..b Geldner supplies ‘die Satzungen’ without comment; at ..b ru¯pám (‘ihr Aussehen’), which
occurs as an object of finite forms, but he notes daívya¯ni vrata¯´ni as possible; at ..c he suggests
inferring vratám, díśah. , or ru¯pám, or ‘im letzten Falle: unveränderlich’.

It appears, then, that the majority of negated participles in the Rigveda are synchron-

ically non-participial, i.e. adjectival, derivatives from participial stems, but that a few

appear to be synchronically participles with negative polarity. In Lowe (2011a) I argue

that the former type is inherited from PIE, whereas the latter is an innovation at a

more recent period preceding the composition of the Rigveda.93 The contrast, and the

relative antiquity of the two participle corresponding to

types, is most clearly seen the positive sánt- ‘being’

i(n√thaes

two forms ‘be’). The

of the negated form a¯´sant- is

unambiguously laryngeal in the

older and may be of irregular initial long

PIE date, preserving vowel (< ∗n´ -hs-ont-,

etovi∗d√enhceeso‘fbteh’)e;

root-initial it is not the

negative of the positive participle in semantic terms, but a lexically distinct adjective

meaning ‘false’, corresponding therefore to the positive derivative adjective from the

participle, satyá- ‘true’; both satyá- and a¯´sant- show a parallel semantic development.94

93 That the negative prefix derived lexical adjectives from participles in PIE is supported by comparative
evidence: in Latin the negative prefix similarly derives negative adjectives, while real participial negation is achieved by using the independent negative non, as in e.g. the adjective impatie¯ns ‘impatient’ beside no¯n patie¯ns ‘not enduring (obj.)’. In Ancient Greek likewise real participial negation is achieved by means of the negative particles me¯´ or ou(k/kh); synchronically at least, participles cannot regularly be negated
by combination with the negative prefix a(n)-. In PIE, then, a participle compounded with the negative
prefix derived a non-participial adjective, which could not share the syntactic and semantic possibilities of
the simple participle. Whether a participle could be directly negated, perhaps by means of a periphrastic
collocation involving an independent word indicating negation, as in Classical Latin and Greek, cannot be
certain. The Greek and Latin means of negating participles may perhaps be inherited, but if so, this PIE
process must have been lost in PII times. 94 The meaning of satyá- is at least of PII date, since Avestan haiθiia- can also mean ‘true’, and is possibly
of PIE date, if Goth. sunja ‘true’ is cognate (cf. Lehmann : ). On this and other words for ‘truth’ in Indo-European languages see also Frisk (: esp. –, , ). The replacement of a¯´sant- by ánr. ta- as the regular Sanskrit antonym of satyá- was almost complete by the time of the Rigveda: at an earlier stage the

---

<!-- page 296 -->

6.9 Adverbs 
In contrast the other form of the negated participle corresponding to sánt-, ásant-, is synchronically regular in both morphological and functional terms, meaning ‘not being’, and can be analysed as synchronically a participle, but with negative polarity.
We are, then, in a similar position as with some of the lexicalized participles discussed above: ‘negated participles’ are formally ambiguous between derived lexical adjectives and synchronic participles with negative polarity. That is, we see here two distinct morphological processes, one derivational, the other inflectional, both of which were likely productive in the language of the Rigveda. In the former case, the negative prefix á(n)- derives lexical adjectives from participial stems, while in the latter, the same prefix is used to produce a semantically negated form of the participle, a form that is itself still a participle, that is still part of the non-finite verbal system. The former process appears to be the more common but the latter also appears to be synchronically regular.

. Adverbs

A further property that contributes to distinguishing synchronic participles from

lexicalized and non-participial forms is the use of the nominative/accusative singular

neuter of adjectives as an adverb. It is widely assumed that, as with any other adjective,

the n./a.sg.nt. of participles could be used adverbially in the Rigveda, but a detailed

consideration of the forms involved shows that, while there appear to be no restrictions

on lexical adjectives being used as adverbs, no synchronic participle is ever used in

this way.

An early list of supposed√participles used as adverbs is given by W√ackernagel (1918:

394–5): ja¯grat- ‘wak√efully’ ( gr. ‘awake’), tr. pát ‘full, to s√atisfaction’ ( tr. p ‘be satisfied’),

d√ravát ‘swiftly’ to ( dhr. s. ‘be bold’),

dru ‘run’, drahyát patayát ‘in flight’

(‘se√cuparetly‘f(l?y)’)’ ,(?pradvrá. ht

‘be firm’), dhr. s.át ‘boldly’ ‘headlong’, ¯ıs. át ‘a little’.95

Besides these forms, Wackernagel–Debrunner (AiG: vol. 2:2: 164) considered tárat at

RV 9.58.1c–4c and bhárat at 9.52.1b to be adverbial a.sg.nt. participles, but these are

now generally accepted to be injunctives. Pinault (1989: 86–7) accepted all these and

added Atharvavedic sta¯yát ‘in secret’.96 That most of these forms are used as adverbs is

semantic difference between a¯´sant- and ánr. ta- can be assumed to have been something like ‘not true’ versus ‘not right’ respectively; the tendency of words related to the latter form to become used in the sphere of truth
is clearly paralleled by Avestan aˇs.a- ‘truth’ (on which see Hintze : –). Late RV asatyá- ‘false’, as also YAv. aŋhaiθiia- ‘id.’, are clearly secondary formations. On the sometimes (incorrectly) related santya-, see Hoffmann ().
95 Repeated without criticism by Nishimura (: ), who assumes that they ‘have originated
obviously from the present participle’. 96 Pinault (: –) derives AV sta¯yát from ∗sta¯yánt- ‘d’un prés. ancien ∗(s)ta¯yáti, cf. aussi ta¯yú-
et sta¯yú- . . .’; the existence of a finite verbal stem, and even of an -nt- adjective/participle, is therefore hypothetical. Alternatively, sta¯yát could be a.sg.nt. of an -nt- adjective related to the Caland system (cf. sta¯yú-).

 The category of participles

‘We males would kindle you,/ the male, O male,/ O Agni, you who shine on

What is questionable is whether any of these adverbs can be treated as case forms

of participles in synchronic (or even diachronic) terms. The adverb tr. pát has no corresponding participle or finite verbal stem in the Rigveda, and might better be

treated as nt.sg. of an -nt- Caland adjective connected with other Caland forms of

the PIE root, such as Gr. terpnós ‘delightful’, or terpikéraunos ‘delighting in thunder’.97

dhr. s.át ‘boldly’ is the n./a.sg.nt. of the Caland adjective dhr. s.ánt- (see §6.10). d√ravát ‘swiftly’ is the n./a.sg.nt. of drávant-, which corresponds to the present stem of dru

‘run’ but is in fact largely lexicalized as an adjective meaning ‘swift, quick’ (§6.6.2), so

synchronically the adverb is derived from an adjective, not a participle. drahy√át does not even have a clearly corresponding verbal root (the regular full-grade of dr. h is darh-, so the connection is uncertain). The adverb ¯ıs.át ‘a little’ is first attested postRigveda, but in any case cannot be derived from the verbal stem seen in ¯´ıs.ate, as Wackernagel supposed.98 The form pravát ‘headlong’ is derived from the preverb prá,

p∗√arparlluelatsoofroirgminsallilkyearpgaurae¯dvábty-

‘distance’ (in (6.10)), Pischel and Geldner

udvát- ‘height’ etc., not from a root (1892: 63–76); cf. Mayrhofer (EWA:

vol. 2: 186).

Wackernagel (1918: 394–5, fn. 2) argued that the first element of the compound

ja¯grat-svapná- ‘dream of a waking person’ at RV 10.164.5c must be adverbial, meaning

‘waking dream’. But this ‘adverb’ does not otherwise occur, and the first element is

better taken as a participle used as a noun: compare svapne-dus.vapnyá- ‘bad dream

97 A finite thematic aorist form átr. pam (sg.) occurs in the Atharvaveda, but even if this form is old, it does not change the functional isolation of tr. pát.
98 On this form, see Renou (a: ), who compares ¯ıs.át with Av. išarә and argues that it could represent a form in -n-t-, related to the ∗-r-/-n- stems by Benveniste (b: –).

6.10 Caland adjectives 

(occurring) in sleep’ in which the first element is clearly a noun and which occurs beside ja¯grad-dus.vapnyá- ‘bad dream of a waking person’ (AV 16.6.9).
All the evidence so far considered gives no support to the assumption that participles could be used as adverbs in the a.sg.nt. The most difficult form to account for is patayát at 1.4.7c ((6.77)) from the participle patáyant- ‘flying’, taken for an a.sg.nt. participle e.g. by Jamison (1983a: 61, fn. 32). It is hard to derive a meaningful translation from an adverbial interpretation; but there are only two alternative possibilities, neither of which is entirely satisfactory. One is to follow Grassmann (1873: 764) in analysing patayát as the first element of a hypocoristic compound of which the second element has to be supplied from the following compound.99 Following Geldner’s (RV: vol. 1: 5) note ad loc. patayát would represent a compound ∗pa¯tayátsakha-; the short vowel of the root could represent a later corruption based on the erroneous reanalysis of the hypocoristic compound as an adverb. The second alternative is to see the form as a nonce metrical replacement for the regular a.sg. masculine patáyantam, meaning it could be interpreted adjectivally, agreeing with the object of the clause.100

(6.77) ém a¯śúm a¯śáve bhara/

yajñaśríyam. nr. ma¯´danam/

to=pcl swift.a swift.d bring.imp.2sg sacrifice-glory.a man-intoxicator.a

patayán

mandayát-sakham

fly.prs.ptc.a.sg.m(?) rejoice.cs.prs.ptc-friend.a

‘Bring to the swift one the swift (Soma),/ who is the glory of the sacrifice,

intoxicator of men,/ who makes his friend fly and rejoice (or who makes his

friend rejoice as he flies).’ (RV 1.4.7)

The problematic patayát aside, we can safely conclude that Rigvedic Sanskrit did not permit the formation of adverbs from the a.sg.nt. of participles, whereas this was permitted for any lexical adjective; this restriction may well be inherited.101 The regular formation of adverbs from the a.sg.nt. of adjectives, including lexicalized participles, but never from synchronic participles in the Rigveda, demonstrates a clear functional distinction between the two categories.

. Caland adjectives
In this chapter we first considered aorist and stative participles, core members of the participial system, and then moved out toward the periphery, as it were, considering participles with analogical bases, lexicalized stems, participial derivatives, and lastly

<!-- page 299 -->

 The category of participles
adverbs, none of which are synchronically participles but which do help us to make some judgements about what is or is not a participle. In the final part of this chapter we consider another category that is strictly speaking unrelated, at least synchronically, to the participial system: Caland adjectives.
Toward the end of the 19th century Willem Caland, a Dutch philologist, noticed that in Sanskrit and Avestan certain adjectival suffixes, namely -ra- and -ma-, alternated with another suffix, -i-, when the adjective appeared in compounds (Caland 1892: 266– 8; 1893: 592). Wackernagel (1897: 8–14) noted further parallel alternations involving other adjectival suffixes, and observed that such alternations appear also in Ancient Greek, permitting the phenomenon to be projected back to Proto-Indo-European. The types and extent of these suffixal alternations were further investigated and expanded over the following decades (e.g. Risch 1937; 1974; Chantraine 1967; Watkins 1971; Bader 1975a, 1975b), such that Nussbaum (1976) was able to speak of the ‘Caland system’ of suffixal alternation found with a certain set of Indo-European roots. Any root that is part of this system could form nouns, adjectives, and also verbal derivatives using particular ‘Caland suffixes’, while roots that were not part of the Caland system utilized other suffixes. The roots that participate in the Caland system are those that form basic adjectives with a ‘property concept’ meaning, i.e. basic adjectival concepts such as colour and size.102 Rau (2009) builds on Nussbaum’s work, exploring both the adjectival and verbal components of the Caland system in great detail.
The Caland system is of relevance to the category of participles in Rigvedic Sanskrit insofar as certain adjectival formations that were part of the Caland system are superficially identical to participle formations. This is most clearly the case with Caland adjectives in -nt- and active participles in -nt-.103 The existence of a category of lexical adjectives in -nt- distinct from the category of participles was well recognized before Caland’s initial observations (e.g. Bartholomae 1888: 563f.);104 its association with the Caland system was only obliquely noted by Caland himself (Caland 1892: 267), but was accepted by Bartholomae (1900: 136, fn. 2) and later (and secondarily) in the same volume by Hübschmann (1900).105 The category was taken for granted as part of the Caland system by Bader (1975a), Nussbaum (1976), and most recently Rau
102 Balles (; ; ), Rau (); on the term ‘property concept’, see Dixon (; ). 103 Discussed in more detail in Lowe (c). 104 Nevertheless some authors treat all Indo-European ∗-nt- adjectives as participles in origin; cf. e.g. Shatskov (). 105 Bartholomae’s note actually points out that the alternation between Sanskrit maha¯´nt- ‘great’ and máhi- ‘id.’ in compound had been noted much earlier by Böhtlingk–Roth (PW: vol. , : ). Of course the latter merely noted the connection in this word, and did not claim any systematic rule or pattern for Sanskrit or PIE.

---

<!-- page 300 -->

6.10 Caland adjectives 
(2009). The evidence for a Caland -nt- suffix, in Sanskrit and other Indo-European
languages, is collected and analysed in Lowe (2013c).106 The most widely accepted Caland adjective in ∗-nt- reconstructable for PIE is the
very one first noted by Caland, attested in Sanskrit br. hánt- ‘high’, Avestan bәrәzan. t‘id.’, the Irish PN Brigit, Germanic names in Burgund-, and the Tocharian compound
(A) kom. -pärka¯nt, (B) kaum. -pirko ‘sunrise’, with alternative suffixes seen in Avestan bәrәz-i- (∗-i-), Hittite park-u- and Armenian barj-r (∗-u-), and Tocharian (A) pärk-är, (B) park-re (∗-ro-). The meaning of br. hánt- ‘high’ and √related forms is a typical property concept meaning. The root from which it is built, br. h ‘be high’, is primarily adjectival rather than verbal: verbal stems formed to this root are rare and sporadic
in attested languages, while adjectival and nominal derivatives are more common. In
particular there is no verbal stem to which br. hánt- could be a participle. It is fairly clear in this case, then, that we are dealing with a form that is both synchronically and
diachronically a lexical adjective, which never had any necessary connection with the
verbal system or verbal tense-aspect stems.107 A few other Rigvedic forms in -nt- can
likewise be attributed to the Caland system with relative certainty: the isolated hapax r. hánt- ‘small(?)’ (RV 10.28.9c);108 dódha(n)t- ‘violent’;109 maha¯´nt- ‘great’;110 rúśant‘bright’.111
In many other cases, however, the categorization of a form in -nt- to the Caland
system rather than the participial system is less obvious. In Lowe (2013c) I argue that a
number of Rigvedic forms in -nt- have been incorrectly labelled participles in previous
literature and should rather be treated as Caland system adjectives; some of these
106 Since it is strictly tangential to the present topic, I treat all adjectival -nt- formations as part of the inherited Caland adjective system, but it is possible that some such forms were synchronically separate and/or had distinct diachronic origins, e.g. by reanalysis of -n- stems (as suggested by Nikolaev : f. and p.c.). √ 107 Following this analysis, Mayrhofer (EWA: vol. : ) lists br. hánt- separately from the verbal root
br. h and makes no suggestion that it could be a lexicalized participle. In contrast, Klingenschmitt (: –), comparing the Hittite and Tocharian evidence for finite forms of this root, concluded that it had a root aorist in PIE and that ∗bhrghónt-, the predecessor of Sanskrit br. hánt-, was a participle to the aorist stem (likewise Shatskov ).
108 Listed as a Caland adjective by Rau (: ); various etymological connections have been suggested (see Mayrhofer, EWA: vol. : ) but none are secure. If, following Sihler (: ) we take it as an Indic creation based on its apparent antonym br. hánt- ‘high’, it would still be a Caland adjective, albeit secondarily.
109 Listed by Mayrhofer (EWA: vol. : ) as a Caland variant beside dudhrá- ‘violent’, dúdhi- ‘id.’ etc., but not noted by Rau (). For alternative explanations, see Goto¯ (: –), who assumes this is a participle to an otherwise unattested verb, and Schaefer (: – with references).
110 A Caland adjective according to Nussbaum (: –), Rau (: ), and cf. fn.  above. Caland variants include Gr. megálos ‘great’, Hitt. mekki ‘much’, RV máhas- ‘greatness’; Lat. inge¯ns ‘huge’ may be cognate with maha¯´nt-.
111 Mayrhofer (EWA: vol. : ); it is not mentioned by Rau (), but Nussbaum (: ) suggests a possible correspondence with OIr. lóchet ‘flash (of lightning)’, presumably within the Caland system, and such a connection seems sensible to avoid positing yet another adjectival -nt- suffix. Its accent in compound, e.g. rúśat-paśu- ‘whose beasts are bright(-coloured)’, is evidence against a participial analysis (cf. §..).

---

<!-- page 301 -->

 The category of participles

forms have previously been associated with the Caland system by previous authors, while others have not.
For example, the form járant- ‘old’ (along with its cognates Greek gé√ro¯n ‘old man’ and Oss. zärond ‘old’) is sometimes analysed as a present participle to j¯r. ‘be old’, at least in origin (synchronically lexicalized), and sometimes as a Caland adjective.112 Support for the latter analysis comes from the property concept nature of the root, and alternating Caland formations, including e.g. jívri-/jírvi- ‘weak’ (Pinault 1988), and jarás- ‘old age’. Although a verbal stem exists to which járant- could in principle be a participle, it cannot be aligned with this stem synchronically since it differs in argument structure, the subject of járant- being an experiencer of ‘being old’ ((6.78)) while the subject of the corresponding (transitive) finite stem járati is an agent of an eventuality ‘make old’ ((6.79)).

‘I find no use in a gambler,/ just like an old horse for sale.’ (RV 10.34.3cd)

śarádo ná ma¯´sa¯/ ná dya¯´va índram

<!-- page 302 -->

6.10 Caland adjectives 

The correct analysis of juránt- was provided by Nussbaum (1976: 18–20), who noted that the two attested forms of the word are both weak case forms, d.sg. juraté (RV 7.68.6a) and g.pl. jurata¯´m (2.34.10d), and argued that they therefore represent the weak stem of an earlier amphikinetic paradigm of which járant- represents the strong stem, ie. járant-/jurat´-.113 There is some support for this also in the distribution of forms in the Rigveda, since the ‘secondary’ full-grade weak case forms of járant- found in the RV are found largely in late books.114 Goto¯ (1987: 153, fn. 238) argues the same point without reference to Nussbaum or the Caland system.115
A form not previously associated with the Caland system before Lowe (2012a; 2013c) is the frequent uśánt- ‘willing’, usu√ally analysed as a lexicalized, morphologically regular present participle to the root vaś ‘desire’ (e.g. Wackernagel–Debrunner, AiG: vol. 2:2, §70b, pp. 163–4; Keydana 2013: 273).116 This supposed present participle contrasts with the corresponding finite stem in argument structure, however: finite forms are commonly transitive in the sense ‘desire X’ ((6.82)), while the participle never has an accusative object, meaning simply ‘willing’ ((6.83)). As a characteristic human propensity or experienced state (like ‘fearful’, ‘bold’, ‘friendly’, ‘hungry’, etc.) ‘willing’ fits the category of property concept adjectives.

‘Willing(ly) we would establish you,/ willing(ly) we would kindle you;/ will-

ing(ly) convey here the willing/ forefather-spirits to eat the oblation.’

(RV 10.16.12)

113 On the vocalism of the weak grade stem, see Pinault (: –). 114 The stem járant- is attested in the RV at ..a, ..b, ..b, ..a, ..c, and
..a, which looks like a late attestation, although it is clearly inherited; on the other hand, juránt- is
attested at ..d and ..a, which shows that these archaic forms supply the missing old RV evidence
for this adjective. 115 The forms are also discussed by Kümmel (a: –). 116 While assuming that it is formally a participle, García Ramón (a: ) notes that uśánt- shares
its lack of object with its cognate Greek heko¯´n, ‘a coincidence which has not been given the attention it
deserves’.

---

<!-- page 303 -->

 The category of participles

Supporting evidence that uśánt- represents an inherited Caland adjective comes
from Avestan, where an alternate Caland suffix is found in the -h-stem (i.e. inherited ∗-s-stem) noun vasah- ‘will’.117 Further the Greek form hék¯elos ‘at ease’ appears to show a different Caland suffix, PIE ∗-lo-, to the same root, albeit to an extended stem. Most significant, however, is the comparison with the Greek cognate of uśánt-, heko¯´n, which
with its contrasting stem ablaut suggests a form with amphikinetic ablaut underlying the Sanskrit and Greek forms in PIE, i.e. ∗uék-ont-/uk-nt´- (Rix 1976: 123, 144).118 This ablaut matches that reconstructable for járant-/juránt-, above, and in Lowe (2012a;
2013c) I argue that amphikinetic ablaut was, at least in Late PIE, a distinguishing feature of Caland adjectives in ∗-nt- in contrast to participles in ∗-nt-; this will be
discussed further in §7.2.3.
Like uśánt-, several other forms that could, in formal terms, be treated as participles
are in fact better analysed as Caland adjectives. The form dhr. s.ánt- ‘bold’ is often analysed as a prese√nt or aorist participle,119 but in fact aligns rather with the Caland system of the root dhr. s. ‘be bold’, seen in dhr´. s.i- ‘bold’, Av. darši- ‘id.’, Gr. thrasús ‘id.’ As in (6.84) and (6.85), almost all forms of dhr. s.ánt- in the Rigveda are a.sg.nt. or i.sg.nt. forms used as adverbs, which, as discussed in the previous section, is a possibility
excluded for synchronic participles;120 non-adverbial forms of the word are purely
adjectival in function ((6.86)).

6.10 Caland adjectives 

‘You, bold one, boldly aid Suda¯s/ of acceptable offerings, with all your helps.’ (RV 7.19.3ab)

In the same way, citánt- ‘brilliant, excellent’ and śucánt- ‘flaming, bright, pure’

are best treated as Caland adjectives, beside suffixal variants such as citrá- ‘bril-

liant, excellent’ and śukrá- ‘flaming, bright, pure’ and śúci- ‘id.’ respectively, rather

than as aorist participles. The verbal stem apparently corresponding to the form

tujánt- ‘eager’ is marginal in the Rigveda, attested only in a hapax mediopassive

form tujete at RV 1.61.14b (beside a class 7 nasal present, a class 10 present, and a

passive present stem); tujánt- aligns rather with the Caland system seen in Rigvedic

túgra- and túji- (both PNs), and possibly ‘hatred’ (Kölligan 2002: 144–5). The form

the Ancient Greek bhra¯´jant- ‘sparkling’

s-ste√m noun stúgos to bhra¯j ‘sparkle’

would be the only active form of this root in the Rigveda, if it were a participle;

post-Rigvedic active forms are rare, late, and doubtful.121 A Caland system for this

root is suggested by the s-stem noun bhra¯´jas- ‘brilliance, sparkle’, and the irregular

accent of the compounds bhra¯´jaj-janman- ‘of brilliant origin’ and bhra¯´jad-r. s..ti- ‘whose spears sparkle’ also supports the non-participial status and origin of th√is word.122 The form sáhant- ‘prevailing’ ((6.87)) cannot be a present participle to sah ‘conquer,

prevail’ since the root is otherwise medium tantum, and the closest corresponding

stem (the class 1 mediopassive present) is consistently transitive, including in the

participle sáhama¯na- ((6.88)), also containing a perfect participle to the same root);

the frequent s-stem noun sáhas- ‘power’ support the positing of a Caland system for

this root.123

 The category of participles

(6.88) sá pavasva sáhama¯nah.

‘You, flow, conquering the lovers of war,/ driving away the evil spirits and

dangers,/ being conqueror of enemies with good weapons, O Soma.’

(RV 9.110.12)

There is no verbal root in Sanskrit with which the synchronic adjective p´r. s.ant‘spotted’ can be connected;124 a Caland system is suggested by the Avestan -u-stem adjective YAv. paršu-, beside the Avestan cognate of p´r. s.ant-, paršat- (in the YAv. compound paršat.gauu- PN, lit. ‘he of the speckled cows’). The first element of the unclear br. bád-uk˜tha- ‘of babbling speech(?)’ (RV 8.32.10a), possibly related to the equally unclear br. bú- ‘babbling(?)’, may also represent a Caland adjective in -nt-, and there may be a further Caland variant seen in Greek bárbaros.125
In contrast to these forms that are indistinguishable in forms from genuine participles, but that are underlyingly distinct, I argue in Lowe (2012a; 2013c) th√at there is no reason to treat the form tápant- ‘hot’ as a Caland adjective to the root tap ‘be hot’, beside such forms as Ved. tápu- ‘hot’ and RCS teplъ, as assumed by Nussbaum (1976: 23–4) and Rau (2009: 94, 176). This is because there are no morphological or functional discrepancies between tápant- and the corresponding verbal stem. In fact of the three forms of tápant- found in the Rigveda, two are agentive and transitive in the sense ‘heating’ ((6.89), (6.91)) and only one displays the adjectival stative-intransitive sense ‘hot, burning’ ((6.90) = (5.96)); both senses are found with the corresponding verbal stem ((6.92), (6.93)), where the latter may derive from the former by ellipsis of the object. So although a Caland adjective cannot be ruled out, all examples of tápantcan simply be analysed as synchronically regular participles.

One form not noted in Lowe (2012a) or Lowe (2013c) is vra¯´dhant- ‘s√trong, selfsufficient’, attested nine times in the Rigveda and the only form of the root vra¯dh ‘be strong, self-sufficient’ attested, with the exception of a single, secondary, finite form vra¯dhanta (5.6.7b; cf. Goto¯ 1987: 302 with references). A Caland analysis of this form has been proposed by Catt (2013), who notes that the corresponding root in Avestan is exclusively medial, so that even if genuine finite forms of the verb were attested in Sanskrit, vra¯´dhant- would be unlikely to align with them. The other possibility is that vra¯´dhant- is a lexicalized participle to an unparalleled active stem, as assumed in Lowe (2012a). Functionally the word betrays its non-participial status, being used merely as an adjective meaning ‘arrogant’ ((6.94)). The derivative superlative vra¯´dhantama‘most arrogant’ at RV 1.150.3b is likewise evidence for the adjectival status of this word, since such derivatives are not found with genuine participles.
(6.94) áva vra¯´dhantam abhinad vr. dháś cit down proud.a.sg.m cut.2sg strong even ‘You, strong one, cut down even the arrogant one.’ (RV 10.69.11d)

In contrast to -nt-, the other participle suffixes in Rigvedic Sanskrit, -vám. s- and -(m)a¯na-, are not, at least in principle, ambiguous insofar as their PIE predecessors
can be reconstructed exclusively as participial suffixes and had no purely adjectival
uses.
However, in Lowe (2012b) I argue that in certain Rigvedic forms the suffix -a¯namay reflect not the participial suffix -(m)a¯na- (< PIE ∗-mhno-), but a complex Caland

---

<!-- page 307 -->

 The category of participles

suffix -a¯-na-. This -a¯-na- would reflect PIE ∗-eh-no-, an adjectival formation from the stative stem in ∗-eh-, parallel to adjectival derivatives in ∗-eh-tó-, ∗-eh-nt-, ∗-eh-ti-, etc. discussed by Jasanoff (2003b). The stative formant in ∗-eh- is, following
Watkins (1971), part of the Caland system; furthermore if, following Jasanoff (2003b),
the formant is historically identical to the PIE instrumental singular suffix, then
these formations correspond to the system of deinstrumental derivation discussed by Nussbaum (1996; 1998) whereby the suffixes ∗-tó- and ∗-no- could be attached to e.g. ∗-o-, ∗-u-, ∗-i-stem, and root-noun instrumentals, as in e.g. Latin aegro¯-tus ‘sick’, where aegro¯- reflects the inherited instrumental of the ∗-o- stem seen in aegrum ‘distress’.126
So, the form√tr. s.a¯n. á- ((6.95), (6.96)), usually analysed morphologically as an aorist participle to tr. s. ‘become thirsty, dry’, is functionally no more than an adjective ‘thirsty’, equivalent to the adjectives tr. s.n. áj- ‘id.’, tr. s.ya¯´vant- ‘id.’, tr. s.itá- ‘id.’. There is no corresponding finite stem to which this could be a participle; Jasanoff (2003a: 155) reconstructs a passive aorist ∗átars.i for this root on the basis of tr. s.a¯n. á-, but as discussed above there is no real support for passive aorist participles in the Rigveda
(§6.2.1). An active thematic aorist is marginally attested post-Rigveda in the phrase ma¯´ tr. s.at ‘may he not thirst’, which might support the existence of an earlier root aorist but does not directly support a medial participle. On the other hand the root
is semantically appropriate for Caland formations, and one clear Caland formation, a ∗-u-stem adjective, can be reconstructed for PIE, on the basis of Skt. tr. s.ú- ‘greedy’, Avestan taršu- ‘dry, firm’, and Gothic þaursus ‘dried, withered’.

‘He drenched the thirsty fields and deserts.’ (RV 4.19.7c)

6.10 Caland adjectives 

√

‘be bright, light’ (hapax at RV 6.6.2a),129 piśa¯n√á- ‘adorned’ to piś ‘adorn’ (hapax at RV

7.57.3c),130 (RV ×6)

śub√ha¯ná- ‘beautiful’ (RV ×2) to to ruc ‘shin√e’,132 and possibly

śubh ‘be beautiful’,131 also píya¯na- ‘swollen’

‘shining’ ‘swell’,133

to sah ‘conquer’.135

For none of these forms is a corresponding finite verbal stem attested, while

the Caland systems for these roots are robust.136 In particular, several are attested

alongside root nouns, the instrumental of which would serve as the (stative) stem for

the formation. So beside vipa¯ná- we find víp- ‘inspired speech’ with instrumental vipa¯´

(RV ×5), beside dhr. s.a¯n. á- the root noun (used in the dative as an infinitive) a¯dh´r. s.‘attack’, beside śvita¯ná- the second element of the compound su¯rya-śvít- ‘bright as

the sun’, to piśa¯ná- the compound viśva-píś- ‘all adorned’ and the YAv. noun +pis-

‘ornament’, beside ruca¯ná- the noun rúc- ‘light’, beside śubha¯ná- the noun śúbh-

‘beauty’, and beside prasaha¯ná- the noun sáh- ‘victory’. At the same time, several of the roots involved show evidence of statives in ∗-eh- in other Indo-European languages:

PbeIEsid∗e√ttr.es.ra¯sn.(áL-IVwe: 6fi3n7d);

OHG dorr¯en ‘dry out’, beside dr. s.a¯n. á-, Greek

(LIV: 147); beside śvita¯ná-, OCS pro-svьtě sę ‘lit up’ to PIE ∗

‘dry’ (but c√onfident’

atolsoPI‘dEr∗y√ingd’h)etros

kueit (LIV: 340); beside
