Further evidence for the existence of a Caland suffix in -a¯ná-, at least synchronically

in early Vedic, is provided by the Vedic adjectives in -asa¯na-, e.g. bhiyása¯na- ‘fearful’,

sahasa¯ná- ‘powerful’.137 These adjectives became mildly productive in Vedic, but are

originally adjectival derivatives in -a¯na- based on s-stem nouns, which by implication

means roots which had or could have had Caland systems. Rau (2009) does in fact

list a few of these adjectives under the relevant roots; this is best analysed as a Vedic

129 Beside Caland śvetá- ‘bright, light’, śvítna- ‘id.’, śvitrá- (with Middle Persian cognate spihr) ‘white’ (on
this semantic development, see Roesler : –) and, with an irregular dissimilation, the first element
of the compounds śiti-pád- ‘white/bright footed’ and śiti-pr. s..thá- ‘white/bright backed’. 130 Beside Caland forms like śilpá- ‘colourful; decoration’ < ∗piślá- (Rau : ), péśas- ‘colour,
ornament’, YAv. +pis- ‘decoration’. 131 Beside e.g. śubhrá- ‘id.’, śóbhas- ‘beauty’, śúbh- ‘id.’ 132 Beside e.g. rúc- ‘light’, rocís. - ‘radiance’, Av. raocah- ‘bright; light’. 133 Beside Caland system forms p¯´ıvas- ‘fat’ (n.), p¯´ıvan- ‘fat’, p¯´ıvara- ‘fat’; cf. also LIV (pp. –). 134 Beside Caland forms pr. thú- ‘broad’, práthas- ‘breadth’; cf. also LIV (p. ). 135 Beside e.g. sáh- ‘victory’, sáhas- ‘power’. 136 Three of these forms appear beside active thematic aorists: tr. s.a¯n. á- beside sg. tr. s.at ‘thirst’ (AV+),
dhr. s.a¯n. á- beside sg. adhr. s.as ‘you dared’ (ŚB), and possibly piśa¯ná- beside sg. imp. apiśa¯´ ‘adorn’ (RV ..c; secondarily agent-oriented?). This pattern of correspondence may well be mere chance, but an
intriguing possibility is that the active thematic aorists reflect earlier stative paradigms (as áśayat for earlier ∗aśaya), to which the -a¯na- forms might be regular participles. Semantically there would be little difference
between taking the -a¯na- forms as participles to a (root) stative stem, or as adjectival derivatives from a different stative stem (-a¯- < ∗-eh-).
137 On these, see Renou (: –, ), Leumann (: , fn. ), and Insler (b).

---

<!-- page 309 -->

 The category of participles
extension of the marginal Caland suffix -a¯-na- within the Caland system, rather than as a formation of pseudo-participles to nominal stems.
It is of course impossible to be certain of the correct analysis of isolated forms like piśa¯ná- and the others. Given the productivity of -a¯na- within the participial system (§6.2.2), it is equally possible to analyse some or all of these forms as synchronic participles, albeit analogically created, rather than as adjectival formations within the Caland system. And given the synchronic homophony between the participial and proposed adjectival -a¯na- suffixes, a form created as an adjective might easily have been reanalysed as a participle, or vice versa. None of the forms discussed here have clear participial syntax or semantics, such that there is nothing to prevent us analysing them all as adjectives, both synchronically and diachronically; but since genuine participles can be adjectival in their functionality, nothing can be established for certain.
At this point we must admit the difficulty of drawing too strong a line between adjectival and verbal derivation. An isolated form such as piśa¯ná- could have been created on the basis of multiple weak analogies, rather than one clear analogy. The possible existence of isolated aorist participles to semantically similar roots, the (perhaps largely coincidental) frequency of -a¯na- formations to roots with Caland systems, and the apparent identity between the active participial suffix -nt- and the Caland adjective suffix -nt-, may all have influenced the creation of a form such as piśa¯ná-, which thus could not necessarily be classified as either ‘adjectival’ or ‘participial’, either diachronically or synchronically. The isolation of the forms concerned means that their synchronic status may never have been clear.
Nevertheless, while in some cases there is ambiguity between participles and Caland adjectives, such that a definitive analysis cannot be established, in other cases the evidence clearly favours an adjectival status; this is the case with most forms treated in this section. Forms treated in this section as Caland adjectives were not, then, part of the participial system, either synchronically or diachronically.
. Conclusion
In this chapter I have presented an overview of the Rigvedic participial system, distinguishing ambiguous categories within the core part of the system, considering the ‘edges’ of the system, and finally considering processes and categories that strictly speaking are outside the participial system, despite apparent or superficial relations to it. The synchronic Rigvedic category of tense-aspect stem participles, which have been the subject of this work, contains only those forms that are within the participial system, as defined in this chapter.

---

<!-- page 310 -->


Conclusion
In this chapter I draw on the analyses presented, and proposals made, in the previous chapters, to assess the category of participles in a broader linguistic perspective, both in Rigvedic Sanskrit and more widely. First, I consider the category of Rigvedic tenseaspect stem participles in its internal coherence and in the wider context of the verbal and nominal systems of the language. Secondly, I consider what may be said about the origins of the Sanskrit participles, i.e. about participles in Proto-Indo-European. Finally, I discuss the typology of non-finite verbal categories and the position of Rigvedic participles in a typological perspective.
. The category of tense-aspect stem participles
The subject of this work has been the tense-aspect stem participles of the Rigveda. I initially defined this category as the set of morphological adjectives productively formed to verbal-tense aspect stems. However, only a subset of forms in this morphologically defined category actually function as participles. In Chapter 6 I showed that various functionally divergent forms, or sets of forms, must be considered separate, in synchronic terms, from the category of tense-aspect stem participles, which is otherwise relatively coherent in functional terms. In this section I draw together some conclusions about the status of the tense-aspect stem participles category in Rigvedic Sanskrit.
.. Coherence of the category In syntactic and semantic terms, the clearest division within the category as morphologically defined is that between lexicalized and non-lexicalized participles. From a functional perspective, lexicalized participles cannot strictly be treated as synchronic participles, although morphologically they often cannot be distinguished from them (§§4.8.4 and 6.6). So, lexicalized participles are restricted in the extent to which they can share the argument structure of the verbal stem to which they appear to be built; that is, they regularly do not govern arguments such as objects, even when the corresponding verbal stem regularly does. In addition, lexicalized participles are

---

<!-- page 311 -->

 Conclusion
restricted in terms of their contextual functionality: they are used only in purely nominal or adjectival functions, i.e. functions typical of their new lexicalized word class, and not in the wider range of contextual functions found with participles proper.
This division cuts across the tense-aspect stem categories, and has no morphological marking. In fact some word forms, such as sunvánt-, stuvánt-, and gr. n. ánt-, represent two homophonous but distinct lexemes, one a synchronic participle derived regularly from a verbal tense-aspect stem, the other a synchronic noun with no functional correspondence to the participial system (as formalized in (3.56) and (3.57), and cf. §6.6.2). The same is true of negated participles (§6.8.3), which usually function as secondary adjectival derivatives from participles, but occasionally function as synchronic participles with negative sense. In the case of negated participles, no single form is clearly attested in both functions, but this is likely due to their rarity.
Even this relatively clear division between lexicalized and non-lexicalized participles is not black and white but has, like most categorial divisions, a grey middle ground. In contrast to clearly lexicalized forms such as da¯śvám. s-, r¯´ıs.ant-/rís.ant(§6.6.1) or dvis.ánt- (§6.6.2), forms such as śám. sant- (§6.6.2) or anu¯ca¯ná- (§6.6.3) cannot be definitively assigned to either category. This reflects the sometimes significant data limitations in the finite corpus of Rigvedic Sanskrit.
Besides this major dichotomy within the morphologically defined category of tense-aspect stem adjectives, the category is otherwise relatively coherent in syntactic and semantic terms, showing only minor differences in functional range between subcategories such as the different tense-aspect stems. For example, the formally difficult collection of nonce participles (i.e. the participial nonce-formations, §6.4.2), where one might expect to find considerable functional deviance, is in fact relatively regular in terms of syntax and semantics. This shows that, although morphologically problematic, these forms were created and employed within the context of a functionally clear category of tense-aspect stem participles. Another category in which morphological irregularity is accompanied by syntactic and semantic coherence to the participial system is that of the productive mediopassive aorists (§6.2.2).
Syntactic and semantic distinctions between the different tense-aspects, and even between different case forms of participles, are relatively minor variations within a generally coherent category, and are based on inherent differences between tenseaspects and cases rather than divisions within the participial category itself. So, the fact that stative participles are found in a more restricted range of contextual functions than present participles is simply due to the inherent semantic limitations of stativity. The considerably greater frequency and range of contextual converbal semantics found with nominative participles relative to participles in other cases is due to the tendency for nominatives to play more agent-like roles in the matrix clause (§5.3.10). These differences could be described in terms of slightly differing positions on the cline of verbality; stative verbal stems, for example, may be considered relatively less prototypically verbal than dynamic verbal stems. But even so, participles to stative and

---

<!-- page 312 -->

7.1 The category of tense-aspect stem participles 
dynamic stems are all positioned within a unified, coherent category of tense-aspect stem participles.
The category of tense-aspect stem participles is, then, a functionally coherent one, but it can be defined only with reference to morphology, syntax, and semantics. Not all forms that fit the morphological criteria for participle-hood align with the category in syntactic and semantic terms, while some morphologically problematic forms do align in those terms.
.. The tense-aspect stem
Given that morphology, i.e. here formation to a verbal tense-aspect stem, is not the only criterion for membership of the category of participles, it may be worthwhile attempting to clarify the status of the tense-aspect stem morphology. If only the majority of the functional category of participles are formed to a verbal tense-aspect stem, while many forms apparently derived from a tense-aspect stem are not synchronic participles, is it wrong to use this morphological feature as a defining characteristic of the category under discussion? Is the tense-aspect stem incidental to the category as a whole?
This latter question must ultimately be answered in the negative, both synchronically and diachronically, although the details differ somewhat from these two perspectives. Synchronically there is no necessary reason why a particular morphological feature, such as a tense-aspect stem, should have a single function. It is theoretically possible for the tense-aspect stem formants in the participles to be synchronically entirely unrelated and incidental to the appearance and function of the same formants in the finite verb system. This would be parallel to the clearly distinct (at least in synchronic terms) tense-aspect properties of the finite imperfect and the finite present despite the fact that they share the same present tense-aspect stem morphology (§2.3.4.1). There is, however, real overlap in tense-aspect properties between the finite present and present participles. This overlap is not necessarily identity: the present participle expresses imperfective aspect and/or present tense, while the finite present necessarily expresses both. Nevertheless, this semantic correspondence is most economically explained by assuming a synchronic connection between the two categories, and specifically (at least partially) shared values for the shared tense-aspect formant.
The status of lexicalized (former) participles, such as sunvánt- ‘presser of Soma’, in this context is a synchronic problem with a diachronic explanation. By definition, lexicalized participles were, at some stage in the past, synchronically regular participles, but they have become separated from the synchronic system of verbal derivation by processes of semantic specialization leading to lexicalization. Processes of grammaticalization tend to lead to the fossilization of morphological features, as the function of words can change faster than their form. Morphology can therefore

---

<!-- page 313 -->

 Conclusion
reflect a more archaic stage of of the language than current usage. The paradigmatic recategorization of a word is dependent on semantic change and can lag behind that change. Morphological categories can be understood as generalizations over sets of words with shared morphemes and shared functions; so for example the Sanskrit suffix -si is analysed as 2sg. not because of any property internal to itself, but because that is how words containing that morpheme function. If the phonological form of the morpheme were to change (e.g. to -s, or even to -∅, by apocope), its categorial status would remain so long as it retains its former function. But if the function of a morpheme, or rather the function of a set of words sharing the same morpheme, changes, then a different generalization must be made over that set of words, and they may be assigned to a different morphological category. There may or may not then be analogical pressures to alter a particular morpheme, or to alter the morphological makeup of the words concerned, in order to reflect the new function of the category within its paradigmatic context; what matters is that function (i.e. semantics and, as far as is relevant, syntax) is always ahead of morphology.
On this understanding, finite present stems in the Rigveda are analysed as such because that is their function, and to the extent that their functionality can be broken down and assigned to particular morphemes it may be reasonable to analyse the ‘tenseaspect stem’ formants as representing (at least part of) the specific properties of the present tense-aspect. The fact that there are around ten different but synchronically isofunctional present tense-aspect stem formants, of which different verbs arbitrarily (in synchronic terms) select one or another as their primary means of present stem formation, is a morphological fossilization of an earlier stage of the language where these different suffixes had distinct functions. This is an example of a functional merger of morphologically distinct categories. It is equally possible to have functional splits of a single morphological category. This is certainly the case with the finite imperfect and present in Sanskrit (§2.3.4). The question is whether or to what extent this happened also with tense-aspect stem participles.
.. The participial tense-aspect stem
The evidence of participial syntax and semantics in the Rigveda suggests that tenseaspect stem participles constituted a distinct functional category within the verbal system, which was evolving under the influence of, but not identically to, the finite tense-aspect system. The ongoing obsolescence of the aorist participle, alongside the retention of the finite aorist as a productive category, is one clear example of the evolution of the participial system in distinction from the finite system. At a slightly later stage of the language, a similar process saw the loss of the perfect participle while the finite perfect survived. While the participial system was undergoing changes, the rest of the verbal system was also changing, and this did not necessarily require corresponding changes in the participles. The functional split of the present

---

<!-- page 314 -->

7.1 The category of tense-aspect stem participles 
and imperfect, for example, produced a functionally distinct tense-aspect category, the imperfect, for which there did not exist functionally corresponding participles; again, the independence of the participial system is seen in the fact that no category of ‘imperfect participles’ was created.1
The tense-aspect participles were evolving somewhat separately from the finite verbal system, but they were not evolving in isolation from one another. The Rigveda attests the early stages of a reorganization of the participial system, from aspect marking to tense marking. This accounts, at least partially, for the obsolescence of the aorist participle, leaving a single participial formation for expressing past tense— the perfect participle, corresponding to the single participial formation for expressing present tense (the present participle). The future participle, though it does not express future tense in the Rigveda, ultimately came to do so (appendix: §A.4.3), resulting in a three-way tense distinction in the participial system. This development is also reflected in the increasingly productive substitution of the perfect suffix -vám. s- by the present suffix -nt- in stative perfect participles, showing that -vám. s- was becoming increasingly restricted to the expression of relative past time; in this the participial system anticipates the same development in the finite system.
The systematic independence of the participial system from that of the finite verb is seen also in the correlation of certain participles to tense-aspect stems which either differ in form or do not exist in the finite system. For example, idha¯ná- is synchronically a stative participle in most of its occurrences, even though the synchronic stative stem in finite forms has an infixed nasal formant (§6.2.1). Similarly, yuja¯ná- patterns synchronically as a stative although no finite stative is attested; this may be due to a semantic development affecting the corresponding finite stem (from patientive to agentive-reflexive sense) which isolated the participle (fn. 19, Ch. 6). Another such instance is the occasional use of siñcánt- as the synchronic participle corresponding to the unaccusative stem sicyáte (§6.1.2).
The standard approach to Sanskrit participles, found in several recent works on the Rigvedic verbal system, its to treat them as semantically equivalent to corresponding finite forms (granted the functional differences). Although this is based on the correct intuition that participles are fundamentally verb forms, it is wrong insofar as it does not recognize the independence of the participial category within the verbal system. The change from aspect to tense distinction in the participial system created a major systematic division between participles and finite verbs, and undoubtedly contributed to their contrasting fates: the impoverishment of the participial system, with the loss of aorist and later perfect participles, beside the survival of all inherited tense-aspects
1 In contrast, the lack of imperfect participles in Ancient Greek has an entirely different explanation. In Ancient Greek the present and imperfect still shared the same aspectual reference, and since Greek participles expressed purely aspect, the present participle corresponded functionally to both present and imperfect.

---

<!-- page 315 -->

 Conclusion
(except the stative) in the finite system long enough to be codified into Classical Sanskrit.
Tense-aspect stem participles formed a coherent synchronic category in Rigvedic Sanskrit, which was developing somewhat in parallel to, and somewhat separately from, the finite tense-aspect system. The synchronic coherence of this category is not affected by the existence of a large group of lexicalized participles, which show identical morphology but do not pattern with the rest of the category in terms of syntactic and semantic functionality. The development of lexicalized participles is simply another example of a functional split in a morphological category.2
At a later date the loss of the perfect participle was probably occasioned by its functional replacement by the absolutive (Tikkanen 1987: 109–13). At this stage of the language, however, there is no evidence for any systematic connection between the absolutive and the system of tense-aspect participles.
.. Participles within the verbal and nominal systems
In discussing the category of tense-aspect stem participles in itself, we have necessarily had to compare it to other categories, both verbal and nominal, bordering it. In this section we will develop these observations more fully in an attempt to understand the position of tense-aspect stem participles within the verbal and nominal systems of Rigvedic Sanskrit as a whole.
... Participles within the verbal system I have argued throughout this book that tense-aspect stem participles should be treated as inflectional verbal formations, rather than as derivational formations from verbal stems, since, for example, they obligatorily display the same argument structure as corresponding (inflectional) finite verbs (§4.8). It is necessary to acknowledge a degree of semantic disjunction between participles and finite verb forms inflected to the same tense-aspect stem, primarily in relation to tense-aspect reference. However, this is not problematic for an inflectional analysis of participles, since shared inflectional morphology does not necessarily imply a shared functionality to that morphology. As mentioned, the present tenseaspect stem displays clearly distinct tense-aspect properties even in two finite formations, the finite present and the imperfect, both of which are uncontroversially inflectional verbal categories. What it does suggest, as argued in the §7.1.3, is simply that tense-aspect stem participles formed a distinct and coherent subcategory within the inflectional verbal system.
From a functional perspective, participles share the majority of their syntactic capabilities with non-participial adjectives (§4.9), but their functional range extends
2 Or rather, a series of splits whereby each lexicalized participle separated independently from the participial system due to its own idiosyncratic semantic development. Conceived in this way, lexicalized participles do not constitute a single category, but are simply the set of words that independently became dissociated from the synchronic category of tense-aspect stem participles.

---

<!-- page 316 -->

7.1 The category of tense-aspect stem participles 
somewhat beyond that of the latter category, and participles even seem to be in the process of developing further verbal functionality (e.g. the development of periphrasis, §4.5.2). Their inflectional status and super-adjectival functionality distinguishes them not only from non-verbal adjectives but also from verb-derived adjectival formations like the -tá- adjective. This justifies the terminological distinction proposed in Chapter 1 (§1.2): tense-aspect stem participles are not verbal adjectives but ‘adjectival verbs’: inflectional verb forms which are adjectival in morphological, and partly functional, terms. Due to their adjectival morphology and functionality, tense-aspect stem participles can be analysed as less prototypically verbal than finite verbs in terms of the cline of verbality, but they are nonetheless paradigmatically fully integrated into the verbal system. If anything, they appear to be extending their functional range in the ‘more verbal’ direction.
Participles are commonly assumed to pattern with non-indicative finite verbs in expressing aspect but not tense, just as participles do in Ancient Greek. However, the Rigvedic situation is considerably less clear than the Greek, with participles in the process of developing the ability to express relative tense—an ability shared with finite indicative verb forms. Their differentiation from finite verb forms in regard to tenseaspect is rather in that they cannot stand alone in a clause without a finite verb, and hence regularly express tense and/or aspect only in relation to the matrix verb, rather than absolutely.3
... Participles within the nominal system The position of tense-aspect stem participles with respect to the nominal system is somewhat complicated by the fact that there exists a wide range of nominal and adjectival formations to which participles may stand in a particular relation (in contrast to the verbal system, where there is one other major category, finite verbs, with which participles are connected). The first observation must be that although tense-aspect stem participles share a considerable part of their functional range with adjectives, they can be employed in ways that other adjectives cannot, and cannot be used in certain ways that other adjectives can. So, for example, the nominative/accusative singular neuter of non-participial adjectives can be used as an adverb, while this is impossible with participles (§6.9). Nevertheless, they are functionally similar to adjectives in several respects. Their close relation to the nominal system is apparent also in the fact that participles undergoing lexicalization become synchronic adjectives or nouns (rather than, say, finite verbs); it may also be attested in the possible analogical influences between the participial system and the adjectival Caland system, which may have supported the creation of Caland-type adjectives in -a¯na- (§6.10).
It is not easy to assess the extent to which tense-aspect stem participles constitute a distinct category within the nominal system, as they do a distinct category within
3 The only partial exception to this is participles expressing contingency (§..).

---

<!-- page 317 -->

 Conclusion
the verbal system. It might be argued that, as adjectives with verbal features, they are not qualitatively different from the various categories of nouns and adjectives that also display ‘verbal’ features of one sort or another. Most obvious in this respect is the -tá- adjective, which at a later stage of the language was undeniably integrated into the verbal system, and even in the Rigveda appears to be more ‘verbal’ than many other adjectives (§6.5). Various other adjectives, which were not integrated into the verbal system at a later date, are often said to be relatively ‘verbal’ in the Rigveda, e.g. reduplicated adjectives in -i- like cákri-. There are even different ways to be verbal. While the -tá- adjective is a resultative formation which does not necessarily reflect the argument structure of the base verb, other noun and adjective categories, such as the reduplicated adjectives in -i-, can take object complements in the same way as finite verbs and tense-aspect stem participles (cf. §4.8.4).
From the perspective of the nominal system, there is no single syntactic or semantic feature which necessarily sets tense-aspect stem participles apart as qualitatively different from other nouns and adjectives. Rather, they simply display verbal syntax and semantics more consistently and to a greater degree than various other ‘verbal’ adjectives and nouns. However, this quantitative difference in ‘verbality’ reflects the qualitative difference discussed above. While nominal formations such as those in -tá-, -ú-, or -tr. - can be analysed as ‘verbal’ nouns or adjectives, tense-aspect stem participles are, in contrast, ‘adjectival verbs’. This difference in status is due to their position within the verbal system, and not directly to a distinct position within the nominal system; but it does also correspond to distinct functionality relative to other nominal categories. There is, naturally, considerable syntactic and semantic overlap between verbal adjectives and adjectival verbs, and it is even possible for one to develop into or be reanalysed as another diachronically (as with the -tá- adjective and originally, perhaps, with the tense-aspect stem participles themselves), but from a synchronic perspective the difference is clear, in terms of derivational status and ‘core’ properties. While verbal adjectives are fundamentally adjectives which, for one reason or another, may display some verbal features, tense-aspect stem participles are fundamentally verbs which consistently display a significant set of prototypically verbal features.
Tense-aspect stem participles, then, while being superficially similar to ‘verbal’ adjectives and nouns, are fundamentally distinct from them. This is because they are first and foremost a verbal rather than nominal formation—a verbal formation which, due to the particular properties of its inflection, produces morphologically adjectival verbs.
.. Participles within the wider grammar
Participial phrases are not the only kind of embedded clauses and participles are not the only means of making a secondary predication alongside that of the matrix predicate. Besides the functional overlap with adjectives, finite relative clauses, other

---

<!-- page 318 -->

7.1 The category of tense-aspect stem participles 

subordinate clauses, infinitival clauses, and even coordination of clauses can overlap in different ways with the functions of participial clauses.4 A full study of the interrelations and differences between the participial clauses and the various similar strategies for subordinate predication would constitute a separate volume (if not more than one), but a few observations should be made.5
In §4.2 I formalized adnominally-used participles as heads of closed adjuncts (adj) with gapped heads. In this way I effectively treat them in functional terms as headless, non-finite, relative clauses. In phrase-structural terms adnominal participles are very different from relative clauses: a participial clause is a VP, not an S or CP, and in Sanskrit headless finite relative clauses are impossible. Nevertheless the f-structure formalization captures the fact that relative clauses and adnominal participial clauses are functionally very similar: both express a verbal predication about a nominal constituent that participates in a superordinate clause. So, an adnominally used participle such as tra¯´yama¯n. a- ‘who protects (us)’ at RV 7.35.10a ((4.117), repeated here as (7.1)) does not significantly differ in its contribution to the meaning of the clause from the contribution that a finite clause, yás tra¯´yate ‘id.’, would make ((7.2)).

(7.1) śám.

no deváh. savita¯´ tra¯´yama¯n. ah.

propitious us.d god Savitr. protect.prs.ptc.n.sg.m

‘Propitious for us be the god Savitr., who protects (us).’ (RV 7.35.10a)

(7.2) śám.

no deváh. savita¯´ yás tra¯´yate

propitious us.d god Savitr. who protect.prs.3sg

‘Propitious for us be the god Savitr., who protects (us).’ (based on RV 7.35.10a)

Similarly, converbal and absolute participial clauses parallel subordinate clauses: in the same way that converbal participles can express temporal relations and more specific semantic relations such as manner, cause, or purpose, so these same functions can be expressed by subordinate clauses introduced by complementizers with meanings such as ‘so that’, ‘because’, ‘when’, etc. Viti’s (2007) discussion of the functions of Vedic subordinate clauses distinguishes types which all have parallels in the converbal and absolute functionality of participles: temporal, conditional, causal, purposive, concessive, and completive clauses. We have even seen the direct influence of temporal subordinate clauses on participial clauses in the irregular addition of yád ‘when’ to participles expressing contingency ((5.89) and (5.90)).

4 Cf. Behrens et al. () on the sorts of constructions that compete with participial clauses. 5 A detailed study of the overlapping functionality of participial and finite subordinate clauses in Ancient Greek has been undertaken by Buijs (). Hettrich () provides a detailed analysis of the uses of finite subordinate clauses in RV Sanskrit, but does not provide detailed comparisons with participial clauses and similar constructions. An interesting diachronic consequence of this functional overlap in Proto-Baltic is noted by Cowgill (): confusion between the finite verb and participle in subordinate clauses led to the pl. indicative replacing the inherited form of the nominative plural participle.

---

<!-- page 319 -->

 Conclusion
However, participles are not simply identical to relative and subordinate clauses of different kinds. In §4.7 I discussed the cohesion of the PtcP and the fact that, although discontinuity of PtcPs is permitted, it is not particularly common. Discontinuity of the PtcP is not entirely unrestricted, moreover: preverbs modifying participles are constrained to appear in a constituent with at least one other element of the PtcP. Even so, in this respect participial clauses are distinctly different from embedded finite clauses: in Sanskrit, finite embedded clauses are never discontinuous within the matrix clause. Subordinate clauses also tend either to precede or to follow the whole matrix clause, whereas participial clauses often appear within the matrix clause. At the same time, embedded finite clauses are usually explicitly marked by either a complementizer or relative pronoun, whereas no such explicit subordinator appears with participial clauses.
All these differences reflect the fact that participial clauses are desententialized to a greater degree than finite subordinate clauses; that is, the predication they make is less independent, less clausal, in a way similar to how finite subordinate clauses are relatively desententialized in comparison with finite main clauses.6 The incipient development of periphrastic functionality could even be taken to reflect an ongoing desententialization of participial clauses and increasing integration into the main clause. Participial clauses are concomitantly more integrated into their matrix clause, which is why they are not explicitly marked and can be discontinuous, interspersed with elements of the matrix clause.
In expressive terms, then, a participial clause is more flexible than a finite subordinate clause: it permits the same sorts of meaning to be expressed but without the syntactic baggage, as it were, of a full subordinate clause. But at the same time participial clauses are semantically less specific: they lack the subordinator that can make explicit the semantic role of a finite embedded clause; as we have seen, it is often therefore difficult to define precisely the contextual functionality of a particular participial clause. Participial and finite embedded clauses are therefore two distinct strategies for conveying the same kinds of meaning, with distinct syntactic properties that could render one or the other more appropriate in a given situation. Participial clauses might be preferred, for example, when the speaker had no need to make explicit the semantic relation between embedded and matrix predication, or actively wanted to leave it ambiguous. However, in terms of the Rigveda at least, it is likely that metrical and poetic considerations play at least as large a part in the choice between constructions as any purely linguistic reason.
6 Tikkanen () discusses the fact that converb (and therefore converbal participle) clauses are equivalent to converbal subordinate clauses, but further desententialized. Cf. also Behrens et al. (: ) on this difference between participial and finite subordinate clauses.

---

<!-- page 320 -->

7.2 Participles in Proto-Indo-European 
The position occupied by the tense-aspect stem participles in the linguistic system of Rigvedic Sanskrit was marginally shared with the absolutive, which was briefly discussed in §4.1. Although not sharing the semantic range of the participles, the absolutive could, like participles, express the equivalent of a temporal subordinate clause. In the Rigveda absolutives are sufficiently rare to be considered marginal, but the later productivity of the past absolutive may have contributed to the loss of the perfect participle which (discussed in §5.5.4) was itself relatively limited in semantic terms, compared with the present participle, being largely restricted to the expression of temporal relations. What this shows is that despite the relative impoverishment of the participial system in later Sanskrit, the area of the syntactic system inhabited by them in Rigvedic Sanskrit was not eliminated or necessarily reduced. Rather, it retained its major role throughout the history of the language, only in the Classical period inhabited not by a range of tense-aspect stem participles but (primarily) by the present participle and the past absolutive.
. Participles in Proto-Indo-European
As mentioned briefly in §1.3, the status of tense-aspect stem participles as a coherent category in Proto-Indo-European, and the function of such forms (whatever their status), is not universally agreed upon. In this section I address the questions of how far it is possible to project back the features of Rigvedic participles into PIE, and what can be said about the origins and status of the category in the parent language.
.. Clausal syntax
The syntactic and semantic employment of tense-aspect stem participles in the Rigveda matches very closely the employment of tense-aspect stem participles in Ancient Greek. In the very earliest stages of some other Indo-European languages, however, cognate participle categories are syntactically and semantically somewhat more restricted, appearing more adjectival and correspondingly less verbal in their functionality (cf. §1.3). The question is, then, whether the close similarity between Sanskrit and Greek suggests common inheritance or parallel innovation, or perhaps whether a combination of the two should be envisaged.
In two important respects the surviving evidence for old Indo-Iranian and Ancient Greek is notably different from the evidence for other Indo-European languages. Excluding the Anatolian subgroup—which undoubtedly separated from the rest of the Indo-European family at a very early date, so early that it may be valid to speak of postAnatolian PIE as a distinct entity—all other Indo-European languages are substantially attested only after they had already come into contact with, and once their literature had been influenced by, either Ancient Greek (Latin, Gothic, Slavic, etc.), Indo-Iranian (Armenian, Tocharian), or Latin, which was itself influenced by Greek (North-West

---

<!-- page 321 -->

 Conclusion
Germanic, Celtic).7 In terms of reconstructing syntactic and semantic functionality, then, the value of all these languages is somewhat diminished, since these features can be more easily affected by language contact than can, for example, morphology. Thus the employment of participles in literary Latin or Old Church Slavonic in converbal functions, precisely parallel to those attested in Ancient Greek and Sanskrit, can be argued to represent Greek influence; but it cannot be proved that such employments are not a common inheritance of these languages as well. Old Indo-Iranian and Ancient Greek are therefore the only two Indo-European languages, excluding Anatolian languages at least, which have not to our knowledge suffered significant linguistic and/or literary influence from another structurally similar Indo-European language prior to their first (literary) attestation.
Secondly, old Indo-Iranian and Ancient Greek are two of the only I-E languages whose oldest surviving major texts are original compositions in verse.8 It should not be a surprise, nor should it prejudice our analysis of its linguistic reality, if the wide-ranging functional employment of participles is found primarily in poetic or literary texts. As discussed in §7.1, participial clauses can license greater polysemy than otherwise equivalent subordinate clauses, a fact which is likely to favour the use of the latter in factual or plain prose texts but the use of the former in verse or literary prose. Moreover, poetry tends towards a greater avoidance of function words than prose language, favouring the use of more syntactically integrated clauses. This does not mean that the ways in which participles are used in the Rigveda and the Homeric epics are somehow non-standard; rather, their use in these texts is an entirely valid, grammatical part of the languages involved, which is simply more appropriate in some literary or linguistic contexts than in others. So if present participles in early Latin, say, are less often used in converbal functions in less literary texts, but more often used in such functions in literary texts, this need not be due to greater Greek influence on the latter, but due to the same inherited functional range as Greek, with the same variations in use.
Principles of reconstruction therefore encourage the view that in Proto-IndoEuropean (post-Anatolian, at least), tense-aspect stem participles could be used in most if not all of the ways in which they are used in the Rigveda and the Homeric epics. That this functional range is not so clearly attested in other Indo-European languages is due to differences in the surviving evidence for these languages, not necessarily any strictly linguistic factor.
7 There is a wealth of literature on the position of Anatolian in respect to the other I-E languages, and whether the term ‘Indo-Hittite’ should be reserved for a putative ancestor of Anatolian on the one hand and other I-E languages on the other. See e.g. Sturtevant (; ; ; ), Cowgill (), and Lehrman (; ; ) for an Indo-Hittite grouping; alternative approaches are Meillet (), Kammenhuber (), Meid (; ), and esp. Melchert (forthcoming). More generally on the dialectology of IndoEuropean, see Ringe et al. () and Nakhleh et al. ().
8 Ignoring the Mycenaean Greek texts, which due to their brevity and functional nature tell us relatively little about syntax.

---

<!-- page 322 -->

7.2 Participles in Proto-Indo-European 

.. Morphosyntactic alignment

In Sanskrit, active tense-aspect stem participles consistently show accusative mor-

phosyntactic alignment, just like the finite verbal stems to which they are formed,

and the same is true in most other Indo-European languages. That is, the subject

of an active participle is the single subject argument S, if formed to an intransitive

stem, or the (more agentive) subject argument A, if formed to a transitive verbal stem,

but never the (more affected) object argument O of a transitive stem.9 In contrast,

the Hittite present participle in -nt-, cognate with the -nt- participles of Sanskrit and

other I-E languages, usually shows an ergative agreement pattern, in which the subject

of the participle is the single-argument S, if formed to an intransitive verbal stem,

or the (more affected) object-argument O, if formed to a transitive verbal stem, but

not (regularly) the (more agentive) subject argument A of a transitive verbal stem.10 An important question concerning the PIE participle in ∗-nt-, then, is whether it had

accusative alignment, ergative alignment, or even something else.11

Hittite participles in -nt- are also perfective when formed to telic verbs (cf. Neu

1968: 117–22; Garrett 1996: 103). So, for√example, Hittite ašant- ‘being’ is the -nt-

participle ‘being’ to

to√tahse‘baete’;litcheinatrliagnnsmitievnetraonodt

eš/aš ‘be’, precisely meaning√of the two

cognate with Skt. sántparticiples fully match.

On the other hand the Hittite telic transitive root kuen ‘kill’ forms an -nt- participle

kunant- meaning ‘killed, having been killed’, which is cognate with, b√ut does not match the alignment or imperfectivity of, Skt. ghnánt- ‘striking, slaying’ to han ‘strike, slay’. In this way, the Hittite participle is more similar to the Skt. -tá- adjective (PIE ∗-tó-)

than the -nt- participle: it is a resultative formation, at least when based on transitive

roots. Interestingly, a few Hittite participles display both accusative and ergative

alig√nment, along with corresponding processual/resultative sense; e.g. Hittite adant-, to ed/ad ‘eat’, can mean either ‘eating’ or ‘eaten’ (on this see Kloekhorst 2008: 183–4).

Such variation is in fact typologically well attested with resultative formations to

transitive unergative verbs (Nedjalkov and Jaxontov 1988: 9; Haspelmath 1994: 174;

Kibort 2012: 331).

If the Hittite alignment is assumed to be original, on the grounds that Hittite is

so much older and more archaic than the other Indo-European languages, then the

accusative alignment of the other I-E languages must be an innovation. It could be

explained as an analogical realignment on the model of the accusative syntax of finite

verbal stems. That is, the functional parallel between participles and finite forms of

9 On the concepts S, A, and O, and the distinction between accusative and ergative alignment patterns,
see Dixon (; ; ). 10 On the Hittite participle, see Hoffner and Melchert (: –). 11 Only the participle in ∗-nt- is in question here, since this is the only productive participle in Hittite.
Mediopassive participles are complicated by their use with patient-oriented stems and their paradigmatic alternation with ∗-nt-, so that nothing definite can be said about them; PIE ∗-uos- (Skt. -vám. s-) originally formed participles to intransitive stative stems, so would have had no interesting morphosyntactic align-
ment variations.

---

<!-- page 323 -->

 Conclusion

intransitive verbal stems, for example PIE participle ∗g um-ské-nt- ‘going’ beside finite 3sg. ∗g um-ské-ti ‘goes’ (ancestors of Rigvedic gáchant- and gáchati respectively), could have licensed the reanalysis of participles to transitive stems, such that for example the participle ∗dhi-dhh-ónt-, beside finite 3sg. ∗dhi-dhéh-ti ‘places’ (ancestors of Rigvedic dádhat- and dádha¯ti respectively), was taken to mean ‘placing’ rather than ‘being placed’.12 Such a development might indicate that the inflectional status of tenseaspect stem participles is a secondary development, or at least that tense-aspect stem participles became more strongly associated and integrated with the finite verbal paradigm at a period following the separation of Anatolian from the rest of PIE. One problem with this assumption is that the evidence of the non-Hittite Anatolian languages, which is admittedly very slim, may align more closely with the nonAnatolian languages than with Hittite (cf. fn. 14 below).
An alternative explanation is that put forward by Haspelmath (1994: 170). He suggests that the ∗-nt- participle in PIE may not have been, according to his terminology, ‘inherently oriented’ towards either the agent (i.e. accusative agreement) or patient (i.e. ergative agreement), but may have been ‘completely free of inherent orientation in Proto-Indo-European’.13 It would therefore have been comparable to ‘contextually oriented participles’, which are attested in some non-I-E languages such as Lezgian. The subject, and therefore the alignment, of a contextually-oriented participle can only be determined pragmatically from the context; in the following examples√, from Haspelmath (1994: 154), the Lezgian imperfective participle kxˆizwaj, from kxˆiz, ‘write’ can be oriented to the more agentive argument (a.), the more patientive argument (b.), or even non-argument participants (c.,d.).

Brosman () argues that the reanalysis began specifically among A-labile verbs (cf. Ch. , fn. ). 13 Puhvel (: ) makes a similar argument regarding the PIE ∗-tó- adjective.

7.2 Participles in Proto-Indo-European 

On this pattern, PIE ∗dhi-dhh-ónt-s could have meant ‘placing’ or ‘placed’, etc.,

depending on the context. Kloekhorst (2008: 183–4) also assumes that the PIE

participle was contextually oriented, arguing that in Hittite the (originally contex-

tual) O-oriented reading was generalized, except in a few archaic cases, while in

post-Anatolian PIE the (originally contextual) A-oriented reading was generalized.14 Schmidt (1964) argues instead that both the PIE ∗-nt- participle and the perfect active ∗-uos- participle were originally found only in intransitive-stative uses (i.e.

the ašant-/sánt- type), with the ergative or accusative alignment found in attested

languages resulting from distinct extensions from intransitive to transitive uses.

Support for the antiquity of the Hittite alignment has been claimed in certain syn-

tactically anomalous -nt- participles found outside Anatolian. For example, Sanskrit

jtáorraenptr-e‘oseldn’tdao√reessnuolttamtivaetcfhortmheatailoignn; msimenitlaorflyfi,nstiatevaj¯´ánra‘ptira‘misaekde’ stool√d’,sbtuut‘prraatihseer’,

However, I have argued above that the forms járant- and p´r. s.ant- are not participles, either synchronically or diachronically (§6.10). Rather, they are Caland adjectives:

primary adjectival formations, not formed to verbal stems. Their meaning and ‘ori-

entation’ is therefore based directly on the verbal root and is not connected to that

of finite verbal stems or participles.16 The apparent intensive participle pépiśat- is

paradigmatically isolated (the only other intensive form is a medial participle attested

post-Rigveda), and although its patient orientation is unexpected when compared

with other active forms of the root (Schaefer 1994: 152–3), its isolation and intensive

morphology make it more likely to be a nonce-formation than an archaism. Mor-

phologically stava¯´n (found only in this n.sg.m. form) cannot be a regular participle,

at least synchronically: there is little to support Watkins’ (1969: 142–5) assumption

that the ending represents an inherited -nt- participle altered by analogy with -vant-

stems. There have been various alternative explanations of this form, the most sensible

Tarhunt-, possibly Lyc. Trqqñt-. ‘continually’, to transitive ékho¯ ‘I hold’; cf. Smyth 1956: §2062a). In any case, as evidence

Altogether, then, the evidence for -nt- participles to transitive stems showing

patient-oriented alignment, particularly in Sanskrit but also in other non-Anatolian

languages, is extremely slim. This does not mean that participles in early PIE (i.e. in the

stage before Anatolian separated) were not patient-oriented as they are in Hittite, but

it means that there is no direct support for such an analysis. It is, however, more likely

that the accusative alignment is a replacement of an original ergative alignment than

the other way around, since the strong accusative alignment in the finite verbal system

provides a good base for analogy, whereas there is no obvious basis for a development in the opposite direction.18 Most likely, perhaps, is that the PIE ∗-nt- participle was

originally contextually oriented, and that post-Anatolian PIE generalized accusative

alignment after the analogy of the finite system, while Hittite (and perhaps, to an

extent, other Anatolian languages) generalized the ergative alignment as part of a

Even if such claims have substance (but cf. e.g. Villar ; ; Rumsey a; b; Zwolanek ), they apply to a period too remote to be of relevance to the present topic.

<!-- page 326 -->

7.2 Participles in Proto-Indo-European 
reanalysis as a resultative formation.19 Either way, the thoroughgoing generalization of accusative alignment in post-Anatolian PIE strongly suggests an increased association and integration of the ∗-nt- formation with the finite verbal system.20
.. Ablaut and origins
The evidence of morphosyntactic alignment, then, suggests that the category of participles in ∗-nt- was, at an early stage of PIE, less integrated into the verbal system than they were in the post-Anatolian period, and less than they are in Rigvedic Sanskrit. Whether the ∗-nt- participle began life as a non-verbal adjective is a more difficult question, as it involves projecting back well beyond the limits of the comparative method. However, the close similarity between participles and Caland adjectives in ∗-nt- is notable, and may suggest a common origin.
One respect in which PIE participles in ∗-nt- are often considered to diverge from the verbal stems with which they were associated is in terms of ablaut. It is often assumed that in PIE ∗-nt- participles displayed amphikinetic ablaut, i.e. with accented é in the root in strong cases, and in the ending in weak cases.21 However, the best evidence for amphikinetic ablaut in ∗-nt- formations is in two forms that I have argued above to be Caland adjectives: Skt. járant-/ juránt-,
19 The ‘perfective’ sense of Hittite participles to telic roots is likely to be a part of this reanalysis, since otherwise the -nt- formation is consistently associated with an imperfective/processual sense. It is notable that aorist participles are rare or nonexistent in I-E languages outside Greek, where the non-finite verbal system has been filled out to such an extent that no inheritance need be assumed, and Indo-Iranian, where, as I have discussed in detail above, aorist participles often lack perfective or anterior sense. Although the Rigvedic data is consistent with the assumption that PII inherited the ability to form participles to perfective aorist tense-aspect stems, this does not necessarily mean that there was ever a fully productive process of aorist participle formation in PIE or PII. It might be e.g. that the PIE participial suffix ∗-nt- (and ∗-mhno-) originally had specifically imperfective semantics, and as such could not easily be used to derive participles from aorist stems, at least at an early stage. Alternative reconstructions of the PIE verbal system permit other explanations. For example, following Willi’s () analysis of the PIE verb, the apparent aorist participles in RV Sanskrit could have originally been imperfective participles to roots which later got dragged into the ‘aorist’ category, ending up as an unproductive and functionally ambiguous category morphologically aligned with the aorist. This could contribute to the explanation of the common imperfective or ‘presentlike’ use of aorist participles in the Rigveda (although it should be stressed that, as discussed in §.., the RV data can be explained by language-internal developments without recourse to such assumptions regarding inheritance).
20 This increased integration must follow the extension of what was originally an adjectival root formation (as discussed in the following section) to verbal tense-aspect stems, since in Hittite, participles are formed not to roots but to verbal stems, including a number of stems that are cognate with certain present tense-aspect stems in other I-E languages. So e.g. the Hittite participle šarninkant- is formed to the nasalinfix stem šarni(n)k- ‘compensate’ of the root attested in Hitt. šarku- ‘eminent’ (Friedrich : §–, pp. –, gives examples of participles to all Hittite√stem types); this is entirely parallel to e.g. Skt. yuñjánt‘yoking’ to the nasal-infix present stem of the verb yuj ‘yoke’. Since it is reasonable to assume this shared feature is inherited, we must reckon with an ongoing process of integration into the verbal system over a considerable period of time.
21 For introductions to PIE accent and ablaut patterns, see Fortson (: –), Clackson (: –), and Meier-Brügger (: –).

---

<!-- page 327 -->

 Conclusion

reflecting PIE ∗gérh-ont-/grh-nt´-, and Skt. uśánt- beside Greek heko¯´n, reflecting PIE ∗uék-ont-/uk-nt´- (see §6.10).22
Assumptions that ∗-nt- participles in PIE were amphikinetic are primarily based on

these two forms,23 but their status is at the least highly ambiguous, while unambiguous

participles show ablaut patterns parallel to the stems from which they are derived (e.g.

hysterokinetic or acrostatic, but never amphikinetic since this does not occur in the

finite verbal system). Besides the stems underlying Skt. járant- and uśánt-, there are only two other PIE ∗-nt- stems for which there is (at least alleged) evidence of such a

paradigm (Schaffner 2001: 610–22). One is the stem underlying Skt. br. hánt-, while the other is the stem underlying Skt. dánt- ‘tooth’.24 Sanskrit br. hánt- is also not a participle but a Caland adjective; in any case, the evidence for amphikinetic ablaut is not strong.

Schaffner (2001) argues for amphikinetic ablaut for the ancestor of the noun dánt-

‘tooth’, but the evidence perhaps more likely suggests hysterokinetic ablaut. This noun,

together with its many cognates (Latin d¯ens, Attic Gr. odoús, Aeolic édontes, Goth.

tunþus etc.) participle to

is widely the root ∗

a√ssumed (following hed ‘bite, eat’, but

Schindler Schaffner

1975: 62) to reflect a PIE aorist (2001: 617–20) has convincingly

argued that this is unlikely. Schaffner points out that an original root aorist participle should have had a full-grade suffix in ∗-e- (Forssman 1964; Hoffmann 1969b: 264ff.),

while all the positive evidence of attested forms points rather to an o-grade (Gr.

odoús, Lith. dantìs, ON to˛nn, OE to¯þ, OHG zant). At the same time, any suggestion

of a thematic aorist stem is countered by the clear evidence for the zero grade: Skt.

oblique stem dat´-, Arm. atamn, possibly Lat. dent-, and OIr. d¯et. Schaffner therefore

reconstructs an amphikinetic non-participial adjectival or substantival agent noun ∗héd-ont-/hd-nt´- (for which, however, no direct evidence of the strong stem remains,

assuming Aeolic édontes is not original). The only plausible alternative is that the fSocrhminrdelfelrec(t1s97a5h)yasrtgeuroeksintheatitcPpIaErt∗ic√iphleedtooarirgoiontalplyremseenatnstte‘mbit,ei’,.eo. n∗hthde-óbnats-i/shodf-ntht´-e.

However, the evidence is rather unclear (as discussed by Kümmel 1998: 203–4): most

if not all of the evidence for the Narten present can be explained by language-specific

22 The accent of Greek heko¯´n (gen. hekóntos) is an exception to the rule that nouns and adjectives in -o¯n, -ontos are paroxytone (Probert, : ), and can only be related to the oxytone accent of the Greek

OHG wîgant, etc.; whatever its explanation it cannot contribute to a discussion of PIE ablaut patterns.

7.2 Participles in Proto-Indo-European 

sound laws, e.g. Lachmann’s Law (Kiparsky 1965; Jasanoff 2004) for Latin, Winter’s

Law (Matasović 1995) for Balto-Slavic. It is also highly surprising that there should be

is no obvious reason why most Indo-European languages would replace a perfectly

serviceable root a finite aorist to

a∗o√rihst

with suppletive forms from unrelated roots (only in OCS is ed attested, a characterized s-aorist jasь). The only plausible

explanation would be that, during the reanalysis of the basic lexical meaning of the

root as atelic ‘eat’ (presumably on the basis of the iterative/durative Narten present),

the lengthened grade present came under analogical pressure to be rebuilt as an

ordinary hysterodynamic root present. This might have caused the obsolescence of

an original root aorist, and would explain, for example, the root present attested in

of reanalysis, since the meaning ‘tooth’ is more appropriate to an iterative formation

(‘bite repeatedly’ > ‘eat’) than a perfective formation (‘bite (once)’ / ‘having bitten’).25

In any case, Schaffner’s proposal of amphikinetic ablaut for this form is based on the

assumption that it cannot be a participle; if it can be treated as a present participle,

there is no reason to assume amphikinetic rather than hysterokinetic, so whatever its

explanation an amphikinetic participle is hardly likely.26

Besides these forms, the most significant evidence for amphikinetic ablaut in PIE
